- **References:** `<j4isj0d9pjsoc92mf6h7tdmcbsnem3kf39@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409072338.3ded2d39@posting.google.com> <c0quj0t46eakjl6n21e5srmk00ilrrf6tg@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <2tg1k09dhunkm7r4408ahkr13540j8388i@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409092007.46cc04db@posting.google.com> <2514k09nj41epdgrmsgiejth6lh0kpc8n5@4ax.com> <10k4fqr41odggdb@corp.supernews.com> <mt76k0dbt25is9dnsgmqcfqj3nehr293hc@4ax.com> <10k6j81qr3092c2@corp.supernews.com> <6rd8k0l8ejudlnug25mppml9qu3qod2rfq@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:6rd8k0l8ejudlnug25mppml9qu3qod2rfq@4ax.com...
> On Sat, 11 Sep 2004 15:06:43 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
> wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> >> I wish we could invoke a method the way we call an intrinsic
function
> >> >> -- by saying its name. A method is a verb.
> >> >
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> two-year-olds talk.

Consider, *preparing* (the act of a person who prepares)
Wagner's Vegetarian Medley cannot exist independently of
Wagner writing the recipe. Wagner is not necessary to
*prepare* the dish according to the recipe.

Also, invoke has a sense of 'do the following'. Thus "Do the
following, 'Baking a cake'", makes no sense; but "Do the
following, 'Bake a cake'", does make sense. I have not yet
found a sense of invoke the includes 'start'.

[invoke: to cause, call forth, or bring about. -- RHCD]

> >One other point, Mr Wagner. INVOKE in COBOL has
> >a similarity to LET in BASIC. It is not necessary for one to
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> objects of Let.

As I show above, the entire command is the object of invoke,
not just the method name. Computer languages are not natural
languages. But, if one defines 'this command' as the text
following 'invoke', then 'invoke this command' gives the
verb-noun combination you're trying to achieve. And, this is
why 'invoke' is not necessary to the understanding.

> >Consider that
> >    [Invoke] EmployeeMaster:'Read' using EmployeeRecord
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> verbs such as READ.

Mr Wagner, better? Or just different? INVOKE is required
for syntax. It is not required for understanding. Treating
INVOKE as more than it is invites confusion.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 24)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-12T17:25:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<c0quj0t46eakjl6n21e5srmk00ilrrf6tg@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <2tg1k09dhunkm7r4408ahkr13540j8388i@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409092007.46cc04db@posting.google.com> <2514k09nj41epdgrmsgiejth6lh0kpc8n5@4ax.com> <10k4fqr41odggdb@corp.supernews.com> <mt76k0dbt25is9dnsgmqcfqj3nehr293hc@4ax.com> <10k6j81qr3092c2@corp.supernews.com> <6rd8k0l8ejudlnug25mppml9qu3qod2rfq@4ax.com> <10k8miq8v4lq21b@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Sun, 12 Sep 2004 10:15:49 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
>*prepare* the dish according to the recipe.

The definition you gave is either the gerundial or intransitive form
of prepare. An intransitive has no direct object, therefore could not
take the command is its object. Example: "He is  preparing for a
carreer." By citing the gerundial form, you supported my argument.

>Also, invoke has a sense of 'do the following'. Thus "Do the
>following, 'Baking a cake'", makes no sense; but "Do the
>following, 'Bake a cake'", does make sense. I have not yet
>found a sense of invoke the includes 'start'.

"Do the following" is a complete sentence in which following is an
adjective and the word action has been elided. Its full form is "Do
the following action:", where action is the object of do. "Bake a
cake" is also a complete sentence. The rules of punctuation call for a
colon between the two sentences because "Bake a cake" expands the word
action. If a list of sentences followed, that would be an enumeration:
"Do the following: Bake a cake, Decorate it, Serve it to the guests."

The word invoke is not a sentence, it's a verb. If it were a sentence,
it would have punctuation after it, for example:

Invoke this: Bake a cake.
Hark! The herald angels sing.

>> >One other point, Mr Wagner. INVOKE in COBOL has
>> >a similarity to LET in BASIC. It is not necessary for one to
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>why 'invoke' is not necessary to the understanding.

A sentence cannot be the object of a verb, but a gerund (ending with
-ing) can be.

Read a record.
Carry out reading a record.
Perform reading a record.

>> >Consider that
>> >    [Invoke] EmployeeMaster:'Read' using EmployeeRecord
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>Mr Wagner, better? Or just different?

Grammatically correct is better.

> INVOKE is required
>for syntax. It is not required for understanding. Treating
>INVOKE as more than it is invites confusion.

The word invoke is NOT required for syntax. The standard says we can
use Inline Invocation instead.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 25)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2004-09-12T17:24:35-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<c0quj0t46eakjl6n21e5srmk00ilrrf6tg@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <2tg1k09dhunkm7r4408ahkr13540j8388i@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409092007.46cc04db@posting.google.com> <2514k09nj41epdgrmsgiejth6lh0kpc8n5@4ax.com> <10k4fqr41odggdb@corp.supernews.com> <mt76k0dbt25is9dnsgmqcfqj3nehr293hc@4ax.com> <10k6j81qr3092c2@corp.supernews.com> <6rd8k0l8ejudlnug25mppml9qu3qod2rfq@4ax.com> <10k8miq8v4lq21b@corp.supernews.com> <n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com...
> On Sun, 12 Sep 2004 10:15:49 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
> wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> >> >"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in
message
> >> >> >news:2514k09nj41epdgrmsgiejth6lh0kpc8n5@4ax.com...
> >> >> >[snip]
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> carreer." By citing the gerundial form, you supported my argument.

You take that back! I am not supporting your argument
at all. <g>

What I am saying is that, as it relates to computer languages,
the gerundial form implies something different than the verb.
Perhaps it's just me; but, as I see it, the gerundial form, 'the
act of', is a process to be described to the computer. The
verb is a command to follow the process described.

> >Also, invoke has a sense of 'do the following'. Thus "Do the
> >following, 'Baking a cake'", makes no sense; but "Do the
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> "Do the following: Bake a cake, Decorate it, Serve it to the guests."

Mr Wagner, the last is not a valid form for COBOL where
natural language rules do not apply.

invoke cake 'bake'
invoke cake 'decorate'
invoke cake 'serve'

[invoke: to cause, call forth, or bring about. -- RHCD]

to cause the baking of a cake
to cause the decorating of a cake
to cause the serving of a cake

Mr Wagner, the weakness in your argument, as I see it, is
your insistence at applying the rules of natual language to an
artificial language. COBOL is not a natural language. It is a
contrived imperative language.

[contrive: to bring about or effect by a device, strategem,
plan, or scheme. -- RHCD]

[imperative: noting or pertaining to the mood of the verb used
in commands, requests, etc. -- RHCD]

Verbs are chosen for method names because they fit the nature
of the language. With respect to COBOL, using gerunds is,
well, unnatural!

> The word invoke is not a sentence, it's a verb. If it were a sentence,
> it would have punctuation after it, for example:
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
> Grammatically correct is better.

Mr Wagner, grammatically correct is irrelevant because
COBOL is not a natural language.

> > INVOKE is required
> >for syntax. It is not required for understanding. Treating
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> use Inline Invocation instead.

See FDIS, page 94, 8.4.2.4.3 General rules.
"1) An inline method invocation references a temporary data item
with the same class, category, and content as the temp-identifier
that would be returned from the execution of the applicable form
of INVOKE statement, as follows:

INVOKE identifier-1 literal-1 USING arguments RETURNING
temp-identifier
INVOKE identifier-1 literal-1 RETURNING temp-identifier
INVOKE class-name-1 literal-1 USING arguments
RETURNING temp-identifier
INVOKE class-name-1 literal-1 RETURNING temp-identifier

where:

a) arguments are the operands specified within parentheses in the
inline method invocation, if any;

b) temp-identifier has the same description, class, and category
as the RETURNING parameter in the specification of the method
identified by literal-1 and either identifier-1 or class-name-1;

c) temp-identifier is a temporary item that exists for the purpose
of effecting the inline invocation in this way and for no other
purpose."

In other words, 'Inline method invocation' can be used only when
the method returns a value that meets the requirements for a
'temp-identifier'. Otherwise, use an INVOKE statement, where
'INVOKE' is required for syntax.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 26)_

- **From:** Warren Simmons <wsimmons5@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2004-09-13T02:14:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<41450215.2070205@optonline.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<c0quj0t46eakjl6n21e5srmk00ilrrf6tg@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <2tg1k09dhunkm7r4408ahkr13540j8388i@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409092007.46cc04db@posting.google.com> <2514k09nj41epdgrmsgiejth6lh0kpc8n5@4ax.com> <10k4fqr41odggdb@corp.supernews.com> <mt76k0dbt25is9dnsgmqcfqj3nehr293hc@4ax.com> <10k6j81qr3092c2@corp.supernews.com> <6rd8k0l8ejudlnug25mppml9qu3qod2rfq@4ax.com> <10k8miq8v4lq21b@corp.supernews.com> <n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com> <10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Rick Smith wrote:
> "Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
> news:n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com...
…[207 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
I will add to this discussion the following:

The COBOL spec, as far as possible, was contrived, if that is the word
to emply that except for IF, each statement was a command to the 
computer to do something. It's relationship to ENGLISH is only that
people can read it, and understand what it is saying to the computer.

That is, until new features somehow seem to contrive overwise.
Back then, program listings were in computer instrution form including
in some cases binary. When the techs tried to explain what was wrong
and how long it was going to fix the problem, they had to show the
"authorities" gibberish. 'What the hell does that mean?" So, COBOL,
by in large issues commands as in the army, etc.

Today, the only difference is that if something is needed, it can be
added to the logic of the computer. When I say "squat", I want to see
bare butts. In COBOL, that might be: SQUAT. DISPLAY BUTS. 	

The fact that the source can now be stated in lower case, etc. may
tend to suggest that the method is to write something for someone to
read, but it isn't.  It's something that a program has to read, and
compile to comply with a set of specifications on how it will be
done on a specific computer and match what is done on other computers
doing the same thing.

Warren Simmons
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ INVOKE (English vs COBOL (was: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 27)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-13T02:44:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hQ71d.677132$ic1.71235@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<c0quj0t46eakjl6n21e5srmk00ilrrf6tg@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <2tg1k09dhunkm7r4408ahkr13540j8388i@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409092007.46cc04db@posting.google.com> <2514k09nj41epdgrmsgiejth6lh0kpc8n5@4ax.com> <10k4fqr41odggdb@corp.supernews.com> <mt76k0dbt25is9dnsgmqcfqj3nehr293hc@4ax.com> <10k6j81qr3092c2@corp.supernews.com> <6rd8k0l8ejudlnug25mppml9qu3qod2rfq@4ax.com> <10k8miq8v4lq21b@corp.supernews.com> <n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com> <10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com> <41450215.2070205@optonline.net>`

```
Relatively early in the development of the 2002 Standard (I think when we were 
still hoping for a '97 Standard), I wrote a number of papers on WHY I thought 
that

  Invoke object-reference "method-name" Using Parameter

was "wrong" and not consistent with the rest of COBOL (or the definitions of 
what was going on).

It seemed to me then (and still does) that what this statement is ACTUALLY 
trying to convey was:

Send-a-message to the object referenced by Object-Reference to INVOKE the 
"method-Name" on itself - using parameters ...

  or my COBOL-like (sort-of)

Send-Message "method-name" TO object-reference Using Parameter-1 ...

These papers "got nowhere" and the INVOKE syntax became a "de facto" in COBOL 
(as extensions) for a number of implementations - long before the final drafts 
of the Standards went out for public review.

I still do NOT agree that one "invokes" the Object-Reference - but COBOL is not 
English and no matter how much it is influenced by English, the differences 
exist and always will exist.
```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:hQ71d.677132$ic1.71235@news.easynews.com...
[snip]
> I still do NOT agree that one "invokes" the Object-Reference - but COBOL
is not
> English and no matter how much it is influenced by English, the
differences
> exist and always will exist.

FDIS, page 468, 14.8.22 INVOKE statement
"The INVOKE statement causes a method to be invoked."

Mr Klein, one problem may be that methods are qualified by
either a class name or an object reference; but the syntax
does not follow the normal rules for qualification. That is,

   invoke method-name of object-reference
or
   invoke literal of class-name

might seem more COBOL-like and therefore more agreeable.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: INVOKE (English vs COBOL (was: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 29)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-13T14:40:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0fabk0t5dsh4le8msfudnui8lffg7jfipm@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<2514k09nj41epdgrmsgiejth6lh0kpc8n5@4ax.com> <10k4fqr41odggdb@corp.supernews.com> <mt76k0dbt25is9dnsgmqcfqj3nehr293hc@4ax.com> <10k6j81qr3092c2@corp.supernews.com> <6rd8k0l8ejudlnug25mppml9qu3qod2rfq@4ax.com> <10k8miq8v4lq21b@corp.supernews.com> <n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com> <10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com> <41450215.2070205@optonline.net> <hQ71d.677132$ic1.71235@news.easynews.com> <10ka73craibvu63@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Mon, 13 Sep 2004 00:03:47 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
wrote:

>"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
>news:hQ71d.677132$ic1.71235@news.easynews.com...
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>might seem more COBOL-like and therefore more agreeable.

Many people, especially those versed in other programming languages,
dislike Cobol's qualification syntax. They'd prefer to see qualifiers
before the target. I think they used OO as an opportunity to bend the
rules to their preference. 

Also, I think they view Invoke as just another Call. Since the word
CALL was already taken, they used a synonym. 

I don't see it that way; I see methods as verbs. We all know READ
doesn't produce inline code to read a file. It sets up parameters and
calls a file system. Similarly, LOG(n) doesn't generate inline code to
compute a logarithm; it calls an external program. 

The design of a language need not, indeed should not, follow
implementation details. If it did, most things we do would be written
as CALL. Cobol would be as awkward as C, which makes you call a
function to compare two strings.

I think Micro Focus got it right with their Vocabulary feature, which
turns method names into verbs that are referenced with normal Cobol
syntax. For example, FIND 'ABC' INSIDE collectionclass, where FIND is
a method of collectionclass.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: INVOKE (English vs COBOL

_(reply depth: 28)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-09-13T17:50:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%5l1d.419674$gE.165944@pd7tw3no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<hQ71d.677132$ic1.71235@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<c0quj0t46eakjl6n21e5srmk00ilrrf6tg@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <2tg1k09dhunkm7r4408ahkr13540j8388i@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409092007.46cc04db@posting.google.com> <2514k09nj41epdgrmsgiejth6lh0kpc8n5@4ax.com> <10k4fqr41odggdb@corp.supernews.com> <mt76k0dbt25is9dnsgmqcfqj3nehr293hc@4ax.com> <10k6j81qr3092c2@corp.supernews.com> <6rd8k0l8ejudlnug25mppml9qu3qod2rfq@4ax.com> <10k8miq8v4lq21b@corp.supernews.com> <n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com> <10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com> <41450215.2070205@optonline.net> <hQ71d.677132$ic1.71235@news.easynews.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:

>Relatively early in the development of the 2002 Standard (I think when we were 
>still hoping for a '97 Standard), I wrote a number of papers on WHY I thought 
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Interesting. So now I go back to Raymond Obin's book "OO An Introduction 
for COBOL Programmers" - 1993 published on behalf of the OCTG. First 
mention of  INVOKE - Page 11 :-
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
3.1.3. Using Methods.

Methods in an object can be called. Calling a method is like calling a 
program except that the method is a 'program within an object'. (<< that 
ties back with when I viewed a method as a 'mini-program' - still is but 
not quite >>). To call a program, we use the name of the program; to 
call a method, we use a combination of the object and the name of the 
method within the object.

In an application, methods can be called  from a number of different 
places (<<my Cart Wheel>>); the end-user can cause methods to be called 
through the user interface; other methods can call methods as they 
execute; and the system can call methods, for example, a "MakeBackup" 
method could be called every day at 5.00pm.

The he has a greyed, highlight box :-

Calling a Method

The procedure code in an object is used by Calling it. This is done just 
like calling a program except that it is a call to a Method in an Object.

The term Invoke a Method is also used.
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Reasonably clear to me - and a couple of "co-conspirators" to get the 
book out, (amongst others), Schricker and Klein  :-)

CALL <aProgram> using aaaaaa returning bbbbbb

CALL <aProgram> <entry-point> using aaaaaaa returning bbbbbbb
INVOKE <aProgram = it's object version> <entry-point = methodname> using 
aaaaaaa returning bbbbbbb

Add entry-points and CALL and INVOKE look the same to me.

Anyway as you concluded - lump it or like it, (and I like it), we are 
stuck with the INVOKE syntax as is.

Jimmy
```

news:4145F413.4050303@optonline.net...
> Rick Smith wrote:
> > "Warren Simmons" <wsimmons5@optonline.net> wrote in message

> How about "invented"...

Let's see, Mr Simmons.

[invent: 1. to originate as a product of one's own device
or contrivance. 2. to produce or create with the imagination.
-- RHCD]

The first definition includes the notion of 'contrived'. The
second seems too general; that is, it lacks the idea that
COBOL was the result of a plan to create a language
for a specific purpose (business oriented). Based upon
my knowledge of its history, I think C is closer to
invented; while C++ might be seen as contrived (object
oriented). Also, FORTRAN might be seen as contrived
(scientific oriented).

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 26)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-13T12:49:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <2tg1k09dhunkm7r4408ahkr13540j8388i@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409092007.46cc04db@posting.google.com> <2514k09nj41epdgrmsgiejth6lh0kpc8n5@4ax.com> <10k4fqr41odggdb@corp.supernews.com> <mt76k0dbt25is9dnsgmqcfqj3nehr293hc@4ax.com> <10k6j81qr3092c2@corp.supernews.com> <6rd8k0l8ejudlnug25mppml9qu3qod2rfq@4ax.com> <10k8miq8v4lq21b@corp.supernews.com> <n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com> <10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Sun, 12 Sep 2004 17:24:35 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
wrote:

>Mr Wagner, the weakness in your argument, as I see it, is
>your insistence at applying the rules of natual language to an
>artificial language. COBOL is not a natural language. It is a
>contrived imperative language.

Special pleading is a common defense for illiteracy. People say the
stodgy old rules don't apply because they've been ... what, liberated
by ignorance. Terms like split infinitive and dangling participle
recall images of a  humorless schoolteacher and tests we barely passed
with a C. It's a new world now, a world where the rules are whatever
we make up.

To that I say, you can't send up what you don't have down. You can't
break the rules until you've first mastered them. Anything less is
lazy uncouthness.

>> The word invoke is NOT required for syntax. The standard says we can
>> use Inline Invocation instead.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>of INVOKE statement, as follows:

>In other words, 'Inline method invocation' can be used only when
>the method returns a value that meets the requirements for a
>'temp-identifier'. Otherwise, use an INVOKE statement, where
>'INVOKE' is required for syntax.

Returning an object of its own class is not remarkable. Would you
expect a pair of elephants to give issue to a goldfish?

Cobol cannot do type conversions between user-defined objects, it can
only diagnose mismatches. So unless you're returning a string or a
number, INVOKE has no advantage over Inline Invocation.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 27)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2004-09-13T09:10:39-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ci468f$naj$1@panix5.panix.com>`

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 29)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-09-13T11:58:06-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com> <10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com> <d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <ci468f$naj$1@panix5.panix.com> <vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

><http://groups.google.com/groups?selm=afd5j01oq7bnh3117sh7sur0d11k9ss21p%404ax.com&output=gplain>
> >
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> better opinions.

No one said that at all. Not even close. Your response (above) and
reference to 'social class' at the was clearly due to your 'reading'
words that were never written.

Specifically what was said was: learning what to expect improves your
expectations.

> In this discussion, I didn't compare formal education
> of two individuals nor recommend someone "should take a course."  

Yes. Your expectation was not that which was defined for the action at
issue. The issue was not about opinion, it was about facts (though you
failed to understand that).

> I
> advocated following the rules of English, which are easily found on
> Web sites.

Many things are found on web sites, most of which should be
disbelieved.

Regardless of that there is no reason at all for Cobol to follow any
'rules of english'.  It is not english, it is a contrived language
with its own rules. It is not supposed to be judged alongside
literature, or even formal writing. Indeed it is to be used by speaker
of many different languages, most of which have quite different rules
from those that english have currently.

One could point out that many writings follow perfectly the 'rules of
english', such as legal documents, but this not make the meaning any
more fluent.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 30)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-13T21:05:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com> <10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com> <d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <ci468f$naj$1@panix5.panix.com> <vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com>`

```
On 13 Sep 2004 11:58:06 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:


>... there is no reason at all for Cobol to follow any
>'rules of english'.  It is not english, it is a contrived language
>with its own rules. It is not supposed to be judged alongside
>literature, or even formal writing. 

It's no good for poetry. I've tried writing  blank verse in Cobol --
iambic tetrameter. Words like EQUAL and GREATER mess up the feet.

>Indeed it is to be used by speaker
>of many different languages, most of which have quite different rules
>from those that english have currently.

In the Good Old Days, manuals and the CODASYL standard (? not sure
about that) touted Cobol's English-like grammar as a benefit. Now,
references to English are gone.

>One could point out that many writings follow perfectly the 'rules of
>english', such as legal documents, but this not make the meaning any
>more fluent.

At least they don't use Hungarian type prefixes.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 31)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-09-13T21:49:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<RBo1d.2681$%S.747@pd7tw2no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com> <10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com> <d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <ci468f$naj$1@panix5.panix.com> <vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>It's no good for poetry. I've tried writing  blank verse in Cobol --
>iambic tetrameter. Words like EQUAL and GREATER mess up the feet.
>  
>
Well that sounds to me like the ultimate exercise in futility so far as 
COBOL is concerned.  Isn't that somewhat like flagellation -  I 'm 
thinking of the ragheads in S.Iraq beating themselves with chains and 
the adherents of Opus Dei in Spain.

All is not lost however - seeing  as you are dabbling in OO. Set up a 
Poetry super class with sub-classes. And just think what you can *DO* 
with in-line invocation and the wretched Vocabulary class :-)

Nah. Come to think of it - much nicer, during the last days of summer if 
you take your nice lady-friend down by the river and have another picnic 
- what was it quiche and a bottle of plonk ?  That's more up my street, 
even though I enjoy some poetry.
Actually my 'beloved' and I are off to the mountains on Friday to do 
just that - sit alongside a large pond (Forget-Me-Not Pond in our 
Kananaskis Provincial Park), hear the rush of the exceedingly cold but 
crystal clear Elbow River some 200 yards away and look up at the 
glorious blue-grey backdrop of the mountains, and fingers crossed, a 
clear blue sky. Chances are there will only be the two of us at this 
local beauty spot.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 31)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-09-13T20:28:19-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com> <10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com> <d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <ci468f$naj$1@panix5.panix.com> <vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote

> In the Good Old Days, manuals and the CODASYL standard (? not sure
> about that) touted Cobol's English-like grammar as a benefit. Now,
> references to English are gone.

You are wrong. I strongly doubt that anyone claimed that Cobol was
designed to use  'English grammar' as you appear to claim.  What they
have said is that, for example: "COBOL was designed ... using an
English-like command syntax."

In fact Cobol doesn't have a 'grammar' at all, let alone the English
one, it has a syntax (which is very much a subset of grammar), and not
that of English but a highly stylised and contrived one of its own.

That is: the Cobol syntax is like English.

Not: Cobol is like English grammar.
 

> At least they don't use Hungarian type prefixes.

You may wish to note that 'Hungarian notation' (prefixes) have nothing
to do with the Hungarian language.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 32)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-14T04:48:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<oKu1d.1332535$6p.229903@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com> <10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com> <d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <ci468f$naj$1@panix5.panix.com> <vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com>`

```
"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com...
> Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> English-like command syntax."
<snip>

From page XVII-1 of the '85 Standard (my "high-lighting" of text),

"1. THE DEVELOPMENT OF COBOL
 1.1 ORGANIZATION OF COBOL EFFORT

On May 28 and 29, 1959, a meeting was held for the purpose of considering both 
the desirability and the feasibility of establishing a common language for 
programming of computers in business data processing applications. This meeting 
was attended by representatives from users, both in private industry and in 
government, computer manufacturers, and other interested parties. It was agreed 
that the language must be open-ended and capable of accepting change and 
amendment, that it should be problem-oriented and machine-independent, and that 
it should use

  >>> simple English or pseudo-English <<

and avoid symbolism as far as possible. The Conference on DAta Systems Languages 
(CODASYL) developed out of this meeting."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 35)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-14T19:43:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<XRH1d.1363614$6p.234182@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com> <10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com> <d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <ci468f$naj$1@panix5.panix.com> <vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <oKu1d.1332535$6p.229903@news.easynews.com> <217e491a.0409141110.db9baaa@posting.google.com> <ci7gi4$pkf$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
The quote that I replied to was a reply to Robert's statement,

"In the Good Old Days, manuals and the CODASYL standard (? not sure
about that) touted Cobol's English-like grammar as a benefit. Now,
references to English are gone."

I think that statement from the history section of the '85 Standard implies (or 
maybe I just infer) that the original design *DID* think it was a "benefit" to 
be "English-like" and to that extent I agree with RW (whether you call that 
grammar or otherwise).

I think it is true that:

A) COBOL uses a grammar (for the procedure division) that is similar but NOT 
identical to a VERY small subset of English grammar.  IMHO, the "grammar" of the 
other 3 divisions has little to do with English

B) COBOL most definitely has a "grammar" by any commonly held definition of 
grammar that I have ever heard of. (I know that RW has referenced Chomsky 
before, so you might want to look at:
  http://encyclopedia.thefreedictionary.com/Chomsky%20grammar

C)  There was an original design criterion to make COBOL "English like" but that 
in each successive revision of the Standard this has become less and less 
important  (c.f. other thread on INVOKE for example)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

On 13 Sep 2004 20:28:19 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>that of English but a highly stylised and contrived one of its own.

Vocabulary describes valid combinations of letters to form words.
Syntax describes valid combinations of words to form sentences.
Grammar is Vocabulary + Syntax. It defines a language.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 33)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-14T08:13:02-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com> <10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com> <d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <ci468f$naj$1@panix5.panix.com> <vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <c5mdk0l0c7g8g2f2uea3qrsf16ub6pfbte@4ax.com>`

```
> Vocabulary describes valid combinations of letters to form words.
> Syntax describes valid combinations of words to form sentences.
> Grammar is Vocabulary + Syntax. It defines a language.

I would contend that the Reserved Word List and the rules for formation of
COBOL words are suffucient to define the *vocabulary*, that the syntax and
general rules are sufficient to define valid "utterances" (in terms of
spoken languages) or perhaps better "constructs" in terms of written
language and therefore describe the *syntax*.

If, as you state, "Grammar is Vocabulary + Syntax", by your own definition
the rules of COBOL constitute a *grammar* for that language.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 34)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-14T21:37:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com> <10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com> <d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <ci468f$naj$1@panix5.panix.com> <vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <c5mdk0l0c7g8g2f2uea3qrsf16ub6pfbte@4ax.com> <ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
On Tue, 14 Sep 2004 08:13:02 -0700, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

Richard wrote:
>>>In fact Cobol doesn't have a 'grammar' at all, let alone the English
>>>one, it has a syntax (which is very much a subset of grammar), and not
>>>that of English but a highly stylised and contrived one of its own.

>> Vocabulary describes valid combinations of letters to form words.
>> Syntax describes valid combinations of words to form sentences.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>the rules of COBOL constitute a *grammar* for that language.

Yes, they do. (For simplicity, I omitted + Punctuation.) I can't think
of a case where Cobol's procedure division syntax violates English
syntax. Can you? The rule-breaker is INVOKE (or CALL) taking as its
object a method name written as a non-gerundial verb. Defenders of
INVOKE can say 'The standard didn't tell you how to name a method.
Don't blame Cobol because YOU chose the ungrammatical word.'

INVOKE syntax is not an error, but I see it as a pitfall -- "a hidden
or not easily recognized danger or difficulty." For this reason, I
prefer Inline Invocation. 

Some might say Inline Invocation and Object Oriented in general break
the rules of English, because English is not an object-initial
language. In fact, there are NO object-initial natural languages on
Earth. (Which explains why the Jedi language sounds alien.) That's not
100% true. Counter-examples are "For whom the bell tolls" and "What do
you treasure?"
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 35)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-14T21:47:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rFJ1d.1368943$y4.240719@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com> <10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com> <d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <ci468f$naj$1@panix5.panix.com> <vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <c5mdk0l0c7g8g2f2uea3qrsf16ub6pfbte@4ax.com> <ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com>`

```
> Yes, they do. (For simplicity, I omitted + Punctuation.) I can't think
> of a case where Cobol's procedure division syntax violates English
> syntax. Can you?

Please tell me how the following follows the "rules of English syntax.

  Section-1  Section.
    Paragraph-2.

Other examples,

If A = "B"
    Next Sentence
 .

(Possibly "go to Next-Sentence" would be valid English grammar)

also from the world of "better left behind"

   Go To .
(the altered go to)

What about

  Move Function Current-Date (1:8) to wherever

Or even

Evaluate True
   When A = "B"
    When C = "D"
             Continue
End-Evaluate

wouldn't parse very well in English grammar as I know it
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 36)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-14T22:57:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gisek0h1trjlcdqm7fas7r457qh7uvqvg0@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com> <d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <ci468f$naj$1@panix5.panix.com> <vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <c5mdk0l0c7g8g2f2uea3qrsf16ub6pfbte@4ax.com> <ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <rFJ1d.1368943$y4.240719@news.easynews.com>`

```
On Tue, 14 Sep 2004 21:47:03 GMT, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>> Yes, they do. (For simplicity, I omitted + Punctuation.) I can't think
>> of a case where Cobol's procedure division syntax violates English
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>    Paragraph-2.

That's valid in tables of contents and section headings (such as
throughout the Cobol standard).

>If A = "B"
>    Next Sentence
> .
>
>(Possibly "go to Next-Sentence" would be valid English grammar)

If A equals 'B', skip the following tests .. skip to the next
sentence, with skip elided. Example: If you're a C programmer, NEXT.

>also from the world of "better left behind"
>
>   Go To .
>(the altered go to)

Go to wherever .. the preceeding decision process should have told you
where. It's hard to defend this flawed construct.

>  Move Function Current-Date (1:8) to wherever

Current-Date is a noun. The word Function goes away in recent
versions, although it's a noun too.

>Evaluate True
>   When A = "B"
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>wouldn't parse very well in English grammar as I know it

Employ the following tests:
    When A = "B",  stick your finger in it
    When C = "D", do nothing
    In any other case, install a PermaBond Patch 
This ends the Evaluation phase
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 37)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-14T23:15:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5YK1d.1371962$y4.241254@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com> <d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <ci468f$naj$1@panix5.panix.com> <vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <c5mdk0l0c7g8g2f2uea3qrsf16ub6pfbte@4ax.com> <ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <rFJ1d.1368943$y4.240719@news.easynews.com> <gisek0h1trjlcdqm7fas7r457qh7uvqvg0@4ax.com>`

```
Your rephrases are in some (not all) cases valid English - but they are NOT the 
syntax that is valid as COBOL source code.  By the way, the EVALUATE example has 
an "implicit" "or" which you have ignored.  I *intentionally* didn't put in an 
imperative statement after the first WHEN phrase.

By the way, are you now saying that "tables of contents" (etc) follow the 
"valid" rules of English syntax (grammar)? I think that no theory of "natural 
language" that I know of has ever dealt with this.  Interesting 
question/problem!  (If that is valid, are "flow-charts" valid English?  How 
about diagrams?  Indices?)
```

```
In article <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com>,
 "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:

> "Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
> news:icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com...
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Oh come on already.

Look, I love a nice regular grammer as much as the next compiler hacker 
-- but you folks are taking it way too far.

Why are either of you working in computers?

Wouldn't you be happier smacking students upside the head with a red pen 
when the screwed up a case or whoed when they should have whomed?

Get over it -- Cobol is an ARTIFICAL language.  It does not follow the 
rules of English.  Not only doesn't it, it should not follow the rules 
of English.  Speaking English is no qualification (some would say an 
anti-qualification) toward being able to THINK logically.

Being able to THINK logically is an absolute prerequisite to being able 
to instruct a computer that only understands logical operations.
```

"Joe Zitzelberger" <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote in message
news:joe_zitzelberger-1C3795.22124714092004@knology.usenetserver.com...
[snip]
> Oh come on already.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Why are either of you working in computers?

In my case, toubleshooting computer programs was more
challenging that troubleshooting electronics. For me, there's
nothing like tracking down a logic error to keep the mind
active.

> Wouldn't you be happier smacking students upside the head with a red pen
> when the screwed up a case or whoed when they should have whomed?

I would be happier if I could spend time every day hitting
a little white ball along wide paths of green grass. It's a
different kind of challenge.

Mr Zitzelberger, I also enjoyed answering your questions.

> Get over it -- Cobol is an ARTIFICAL language.  It does not follow the
> rules of English.  Not only doesn't it, it should not follow the rules
> of English.

I been saying that for a few days now.

>  Speaking English is no qualification (some would say an
> anti-qualification) toward being able to THINK logically.
>
> Being able to THINK logically is an absolute prerequisite to being able
> to instruct a computer that only understands logical operations.

Mr Zitzelberger, for your benefit, I will not address the
question of whether a computer *understands* anything.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 38)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-15T09:19:31-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-921801.09193115092004@knology.usenetserver.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com> <10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com> <d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <ci468f$naj$1@panix5.panix.com> <vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <c5mdk0l0c7g8g2f2uea3qrsf16ub6pfbte@4ax.com> <ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <joe_zitzelberger-1C3795.22124714092004@knology.usenetserver.com> <10kfco0g8drct62@corp.supernews.com>`

```
In article <10kfco0g8drct62@corp.supernews.com>,
 "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:

> > Being able to THINK logically is an absolute prerequisite to being able
> > to instruct a computer that only understands logical operations.
> 
> Mr Zitzelberger, for your benefit, I will not address the
> question of whether a computer *understands* anything.

A computer *understands* the commands it was built to understand.  It 
does not sit around reflecting on the political and social ramifications 
of those commands, so it certainly does not qualify as philosophical 
understanding.  Rather, it understands in the sense of a cat 
understanding the word 'tuna'.

As I typed that, young Albert awoke from his slumber atop the warm 
monitor and began insisting that I was taking far too long to prepare 
the can of 'tuna'.  Thus showing his understanding of the word.  He does 
not, however, understand in the sense that you use the word.  He has 
never studied the migratory patterns of various types of tuna.  Nor has 
he applied his kittenish brain to the problems of overfishing in various 
parts of the world.  In that sense, he does not understand 'tuna', but 
in another sense he very much understands 'tuna'. 

Much like the computer understands, when given a command, it must 
perform said command.  Albert, when hearing the word tuna, understands 
it is time to caterwaul until the can is open and the bowl filled.

This exchange shows the problem of trying to beat the square peg of a 
'natural language' into the round hole of a 'logical language'.  With 
words that can mean so many different things depending upon the context 
it would be impossible to construct a machine that could reliably read 
the language.

Would it be a useful language if sometimes the "READ" command was 
understood by the computer to get the next record -- while other times 
the "READ" command caused the computer to open a file containing the 
latest Stephen King novel and enter a wait loop?

I suspect I've gone on far to long about your misunderstanding of my use 
of the word 'understand'.
```

_(reply depth: 39)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2004-09-15T09:49:11-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ci9h8n$pl4$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <joe_zitzelberger-921801.09193115092004@knology.usenetserver.com>`

```
In article <joe_zitzelberger-921801.09193115092004@knology.usenetserver.com>,
Joe Zitzelberger  <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:

[snip]

>I suspect I've gone on far to long about your misunderstanding of my use 
>of the word 'understand'.

Me, personally, I try to avoid using words like 'understanding'... they 
get downright knotty ('What are the phenomena which must be demonstrated 
in order to apply to a situation the evaluation of 'Now it is 
understood'?') and rather than try to fulminate, fume and Hume my way into 
a Critique of such things I'll lazily fall back into Wittgenstein's 
example of 'perfect language':

Two men are working together, building a wall.

One holds out his hand and says 'Brick'.

The other places a brick into the hand.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 45)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2004-09-16T08:04:27-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cibvgb$oe9$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0409151103.42bf4b4a@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-959438.20004115092004@knology.usenetserver.com> <HO62d.423040$M95.10738@pd7tw1no>`

```
In article <HO62d.423040$M95.10738@pd7tw1no>,
James J. Gavan <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:
>Joe Zitzelberger wrote:

[snip]

>>Joe O'Zitzelberger
>>
>Next it willl be Abraham O'Goldberg  !

Well... reminds me of the story of the rabbi who spent his time in 
crossing the Atlantic in steerage trying to learn some English; he'd heard 
that upon arriving at Ellis Island the first question he'd be asked was 
'Can you speak English?' and he wanted to improve his chances.

He gets up to the desk where a bored civil servant, himself an immigrant 
from County Cork, looks down at him and barks out 'What's yer name?'... 
and those hours spent studying in the hold of the ship slip right out of 
his brain.  Meekly he whispers -

'hab' ich schon vergessen' ('i already forgot')

 - and the immigration official can barely hear this so he asks again, 
'What's Your Name?'... and the rabbi repeats, with a bit more volume -

'Hab' Ich schon vergessen!'

... and the official frowns... and shrugs... and writes down what he 
heard... and years later, folks were amazed when they walked past a small 
synagogue on the Lower East Side of Manhattan and saw the sign, telling 
the world that this congregation had:

RABBI: Shawn Fergusson

DD
```

```
I like the guy who went to the judge to change his last name.    The judge asked
what name he wanted, and he said "Foist".   The judge asked him to repeat it,
and he did.    So he made the change.

After the court proceedings, the judge came up to him and asked why he wanted
such a name.   Mr. Foist answered, in a strong Brooklyn accent "In the Old
Country, my family's name was Primero".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 44)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-09-16T14:01:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cic6bd$t8l$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-921801.09193115092004@knology.usenetserver.com> <ci9h8n$pl4$1@panix5.panix.com> <pcY1d.14445$0h7.1114235@news20.bellglobal.com> <ci9kg5$4id$1@panix5.panix.com> <217e491a.0409151103.42bf4b4a@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-959438.20004115092004@knology.usenetserver.com>`

```
English has more far more words in it than any other language, primarily due to
England's history of being conquered and in conquering.   e.g.   Normans had
beef, pork, and venison, while anglo-saxons had cows, pigs, and deer.

Some programming languages also import words and syntax of other languages.   
CoBOL has had a board (as does the French language), which controls its purity.
  But library based languages are promiscuous - accepting just about anything.


Moving away from language-centric thought, OO based development can be done
without even caring about the languages involved.



BTW:    I never understood where the word "assembler" came from.    An assembly
language program isn't assembled into machine language.    The programmer does
the assembling of the commands, the assembler only translates them.    
Macro-Assemblers do some assembling, but that's an enhancement of the idea after
the term was created.

It's fun though that after the word assembler was in use - that the new improved
language couldn't use the same word - so they found the word compile to use.

Other places where words had to be changed (for marketing?) include:

Hierarchal database -> network database (I can't say I am a network database
person anymore though, but have to regress to the earlier term)

conservative/conservationist/preservative/preservationist

atomic power -> nuclear power     This change occurred about the time of the
hydrogen bomb, and the press really believed that changing the word was
meaningful.    Isaac Asimov late in life re-released his Foundation trilogy.  
He avoided the temptation to re-write with his improved writing skills, but he
did change every reference of "atomic" to "nuclear", as our language had changed
since he first wrote that series.

Data base.    This can mean just about anything, depending upon the audience.

Operating System
```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:cic6bd$t8l$1@peabody.colorado.edu...

> Hierarchal database -> network database (I can't say I am a network
database
> person anymore though, but have to regress to the earlier term)

I think these are two entirely different styles of data base design, and
have seen data bases built under both styles.  The only way I can see any
relationship between them is if one assumes an equivalence like hierarchical
= network = unnormalized = nonrelational = obsolete.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

> > Hierarchal database -> network database (I can't say I am a network
> database
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> = network = unnormalized = nonrelational = obsolete.

Part of the confusion.    When IDMS came out with a hierarchal database that
allowed multiple owners, they called it a network database.   Nobody else paid
any attention, so later on a different type of network database was invented.

…[8 more quoted lines elided]…

Possibly.   I'm not sure the date when IDMS (or its predecessor) included
multiple ownership.   It is possible that they had it before they came up with
the term "network", which I suspect was to differentiate them from IMS, which
came out after IDMS did.    My Burroughs experience at the time (around 1980)
did not include database work.

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0409161112.1a46b0c8@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-921801.09193115092004@knology.usenetserver.com> <ci9h8n$pl4$1@panix5.panix.com> <pcY1d.14445$0h7.1114235@news20.bellglobal.com> <ci9kg5$4id$1@panix5.panix.com> <217e491a.0409151103.42bf4b4a@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-959438.20004115092004@knology.usenetserver.com> <cic6bd$t8l$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote 

> BTW:    I never understood where the word "assembler" came from.    An assembly
> language program isn't assembled into machine language.    The programmer does
> the assembling of the commands, the assembler only translates them. 

Originally computers had to be programmed by punching cards with 20
machine code words per card.  An assembler program allowed individual
or groups of machine instructions to be on different cards and it
would assemble them to 20 per card.  This made it much easier to
change a program by adding or reordering instructions. Later the
assembler program would allow the instruction to be a mnemonic code
rather than a bit pattern.

> Macro-Assemblers do some assembling, but that's an enhancement of the idea after
> the term was created.

So is mnemonic -> binary code an enhancement.
 

> Other places where words had to be changed (for marketing?) include:
> 
> Hierarchal database -> network database (I can't say I am a network database
> person anymore though, but have to regress to the earlier term)

No.  Hierarchical and network databases are different things, it is
not just a name change. I have (decades ago) worked with a
hierarchical database that was tape based. Many hierarchical dbs added
features which made it networked (sort of).

> Operating System

An Operating System is some program or function that does
automatically what an operator would otherwise have to do.  For
example write a label on a tape reel. Originally the operator wrote
the name, date etc and stuck it on the tape and wrote this into the
directory of tapes.  Later the computer wrote a tape label with the
name and date etc and wrote this at the start of the reel and into its
directory.

Operators would have to decide which program to run next, later the
Operating System decided this.

What about Mainframe.

In all of last century large electronic equipment was assembled in
frames, usually with covers - telephone exchanges, radio equipment,
etc. There was usually a main frame for the central equipment and
peripheral frames around the outside.  For electronic or
electro-mechanical computers the 'mill' (or computer) was housed in
the main frame in the centre of the room while i/o devices were housed
in the frames around the peripheral of the room.

They are called mainframe computers because the computer requires a
main frame (plus other frames).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 46)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-09-16T19:44:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cicqf1$fjo$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-921801.09193115092004@knology.usenetserver.com> <ci9h8n$pl4$1@panix5.panix.com> <pcY1d.14445$0h7.1114235@news20.bellglobal.com> <ci9kg5$4id$1@panix5.panix.com> <217e491a.0409151103.42bf4b4a@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-959438.20004115092004@knology.usenetserver.com> <cic6bd$t8l$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0409161112.1a46b0c8@posting.google.com>`

```

On 16-Sep-2004, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

> > Hierarchal database -> network database (I can't say I am a network database
> > person anymore though, but have to regress to the earlier term)
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> features which made it networked (sort of).

IDMS (At least Cullinane/Cullinet, I'm not sure about CA) has called their
hierarchical database a "network" database.    Their definition has nothing to
do with computer networks, but more likely with the Bachman diagram showing
owner and children records.    If it could have multiple owners, it was a
"network" database.   Nowadays the term causes confusion because there is
another database structure of the same name.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 45)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2004-09-20T16:33:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cin0or02lu6@news3.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-921801.09193115092004@knology.usenetserver.com> <ci9h8n$pl4$1@panix5.panix.com> <pcY1d.14445$0h7.1114235@news20.bellglobal.com> <ci9kg5$4id$1@panix5.panix.com> <217e491a.0409151103.42bf4b4a@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-959438.20004115092004@knology.usenetserver.com> <cic6bd$t8l$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

In article <cic6bd$t8l$1@peabody.colorado.edu>, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> writes:
> English has more far more words in it than any other language

This claim, though popular, is dubious.  See eg [1], particularly the
links to some interesting discussions of the matter in sci.lang.  In
the "Biggest language (vocabulary)?" discussion, for example, one
poster suggests how the "biggest vocabulary" claim could be made for
the polysynthetic incorporating languages, such as Inuit.

Generally speaking, these "big dict" contests (to borrow a phrase from
a recent alt.folklore.computers thread) are considered meaningless by
linguists - there just isn't any valid basis for determining the size
of a natural language's vocabulary, or for comparing those of two
languages.


1. http://www.cs.tut.fi/~jkorpela/lang/vocab.html
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 48)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-20T13:26:46-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cinee7$2gts$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-921801.09193115092004@knology.usenetserver.com> <ci9h8n$pl4$1@panix5.panix.com> <pcY1d.14445$0h7.1114235@news20.bellglobal.com> <ci9kg5$4id$1@panix5.panix.com> <217e491a.0409151103.42bf4b4a@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-959438.20004115092004@knology.usenetserver.com> <cic6bd$t8l$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cin0or02lu6@news3.newsguy.com> <cin2nb$28u6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cin505$cih$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Actually, I think it'd be rather easier; German has *very* few spelling
irregularities.  There aren't all that many languages in which *spelling*
requires as much effort as in modern English.  One of the problems is that
English spelling became fixed around the same time as the Great Vowel Shift
that marked the transition from Middle to Modern English.  The conservative
Middle English spelling was retained and preferred even when the
pronunciation changed almost to the point of unrecognizability.

    -Chuck Stevens


In most languages that use some sort of alphabet, the spelling reflects the
phonemes pretty closely; in English, the spellings most often reflect the
phonemes as they existed in one of the Anglo-Saxon dialects, or French, or
Norse a number of centuries ago.  The pronunciation changed (most notably
the Great English Vowel Shift described by the Brothers Grimm, yes, *those*
Brothers Grimm), the spelling, however, didn't.

    -Chuck Stevens


"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:cin505$cih$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On 20-Sep-2004, "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
>
> > I agree.  See the "Language and Literature" section of the Encarta
article
> > on Inuit at http://encarta.msn.com/ .   In many cases the distinction
> > between a "word" and a "sentence" in that language disappears.
>
> I wonder how they write spell checkers in, say German.    It would be hard
to
> parse out all of the compound words to determine if they were spelled
correctly.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 48)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-21T15:59:23-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ciqbob$1eo0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <cic6bd$t8l$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cin0or02lu6@news3.newsguy.com> <cin2nb$28u6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cin5fh$741$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:cin5fh$741$1@panix5.panix.com...
> In article <cin2nb$28u6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>,
> Chuck Stevens <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >I agree.  See the "Language and Literature" section of the Encarta
article
> >on Inuit at http://encarta.msn.com/ .   In many cases the distinction
> >between a "word" and a "sentence" in that language disappears.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Well, I thought so.  My professors back in college went even further, and
asserted that that particular language family didn't distinguish between a
"word" and a "sentence" to begin with.

My personal take on this is that the imposition of the distinction between
"word" and "sentence" in the Eskimaleut languages is an artifice to aid
those who are trying to deal with transition from a "word-orientation" to an
"utterance-orientation".  Kind of like trying to draw a firm line between
the list of Chinese verbs and the list of Chinese adjectives, or trying to
impose a true verb tense onto the grammar of Hebrew.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

Rick Smith <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:ciqk7j$d16$1@panix5.panix.com...
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>H'm! Kidding? Joking? Amusing.

I was inspired by an oft-reported anecdote, here taken from:

http://www.crookedtimber.org/archives/002271.html

--begin quoted text:

(When Austin asserted to a Columbia faculty audience that although in many 
languages two negatives make a positive ("He is not unlike his sister"), 
"we know of no language in which two positives make a negative", 
Morgenbesser muttered from the audience, "Yeah, yeah.")

--end quoted text

DD
```

On 22-Sep-2004, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> (When Austin asserted to a Columbia faculty audience that although in many
> languages two negatives make a positive ("He is not unlike his sister"),
> "we know of no language in which two positives make a negative",
> Morgenbesser muttered from the audience, "Yeah, yeah.")

"Yeah, right" also works.

But as the discussion of words vs utterances illustrates that we can
contextually change the meaning of virtually anything.

Humpty Dumpty knew who the master was.


In programming, overloading can do this.   Keywords limit this.    It would be
interesting to know enough about Inuit to analyze how programming languages
could compare to it.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 48)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-22T12:06:44-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cisig4$2vma$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <cic6bd$t8l$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cin0or02lu6@news3.newsguy.com> <cin2nb$28u6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <cin5fh$741$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
In all seriousness, consider as a model for comparison an "inflected"
language like Latin.

The way the Eskimaleut languages were described to me was that it was sort
of like the form of the part of the utterance that served as the "subject"
was dependent not only on the tense of the verb phrase but also the gender
of the direct object, the number of the indirect object if any, the sort and
location of conjunctions used anywhere in the utterance, and the presence of
a negative in any prepositional phrase.   This may be a gross exaggeration,
but it gives the general idea.

In such an environment, the concept of "word" or even "word stem" doesn't
really work in describing the language or its grammar.

    -Chuck Stevens

.
<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:cin5fh$741$1@panix5.panix.com...
> In article <cin2nb$28u6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>,
> Chuck Stevens <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

article
> >on Inuit at http://encarta.msn.com/ .   In many cases the distinction
> >between a "word" and a "sentence" in that language disappears.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

```
On 15 Sep 2004 12:16:18 -0400, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <37ngk0tda284bm7asa956v15rh99v57lrv@4ax.com>,
>Robert Wagner  <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>might appear to contradict that assertion.

[Initial posting to C.L.C.]
I need brick.

[First response]
The word brick doesn't tell us much. To answer your question, we need
to know the operating system, name of the brick factory and batch
number (every batch is different, you know), and the intended usage.
Do you need a structural brick or a veneer brick? Must it be
waterproof? Have you considered concrete block or galvanized sheet
metal.

[Second response]
No matter what your problem, our general-purpose Wonder Brick (tm)
will solve it. If you are building a well, we offer a single-builder
package for $4,000. In addition, we'll need to know the number of
people who'll be drawing water from your well. Given that, we can
structure a run-time package for 1-5, 6-10, 11-25, etc. Cost per user
can be as low as $59 per year, which we think you will find attractive
in comparison to other brick companies. 

[Third response, quoting the Wikipedia brick standard]

"Masonry is strong in compression (vertical loads), but is relatively
weak when subject to tension or sideways loads, unless reinforced.
Walls are often strengthened against sideways loads by thickening the
entire wall, or by building masonry piers (vertical columns or ribs)
at intervals.

The strength of a masonry wall is not entirely dependent on the bond
between the building material and the mortar; the friction between the
interlocking blocks of masonry is often strong enough to provide a
great deal of strength on its own. The blocks sometimes have grooves
or other surface features added to enhance this interlocking, and some
masonry structures forego mortar altogether. Stone masonry without the
use of mortar was common in early civilizations."

Have you considered ALL the perils and researched ALL the
alternatives? Have you considered the possibility there may have been
one brick produced since the dawn of civilization that you DON'T need?

I thought not. Call back when you know what you're talking about.

[Follow-up by original poster]
What are you people yammering about? I just need bricks to build the
wall of my well. I have several pallets that some guy sent, but I
can't figure out how to get the bricks off them. They're wrapped in
plastic or something.

[RW]
Just cut the plastic off with a utility knife. The bricks underneath
are probably the common type. No problem.

[Richard]
Once again, you've done the poster a disservice by failing to
stimulate him to investigate. You don't KNOW what kind of bricks are
underneath. Besides, you were wrong about something two years ago, so
you're wrong about this too.

[DocDwarf]
Centuries before Pliny, Aristotle described precisely the phenomenon
of well water rising from and returning to the sea.

[Seventh response]
Six professionals have taken the time to respond, without
acknowledgement. Don't you love us?

[Eighth response]
Damned ingrates.
```

- **References:** `<217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <joe_zitzelberger-921801.09193115092004@knology.usenetserver.com> <ci9h8n$pl4$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:ci9h8n$pl4$1@panix5.panix.com...
> Me, personally, I try to avoid using words like 'understanding'... they
> get downright knotty ('What are the phenomena which must be demonstrated
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I remember vaguely a sequence from "Beyond the Fringe" involving a visit to
Bertrand Russell.  It ran something like this, where the visitor is speaking
in first person:

I entered the room and noticed a basket in Mr. Russell's lap, and after we
were introduced I commented "Tell me.  Are there any apples in that basket?"
Mr. Russell said "No."  After a short pause, I then queried "Are there some
apples in that basket?"  Mr. Russell said "No," and smiled seraphically as
was his wont.  That left me in a logical cleft stick from which there seemed
but one escape:  "Are there, then, apples in that basket?"  Mr. Russell
exclaimed "YES!", and from that day forward we remained the very closest of
friends.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

Oops! Should have said Multiply.

Multiply A by B.

B is the object of Multiply. A is the object of by.

Also, the OPEN statement is missing a preposition that would
occur in English syntax.

OPEN [FOR] INPUT file-name ...
or
OPEN file-name ... FOR INPUT
