###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 37)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-15T02:50:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<j6O1d.1380394$6p.237151@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com> <10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com> <d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <ci468f$naj$1@panix5.panix.com> <vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <c5mdk0l0c7g8g2f2uea3qrsf16ub6pfbte@4ax.com> <ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <10kfaieo0lolh13@corp.supernews.com>`

```
I think your problem with

   Multiply A by B

is not the "syntax" but the "semantics" (which is why the COBOL "rule" that 
bothers you is a General Rule - not a Syntax rule) - that is, I think you are 
"bothered" by the fact that the resultant value is placed in "B".

Certainly as far as English syntax goes, it is valid to say

    Go home by car
or
    Write the paper by hand

in those cases the "syntax" is the same as in the Multiply example - but the 
"semantics" of BY is different.  I don't think that there is any doubt that many 
"COBOL words" have different semantic meanings than in "normal English" - just 
think of "move" which (by normal English semantics) should have been the "COPY" 
statement (as it doesn't "destroy" the original or "remove" it from its original 
place).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 38)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2004-09-15T02:59:53-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10kfq60f8i8pi30@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com> <10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com> <d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <ci468f$naj$1@panix5.panix.com> <vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <c5mdk0l0c7g8g2f2uea3qrsf16ub6pfbte@4ax.com> <ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <10kfaieo0lolh13@corp.supernews.com> <j6O1d.1380394$6p.237151@news.easynews.com>`

```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:j6O1d.1380394$6p.237151@news.easynews.com...
> I think your problem with
>
>    Multiply A by B
>
> is not the "syntax" but the "semantics" (which is why the COBOL "rule"
that
> bothers you is a General Rule - not a Syntax rule) - that is, I think you
are
> "bothered" by the fact that the resultant value is placed in "B".
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> in those cases the "syntax" is the same as in the Multiply example - but
the
> "semantics" of BY is different.  I don't think that there is any doubt
that many
> "COBOL words" have different semantic meanings than in "normal English" -
just
> think of "move" which (by normal English semantics) should have been the
"COPY"
> statement (as it doesn't "destroy" the original or "remove" it from its
original
> place).

Mr Klein, I am not relying on semantics. BY is a preposition
regardless of its semantics and A and B need not be defined
or, if defined, could be alphanumeric. Since COBOL does
not define elements of English syntax such as: 'noun',
'preposition', or 'prepositional phrase'; it was necessary to
determine an equivalence between the parts of COBOL
statements and elements of the English language.

Syntax deals with the relation between the elements of a
language. That imperative verbs act on objects and that
prepositional phrases modify the relation between a verb
and its object are English syntax. By mapping the parts of
COBOL statements to elements of English syntax,
discrepancies with English syntax may be shown.

Given 'Multiply A by B', substitute the elements of English
syntax. This gives 'verb noun preposition noun'. The object
of the verb 'Multiply' is the noun 'B' which follows the
preposition 'by' and the elements of the prepositional phrase
are reversed. These are discrepancies with English syntax
because the noun following a verb should be its object and
the noun following a preposition should be its object.

Given 'Open input in-file', substitute the elements giving
'verb noun noun'. The object of the verb 'Open' is the noun
'in-file', which follows the noun 'input' which modifies the
relation between the verb and its object, in other words, the
object of the verb follows the object of a preposition. These
are discrepancies with English syntax because the noun
following a verb should be its object and what should be a
prepositional phrase does not contain a preposition.

In fact, the 'Open' example is exactly the same 'verb noun noun'
relationship as 'Invoke Cake "Bake"' which also violates English
syntax for the same reasons.

I have shown three cases where COBOL statements violate
English syntax.

Mr Wagner wrote:
"I can't think of a case where Cobol's procedure division
syntax violates English syntax. Can you? The rule-breaker
is INVOKE (or CALL) taking as its object a method name
written as a non-gerundial verb."

Mr Wagner states that 'a method name written as a
non-gerundial verb' is a 'rule-breaker' ('violates English syntax').
In fact, Mr Wagner's complaint is unrelated to English syntax;
because, as I said four days ago, 'The name of a method is
a noun.' This statement is true even when the name is selected
from a list of 'non-gerundial verb[s]'.

Mr Wagner is not incapable of using verbs as nouns. The text
'INVOKE (or CALL)' uses both INVOKE and CALL as
nouns. It is still a mystery why he would insist that using verbs
as nouns in a COBOL program is, somehow, ungrammatical.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 39)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-15T14:35:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3jjgk0pbc6t3vlddptis35vmogaec33rbk@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <c5mdk0l0c7g8g2f2uea3qrsf16ub6pfbte@4ax.com> <ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <10kfaieo0lolh13@corp.supernews.com> <j6O1d.1380394$6p.237151@news.easynews.com> <10kfq60f8i8pi30@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Wed, 15 Sep 2004 02:59:53 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
wrote:

>Given 'Open input in-file', substitute the elements giving
>'verb noun noun'. The object of the verb 'Open' is the noun
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>prepositional phrase does not contain a preposition.

No, Input is an adjecive in this case. The Input (noun) is the data
residing in, and/or about to be written to, the file.

>In fact, the 'Open' example is exactly the same 'verb noun noun'
>relationship as 'Invoke Cake "Bake"' which also violates English
>syntax for the same reasons.

It violates English because it has 'verb noun verb'. Putting quotes
around Bake doesn't change it from verb to noun.

>Mr Wagner wrote:
>"I can't think of a case where Cobol's procedure division
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>as nouns in a COBOL program is, somehow, ungrammatical.

The words Method, Action and Verb are defined as nouns. However words
classified as one of the three, such as Invoke, Call and a method
name, are defined as verbs and are properly used in the grammatical
center of a predicate to describe an action performed upon an object. 

If you want to perform an action on an action, you have to turn it
into an infinitive (by putting To in front) or a gerund (by putting
-ing at the end). In cases such as 'an archaeological find' an
instance of a action's output can be used as a noun. However, it
doesn't make much sense to talk about the end result before knowing
what it will be.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 40)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-15T09:45:46-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ci9rjr$2clj$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <c5mdk0l0c7g8g2f2uea3qrsf16ub6pfbte@4ax.com> <ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <10kfaieo0lolh13@corp.supernews.com> <j6O1d.1380394$6p.237151@news.easynews.com> <10kfq60f8i8pi30@corp.supernews.com> <3jjgk0pbc6t3vlddptis35vmogaec33rbk@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:3jjgk0pbc6t3vlddptis35vmogaec33rbk@4ax.com...

> No, Input is an adjecive in this case. The Input (noun) is the data
> residing in, and/or about to be written to, the file.

The only adjectives defined and mentioned as such in the COBOL standard are
ALL, LEADING and FIRST in the context of the INSPECT statement.  INPUT is
not in that list.   (I find no instance of "adjecive" in the standard).

Technically speaking, according to the grammar of COBOL, INPUT is a *key
word* that introduces the *input phrase* specifying a particular function.
If I were to assign *English* semantics to it, I would argue that it
functions *adverbially*.    I'm not sure what I would argue if I were
applying Swahili or Tagalog or APL semantics to it, though.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 40)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2004-09-15T13:41:11-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10kgvokos8vnm39@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <c5mdk0l0c7g8g2f2uea3qrsf16ub6pfbte@4ax.com> <ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <10kfaieo0lolh13@corp.supernews.com> <j6O1d.1380394$6p.237151@news.easynews.com> <10kfq60f8i8pi30@corp.supernews.com> <3jjgk0pbc6t3vlddptis35vmogaec33rbk@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:3jjgk0pbc6t3vlddptis35vmogaec33rbk@4ax.com...
> On Wed, 15 Sep 2004 02:59:53 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
> wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> residing in, and/or about to be written to, the file.

FDIS, page 484, 14.8.26.3 General rules (OPEN statement)
"2) The successful execution of an OPEN statement associates
the file connector referenced by file-name-1 with a physical file
if the physical file is available, and sets the open mode of the file
connector to input, output, I-O, or extend, depending on the
keywords INPUT, OUTPUT, I-O or EXTEND specified in the
OPEN statement. The open mode determines the input-output
statements that are allowed to reference the file connector as
shown in table 21, Permissible I-O statements by access mode
and open mode."

Furthermore, 14.8.26.2 Syntax rules, on the same page, use
the word 'phrase' in association with the keywords shown
above.

[for: with the object or purpose of. -- RHCD]

The object of OPEN is ('the file connector referenced by') the
file-name. The INPUT phrase is 'with the purpose of' setting the
open mode. Thus 'Open input in-file' is syntacically equivalent
to 'Open in-file for input'. The last is 'verb noun preposition noun';
but was mapped from 'verb noun noun'. The OPEN statement
is inconsistent with English syntax.

> >In fact, the 'Open' example is exactly the same 'verb noun noun'
> >relationship as 'Invoke Cake "Bake"' which also violates English
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> around Bake doesn't change it from verb to noun.

Bake, as an identifier, is also a noun.

[snip]
> The words Method, Action and Verb are defined as nouns. However words
> classified as one of the three, such as Invoke, Call and a method
> name, are defined as verbs and are properly used in the grammatical
> center of a predicate to describe an action performed upon an object.

I have not seen anything in the FDIS defining method names
as verbs. Perhaps, Mr Wagner, you could direct me to your
source.

FDIS, page 652, 16.1.1 New method
"The New method is a factory method that provides a standard
mechanism for creating instance objects of a class."

Mr Wagner, the word 'new' is an adjective. Are you suggesting
that, in 'Invoke CakeClass "new" ...', 'new' is a verb; because
'method name [is] defined as [a] verb'?

And, if you are free to call 'new' a verb, why am I not free to
call it a noun?
```

wrote:

Yes. New is a constructor. It 'instantiates' new objects.

diminished. People would say Cobol isn't faithful to its roots.

```
RW,
  Are you aware that the '02 Standard removed all references to "verbs" (e.g. in 
the '85 Standard "Move" was a verb).

This was (at least partially) due to the fact that "IF" was considered a "verb" 
in the '85 Standard.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 42)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-09-16T00:12:01-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0409152312.158a05fa@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <c5mdk0l0c7g8g2f2uea3qrsf16ub6pfbte@4ax.com> <ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <10kfaieo0lolh13@corp.supernews.com> <j6O1d.1380394$6p.237151@news.easynews.com> <10kfq60f8i8pi30@corp.supernews.com> <3jjgk0pbc6t3vlddptis35vmogaec33rbk@4ax.com> <10kgvokos8vnm39@corp.supernews.com> <qprhk0pi228ce7titonc05sd95g94s9jq7@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

> >Mr Wagner, the word 'new' is an adjective. Are you suggesting
> >that, in 'Invoke CakeClass "new" ...', 'new' is a verb; because
> >'method name [is] defined as [a] verb'?
> 
> Yes. New is a constructor. It 'instantiates' new objects. 

In English the word 'new' is indeed an adjective, exactly as you have
used it above.  Its use in OO is idiomatic sa in:

       myClass myObject = new myClass(parameters);

in which it may be cosidered as an adjective.  You should note that in
the OO idiom 'new' is _not_ the contructor, myClass(..) is.

Looking at Fujitsu, in order to fit the OO idiom into Cobol it uses
'new' as a method-id that is invoked as if it were the above form.

'New' is _not_ the constructor, 'create' is.  From the docs:

"An object is created as the result of the CREATE method invoked by a
factory object.  The CREATE method is invoked by the NEW method of the
FJBASE class."

While 'new' is used to return the new object reference, the contructor
is 'create'.
 
> >And, if you are free to call 'new' a verb, why am I not free to
> >call it a noun?
> 
> I didn't choose to make New into a verb. Seems to me Construct would
> have been a better choice.

'New' isn't a verb, it is (in Cobol) the name of a method.

> You can call anything whatever you want. That doesn't make it right.

It doesn't make you right either.

> If you gotJ4 to agree, it still wouldn't be right and Cobol would be
> diminished. People would say Cobol isn't faithful to its roots.

J4 have to navigate a path between 'Cobol roots' and 'OO idiom' for
these OO parts. I doubt that anything they do would be to your liking.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 42)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-16T09:13:05-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cice2h$14vn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <c5mdk0l0c7g8g2f2uea3qrsf16ub6pfbte@4ax.com> <ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <10kfaieo0lolh13@corp.supernews.com> <j6O1d.1380394$6p.237151@news.easynews.com> <10kfq60f8i8pi30@corp.supernews.com> <3jjgk0pbc6t3vlddptis35vmogaec33rbk@4ax.com> <10kgvokos8vnm39@corp.supernews.com> <qprhk0pi228ce7titonc05sd95g94s9jq7@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:qprhk0pi228ce7titonc05sd95g94s9jq7@4ax.com...

> >Mr Wagner, the word 'new' is an adjective. Are you suggesting
> >that, in 'Invoke CakeClass "new" ...', 'new' is a verb; because
> >'method name [is] defined as [a] verb'?
>
> Yes. New is a constructor. It 'instantiates' new objects.

"New" may be a "constructor" in some languages.  The term "constructor" does
not appear in ISO/IEC 1989:2002.   "New" is a *method* in the BASE class.

> I didn't choose to make New into a verb.

Neither did the COBOL standards committee.

>Seems to me Construct would have been a better choice.

Woulda.  Coulda.  Shoulda.

> You can call anything whatever you want. That doesn't make it right.

The COBOL standard defines the "parts of speech" as they appear *in COBOL*.

> If you gotJ4 to agree, it still wouldn't be right and Cobol would be
> diminished.

No, in fact I would argue COBOL is *enhanced* both in function and in
utility, particularly to those who wish to use COBOL in contexts other than
the US and the UK.

> People would say Cobol isn't faithful to its roots.

What people?  The Danes?  The South Koreans?  The Germans?  The Japanese?
The Finns?  The Luxembourgeoise?   The Venezuelans?

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 44)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-09-16T20:07:17-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0409161907.5b16f115@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<c5mdk0l0c7g8g2f2uea3qrsf16ub6pfbte@4ax.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <10kfaieo0lolh13@corp.supernews.com> <j6O1d.1380394$6p.237151@news.easynews.com> <10kfq60f8i8pi30@corp.supernews.com> <3jjgk0pbc6t3vlddptis35vmogaec33rbk@4ax.com> <10kgvokos8vnm39@corp.supernews.com> <qprhk0pi228ce7titonc05sd95g94s9jq7@4ax.com> <gLm2d.10836$P_3.1074@fe40.usenetserver.com> <mq3kk0pn9e0ppkttiilh23gj7pg322h38u@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

> Someone said it uses a Construct method inside Base. With any luck, I
> could call Construct directly.

Now why would a programmer want to rely on 'luck' for having something
work ?

That sounds worse than relying on 'intuition' or the 'rules of
something else entirely' to get the correct results. I would strongly
suggest that if you want to be thought of as a competent programmer
you revise your strategy to include 'RingTFM'.

I have seen references that indicate the 'CREATE' method can only be
used in a class method, which is why the idiomatic 'NEW' is provided.

It may well be that you could write your own class menthod that
invokes CREATE and implements whatever else NEW does.  You may think
that it would look 'prettier', many OO programmers would despair at
some arrogant newbie OOer deliberately breaking conventions built up
and refined over nearly 40 years because they thought they were
superior to a solid foundation built on years of experience.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 43)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>

Daniel,

He can currently -  Bill made previous reference to the M/F Class 
Vocabulary, which he doesn't like, I don't like and to which Richard 
commented some time back, 'Like inventing your own language'. Give him 
his due, even Robert said "it was dangerous'.

I just dabbled to see what I got. Go to the Windows Run box and enter 
'runw vocabgen' - didn't like that I had to give it the path for runw. 
Next it assumes you already have a class in place. So I took my template 
Class ISAMFile - snag is with that in the project, all  I have is :-

*>-----------------isamfile.cbl------
copy "\copylib\isamfile.cbl".
*>----------------------------------

I still have to compile in the specific project, but this common source 
is in \Copylib along with any true 'copyfiles', associated with my 
'common' classes which I can reuse in other applications.. The Vocabgen 
didn't like my path to copy files - just as well - the following listing 
would have been five times as long !!!

Normally of course, you would probably have a short vocabulary trying to 
'swap' just a few words. Annoyingly, have used the correct path to 
generqte the vocabulary file it finishes up in \NE\Base\Bin - I kinda 
suspect that's because the runtime is expecting to find all xxx.IF files 
in one place. As generated, I'm not even sure if it would work, but 
anyway here's what it produced - which gives you an idea - look at what 
I've substituted for "new" and "closefile" :-

BTW the following isamfile.if appears as one of the first lines in your 
source, e.g.

copy "isamfile.if".

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------
      $set hidemessage(731)
       class-id.          ISAMfile is external.
       class-control.     ISAMfile                is class "isamfile"
                          FileErrors              is class "filerror"
                          .
       method-id. "new".
       linkage section.
       01 lnk-self                object reference.
       procedure division returning lnk-self
      *> vocabulary
       INVOKED as
       == new   **** ConstructAnInstance
         [[using] (<self>)]
         returning <lnk-self>
       == or
       function == Fnnew
         [[using] (<self>)]
       ==.
       end method "new".
       object.
       78 MaxRecordSize                      value 1500.
       method-id. "closeFile".
       linkage section.
       procedure division returning lnk-SqlResult
      *> vocabulary
       INVOKED as
       == closeFile **** LetsCloseTheSucker
         [[using] (<self>)]
         returning <lnk-SqlResult>
       == or
       function == FncloseFile
         [[using] (<self>)]
       ==.
       end method "closeFile".
       method-id. "deleteFile".
       linkage section.
       procedure division returning lnk-SqlResult
      *> vocabulary
       INVOKED as
       == deleteFile
         [[using] (<self>)]
         returning <lnk-SqlResult>
       == or
       function == FndeleteFile
         [[using] (<self>)]
       ==.
       end method "deleteFile".
       method-id. "DeleteRecord".
       linkage section.
       01 lnk-Key                     pic x(20).
       procedure division using lnk-Key
                          returning lnk-SqlResult
      *> vocabulary
       INVOKED as
       == DeleteRecord
         [using] ([<self>] ,
                   <lnk-Key> )
         returning <lnk-SqlResult>
       == or
       function == FnDeleteRecord
         [using] ([<self>] ,
                   <lnk-Key> )
       ==.
       end method "DeleteRecord".
       method-id. "getFilename".
       linkage section.
       01 lnk-filename                  pic x(100).
       procedure division returning lnk-filename
      *> vocabulary
       INVOKED as
       == getFilename
         [[using] (<self>)]
         returning <lnk-filename>
       == or
       function == FngetFilename
         [[using] (<self>)]
       ==.
       end method "getFilename".
       method-id. "openInputFile".
       linkage section.
       procedure division returning lnk-SqlResult
      *> vocabulary
       INVOKED as
       == openInputFile
         [[using] (<self>)]
         returning <lnk-SqlResult>
       == or
       function == FnopenInputFile
         [[using] (<self>)]
       ==.
       end method "openInputFile".
       method-id. "openIOfile".
       linkage section.
       procedure division returning lnk-SqlResult
      *> vocabulary
       INVOKED as
       == openIOfile
         [[using] (<self>)]
         returning <lnk-SqlResult>
       == or
       function == FnopenIOfile
         [[using] (<self>)]
       ==.
       end method "openIOfile".
       method-id. "openOutputFile".
       linkage section.
       procedure division returning lnk-SqlResult
      *> vocabulary
       INVOKED as
       == openOutputFile
         [[using] (<self>)]
         returning <lnk-SqlResult>
       == or
       function == FnopenOutputFile
         [[using] (<self>)]
       ==.
       end method "openOutputFile".
       method-id. "readNext".
       linkage section.
       01 lnk-ReturnValues.
       05 lnk-record.
          10.
       procedure division returning lnk-ReturnValues
      *> vocabulary
       INVOKED as
       == doreadNext
         [[using] (<self>)]
         returning <lnk-ReturnValues>
       == or
       function == FnreadNext
         [[using] (<self>)]
       ==.
       end method "readNext".
       method-id. "readRecord".
       linkage section.
       01 lnk-PrimeKey                     pic x(20).
       01 lnk-Values.
       05 lnk-record.
          10.
       procedure division using lnk-PrimeKey
                          returning lnk-Values
      *> vocabulary

       function == FnreadRecord

       end method "readRecord".
       method-id. "rewriteRecord".

                          returning lnk-SqlResult

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message 
news:3jjgk0pbc6t3vlddptis35vmogaec33rbk@4ax.com...
> On Wed, 15 Sep 2004 02:59:53 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
> wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Ok, if you think that "input" is an adjective, then consider:

Open Input file1 file2
         Output file3

This would correspond to ("normal English")

Hit the round ball balloon square kite

Ignoring the fact that English grammar requires an "and" in there (somewhere), 
the adjective "round" applies ONLY toh ball - not to "balloon" - so this is 
another place where COBOL syntax is different from English syntax.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <c5mdk0l0c7g8g2f2uea3qrsf16ub6pfbte@4ax.com> <ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <10kfaieo0lolh13@corp.supernews.com> <j6O1d.1380394$6p.237151@news.easynews.com> <10kfq60f8i8pi30@corp.supernews.com> <3jjgk0pbc6t3vlddptis35vmogaec33rbk@4ax.com> <nW%1d.1422123$y4.249030@news.easynews.com>`

```
On Wed, 15 Sep 2004 18:33:55 GMT, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>Ok, if you think that "input" is an adjective, then consider:
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>This would correspond to ("normal English")

Open for input: file1 and file2; for output: file3.

>Hit the round ball balloon square kite
>
>Ignoring the fact that English grammar requires an "and" in there (somewhere), 
>the adjective "round" applies ONLY to ball - not to "balloon" - so this is 
>another place where COBOL syntax is different from English syntax.

He taught young boys and girls. 
I bought one silver pendant and my wife bought two.
We found pre-Columbian arrowheads, grinding stones and flakers.
The boat had red, white and blue sails and flags.

The rule is an adjective can be elided if its relationship to the noun
is appositive,  not if it's restrictive.

Incorrect use:
I bought one pendant in my favorite color, silver, and my wife bought
two. [Two in your favorite color or hers?]

Correct:
They rolled the round balls and balloons down the hill.

Incorrect: 
Reject the lop-sided balls and balloons. [But the balloons are all
deflated.]
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Syntax (was: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 42)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-16T08:52:16-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ciccrg$144e$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <c5mdk0l0c7g8g2f2uea3qrsf16ub6pfbte@4ax.com> <ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <10kfaieo0lolh13@corp.supernews.com> <j6O1d.1380394$6p.237151@news.easynews.com> <10kfq60f8i8pi30@corp.supernews.com> <3jjgk0pbc6t3vlddptis35vmogaec33rbk@4ax.com> <nW%1d.1422123$y4.249030@news.easynews.com> <23shk09h44scm4jaihc8sdtbfbihk9vvog@4ax.com>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:23shk09h44scm4jaihc8sdtbfbihk9vvog@4ax.com...

> He taught young boys and girls.

The COBOL analog, because of the absence of and, is more like "teach boys
girls".

> I bought one silver pendant and my wife bought two.

And likewise "buy pendant silver wife two."

> We found pre-Columbian arrowheads, grinding stones and flakers.

Ambiguous gramattically; requires judgment and context to determine the role
of "grinding" in the sentence.

> The boat had red, white and blue sails and flags.

Ambiguous gramatically, requires judgment and context to determine what
"flags" is.

> Correct:
> They rolled the round balls and balloons down the hill.

Depends on whether it is possible to balloon down the hill or not.

> Incorrect:
> Reject the lop-sided balls and balloons. [But the balloons are all
> deflated.]

Grammatically correct, but meaningless.  Chomsky's famous *grammatically*
correct example of this is "Colorless green ideas sleep furiously".

Ambiguity is acceptable in a human language, not in a computer language.
That's one reason the parts of speech in the grammar of COBOL are things
like key words, phrases, clauses, scope delimiters and operators, and not
nouns, verbs, participles, prepositions, gerunds, etc.

You have at least two people on this forum who have college degrees in
Linguistics from universities whose faculty in that discipline is
well-respected, and you have already commented that your expertise in that
subject has come from rather less formal, and rather less mentored, study on
the subject.  A suggestion:  Never try to teach your grandmother how to milk
ducks.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 40)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-09-15T13:28:57-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0409151228.4a209f57@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <c5mdk0l0c7g8g2f2uea3qrsf16ub6pfbte@4ax.com> <ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <10kfaieo0lolh13@corp.supernews.com> <j6O1d.1380394$6p.237151@news.easynews.com> <10kfq60f8i8pi30@corp.supernews.com> <3jjgk0pbc6t3vlddptis35vmogaec33rbk@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

> No, Input is an adjecive in this case. 

Completely wrong. Cobol does not have 'adjectives'. 'INPUT' is clearly
a 'phrase' as this is how it is referred to in the specification.  It
may casually be taken as being a qualifier to the OPEN.

> The Input (noun) is the data
> residing in, and/or about to be written to, the file.

It is irrelevant whether you read it as:

        Open [as/for] input [the file] filename
   or   Open [the] input [called] filename

because the [] bits are entirely figments of your imagination and have
no significance either way, certainly not to the language, the
compiler, or the effect of running the program.  Claiming that one of
these meanderings of your mind is 'superior' is simply mental
masterbation on your part.
 
> It violates English because ..

It is irrelevant whether it 'violates' or ignores or parodies English
or Serbo-Croatian or even Java.

It is Cobol. It only needs to follow the rules set for Cobol.  These
rules may be made with some sympathty towards how that may be
understood by someone who speaks English, but once made they _are_ the
rules and only those rules are relevant.

If you don't like the way that Cobol is made then I would suggest that
you change to some other language.  I am sure that comp.lang.perl
would just love to hear your assesment of how it should follow the
rules of something else entirely.
```

- **References:** `<ci468f$naj$1@panix5.panix.com> <vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <c5mdk0l0c7g8g2f2uea3qrsf16ub6pfbte@4ax.com> <ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <10kfaieo0lolh13@corp.supernews.com> <j6O1d.1380394$6p.237151@news.easynews.com>`

```
On Wed, 15 Sep 2004 02:50:55 GMT, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:

>  I don't think that there is any doubt that many 
>"COBOL words" have different semantic meanings than in "normal English" - just 
>think of "move" which (by normal English semantics) should have been the "COPY" 
>statement (as it doesn't "destroy" the original or "remove" it from its original 
>place).

The first program I wrote depended on the sending field being cleared.
It was in assembly language, but could as well have been Cobol. After
learning that Move is really Copy, I wondered why computer people
don't speak English.

The answer came to me years later. Move sounds like it's doing useful
work whereas Copy sounds like unproductive overhead. Why copy data
somewhere else; why not use the original? Indeed, I've found poorly
written programs do a lot more unnecessary Moves than well written
ones.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joegk05mipj3jee12evgs016daukv6u7hm@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <ci468f$naj$1@panix5.panix.com> <vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <c5mdk0l0c7g8g2f2uea3qrsf16ub6pfbte@4ax.com> <ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <10kfaieo0lolh13@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Tue, 14 Sep 2004 22:32:25 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
wrote:

>"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message
>news:10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com...
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>B is the object of Multiply. A is the object of by.

No, B is the object of by. It's correct English because By can
occasionally introduce the object being affected. Consider: He came by
airplane, He did right by his children. But Cobol is internally
inconsistent. DIVIDE A BY B stores the result into A whereas MULTIPLY
A BY B stores the result into B. That IS an error.

Memory aid: if you avoid MULTIPLY ..BY, the four arithmetic verbs
store the result into the rightmost word. 

>Also, the OPEN statement is missing a preposition that would
>occur in English syntax.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>OPEN file-name ... FOR INPUT

Minor, like omitting articles.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 38)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2004-09-15T14:52:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ci9kvo0gb7@news1.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <c5mdk0l0c7g8g2f2uea3qrsf16ub6pfbte@4ax.com> <ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <10kfaieo0lolh13@corp.supernews.com> <joegk05mipj3jee12evgs016daukv6u7hm@4ax.com>`

```

In article <joegk05mipj3jee12evgs016daukv6u7hm@4ax.com>, Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> writes:
> On Tue, 14 Sep 2004 22:32:25 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
> wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> No, B is the object of by.

Specifically, "B" is the object of the preposition "by", forming the
prepositional phrase "by B", which acts as an adverb modifying the
predicate "multiply A".  "A" is the direct object of the verb
"multiply".

> It's correct English because By can
> occasionally introduce the object being affected.

It's "correct English" (that is, standard usage) because prepositional
phrases are often adverbial.  I'm not sure exactly what you mean by
"the object being affected", but "B" is the object of one thing only,
and that is the preposition "by", and a preposition does not name an
effect.

> Consider: He came by airplane,

"by airplane", again, is an adverbial prepositional phrase.  The verb
"came" here has no object (as we'd expect, since it's intransitive).

> He did right by his children.

Here "right" is the direct object and "by his children" is still an
adverbial prepositional phrase.  (Well, arguably it's an adjectival
prepositional phrase, modifying "right" - ie, describing the scope of
"rightness" - because English is, after all, ambiguous.  But it
definitely is not an object of the verb "did".)

> But Cobol is internally
> inconsistent. DIVIDE A BY B stores the result into A whereas MULTIPLY
> A BY B stores the result into B. That IS an error.

Is DIVIDE ... BY even allowed without a GIVING phrase?  The MF docs
seem to say that it isn't.

I agree that I'd expect MULTIPLY ... BY to store the result in the
first identifier, though.  If I say, in English, "now multiply your
result by 5", I expect the listener to consider the quantity named by
"result" to now be the product.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 39)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2004-09-15T23:07:23-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3k82d.10805$P_3.2148@fe40.usenetserver.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<ci9kvo0gb7@news1.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <c5mdk0l0c7g8g2f2uea3qrsf16ub6pfbte@4ax.com> <ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <10kfaieo0lolh13@corp.supernews.com> <joegk05mipj3jee12evgs016daukv6u7hm@4ax.com> <ci9kvo0gb7@news1.newsguy.com>`

```
Michael Wojcik wrote:
> 
> I agree that I'd expect MULTIPLY ... BY to store the result in the
> first identifier, though.  If I say, in English, "now multiply your
> result by 5", I expect the listener to consider the quantity named by
> "result" to now be the product.

You know, this never made sense to me, until someone (I believe it was 
in here) said that the reasoning was to make the receiving variable 
always on the right, just like it is in a "move" statement.  If COBOL 
textbooks would include that one little gem (mine didn't), I don't think 
we'd have nearly the confusion about the math verbs that we do.

So, given...

ADD      a TO   b
SUBTRACT a FROM b
MULTIPLY a BY   b
DIVIDE   a INTO b

, "b" has all our results.  (They could even make a pretty chart or 
something...)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 41)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-16T15:30:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qf9jk0hulrfm3ehrtqb8oevd9qgc47u9o3@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <c5mdk0l0c7g8g2f2uea3qrsf16ub6pfbte@4ax.com> <ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <10kfaieo0lolh13@corp.supernews.com> <joegk05mipj3jee12evgs016daukv6u7hm@4ax.com> <ci9kvo0gb7@news1.newsguy.com> <3k82d.10805$P_3.2148@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cic6lj$k1$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
On Thu, 16 Sep 2004 14:06:43 GMT, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
wrote:

>If I were inventing CoBOL, it wouldn't have the ADD, SUBTRACT, MULTIPLY, and
>DIVIDE verbs.

People would complain about the verbosity in:

compute salaried-employee-count = 
               salaried-employee-count + 1

The next release would solve the problem with:

compute salaried-employee-count += 1

People would complain that's error-prone. Omitting one character
changes all counters to 1. So the next release would have:

set salaried-employee-count up by 1

Old-timers groan, continue using += and pray it's never deleted.
Meanwhile, some scholars crash the party insisting on a whole new
paradigm, the benefit of which will be obvious to all right-thinking
programmers. Not knowing what to make of it, old timers let them
design this:

 Class-id. counter inherits from base.

 object section.
 class-control.
      base is class 'base'
      counter is class 'counter'.

 object.
 working-storage section.
 01  counter-value     comp     pic s9(09) value zero.

 method-id. 'increment'.
 procedure division.
    set counter-value up by 1
    exit method.
 end method 'increment'.
 end class 'counter'.

 working-storage section.
 01  salaried-employee-count   object pointer.

 procedure division.
     invoke counter 'new' returning salaried-employee-count.
....
    invoke salaried-employee-count 'increment'  *> add 1 to emp count
....

It's a little longer, but look at its beauty. Some programs run like a
well-oiled machine, others like an empty footlocker falling down a
flight of stairs.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 42)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-09-16T19:19:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1Hl2d.26292$%S.6223@pd7tw2no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<qf9jk0hulrfm3ehrtqb8oevd9qgc47u9o3@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <c5mdk0l0c7g8g2f2uea3qrsf16ub6pfbte@4ax.com> <ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <10kfaieo0lolh13@corp.supernews.com> <joegk05mipj3jee12evgs016daukv6u7hm@4ax.com> <ci9kvo0gb7@news1.newsguy.com> <3k82d.10805$P_3.2148@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cic6lj$k1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <qf9jk0hulrfm3ehrtqb8oevd9qgc47u9o3@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>On Thu, 16 Sep 2004 14:06:43 GMT, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
>wrote:
…[62 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Back when I started with VISOC, the precursor to Net Express  I was 
absolutely bamboozled by that word 'object' appearing all over the place 
- just couldn't figure out whether Micro Focus examples were talking 
about FACTORY or OBJECT (the INSTANCE).  But there was also another OO 
prior to VISOC - so Bill might know if the format you show started with 
that compiler.

For clarity, taking your example, I do the following :-

*>--------------
Class-id. counter inherits from base.

*> object section.  DELETE the damn thing it works without it !
 class-control.
      base is class 'base' *> Don't really know why people include this - it works 
			   *> quite well without a separate reference to Base - however
			   *> it *might* be required if using Repository - COBOL 2002 -
			   *> Haven't got N/E 4.0 - so don't know

      counter is class 'counter'.

*> REPOSITORY. instead of 'Class-Control' If updated to COBOL 2002 - as in N/E V 4.0
*> Versions 'below' N/E V4 - there are ISO Directives to give it a 'Repository look', - but
*> they  really don't do anything for you

*>------------
*>FACTORY.
*>END FACTORY
*>------------
OBJECT
*>-------------
OBJECT-STORAGE SECTION.   *> if you want more than ONE Instance in Micro Focus
*> then you definitely need this syntax - unless your Server Express has been updated
*> like Net Express in V 4.0 to comply with COBOL 2002

working-storage section.  *> You can probably get away with this if ONLY creating one
*> instance, but working-storage *is* COBOL 2002

 01  counter-value     comp     pic s9(09) value zero.

 method-id. 'increment'.
 procedure division. *> PS - really don't need this either ! (i.e. - ignore 'red tape')
    set counter-value up by 1

*> Question - what's wrong with the *mundane* - 'add 1 to counter-value'. I've got four "words" and you have five "words" ?

    exit method. *> don't need this - I only use it as an 'imperative' statement when I want
		 *> to get out of the method before 'finishing'
 end method 'increment'.
*>---------
END OBJECT.  *> Is this a freehand example - or did it compile without this reference 
	     *> to END OBJECT ?
end class 'counter'.
*>--------------

This one threw me - referenced both the Language Reference Manual and OO Manual for N/E V 4.0 - is your use of object pointer kosher - can you give me a text extract from Server Express ?

 working-storage section.
 01  salaried-employee-count   object pointer.

 procedure division.
     invoke counter 'new' returning salaried-employee-count.
....
    invoke salaried-employee-count 'increment'  *> add 1 to emp count
....

Presumably you've set up 'Counter' as a separate class so that you can create an Instance which can be referenced by several classes, each one inter-playing with 'counter' ?
I was going to write 'several classes/programs' - but I don't think you can achieve that with Procedural instead of Classes.

You might just be interested in this :-

Can't draw it, so I'll spell it out. Firstly draw a circle then include four boxes on the perimeter for classes A, B, C and D. A = North, B = East, C = South and D = West. Now draw lines joining (1) A to C, (2) B to D. Now draw lines connecting (3) A to B and D and (4)
C to B and D :-


1. A Trigger program invokes Class A. which in turn invokes Classes B, C 
and D

-          invoke ClassB "new" using self returning os-ClassB

-          invoke ClassC "new" using self returning os-ClassC

-          invoke ClassD "new" using self returning os-ClassD

2. "Self" os-ClassA,  as part of the creation process is passed to each 
of B, C and D

3. Now we can do one of two things :-

3a) os-ClassA can invoke each of the others so that :-

B receives C and D

C receives B and D

D receives B and C

e.g. -        Invoke osClassB "passHandles" using os-ClassC, os-ClassD

 The above is reasonably clear, when you follow it through.

3b) First steps into OO this second approach is  not immediately 
apparent, (but is a technique applied with GUIs returning events to a 
Business Class). Note above, I passed self (os-Class A) to each of the 
recipients. I can get the handles to the other objects in *reverse* :-

from os-ClassB invoke os-ClassA "getHandles" returning os-Others (occurs 
2 - C and D)

from os-ClassC invoke os-ClassA "getHandles" returning os-Others (occurs 
2 - B and D)

from os-ClassD invoke os-ClassA "getHandles" returning os-Others (occurs 
2 - B and C)

Now if you refer back to the diagram, each of our four class objects has 
the ability to invoke methods in  the other three. There is no limit - 
this could be applied just as easily to 12 separate classes.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```

>>OBJECT-STORAGE SECTION.   *> if you want more than ONE Instance in Micro Focus
>>*> then you definitely need this syntax - unless your Server Express has been updated
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Is that meant to be a legit question - or is it you just being arrogant 
? You indicated in an earlier message that you *wanted* instances of 
timer, as there could be more than one used in a single program.  
Further example, given three output LineSequentials in one program, I 
can create three instances from the one class template.

>For more than 2-3 classes, I'd put everything in arrays. 
>
>  
>
Then you could *well* be wrong - as an alternative :-

WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
01 os-CurrentObject  
01 myObject occurs 10 object reference value null.

Given above was Dialogs, which have been created  :-

set os-CurrentDialog to myObject(6)
invoke os-CurrentDialog  "doThis" using xxxx
No confusion - your controlling program/class is *ONLY* pointing at 
Dialog # 6 until you change it.

Apart from which - don't be surprised when you don't see Arrays in the 
J4 Standard.

- **Message-ID:** `<Tis2d.447545$gE.181812@pd7tw3no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<tpekk0lfhqbbrepg91lchutorvi0cdqq5v@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <10kfaieo0lolh13@corp.supernews.com> <joegk05mipj3jee12evgs016daukv6u7hm@4ax.com> <ci9kvo0gb7@news1.newsguy.com> <3k82d.10805$P_3.2148@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cic6lj$k1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <qf9jk0hulrfm3ehrtqb8oevd9qgc47u9o3@4ax.com> <1Hl2d.26292$%S.6223@pd7tw2no> <gv1kk0pacfuf61qntee2l7dmt2s7som55l@4ax.com> <rUo2d.27411$%S.343@pd7tw2no> <tpekk0lfhqbbrepg91lchutorvi0cdqq5v@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>Arrogance is my middle name. Ask any C.L.C. poster who doesn't like my
>opinions.
>
>  
>
It isn't your opinion that is at fault, nothing wrong with *reasoned* 
opinion. When one comes to the wrong conclusion. and on more than one 
occasion I have, it's a little blow to the ego - but admit the error and 
rethink based on info provided. Frankly taking your middle name - that's 
why I questioned so many job moves. Can't help wondering.....? (Plus you 
went to see the 'trick-cyclist').

>>You indicated in an earlier message that you *wanted* instances of 
>>timer, as there could be more than one used in a single program.  
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Well first I'm *not* copying - I've now get a second  handle to the same 
object. Give you the benefit of the doubt Robert, because I don't think 
you have dabbled in GUIs. The GUI classes are individual windows 
controls each subordinate to GUIbase, in turn subordinate to Dependent 
then Base.

So many of the methods are common  to the majority, such as "show" 
"hide" "clear" "setEmtpy" "color" etc. Which means in a controlling 
(Business) class you don't want duplicated methods/procedures which do 
the above - i.e. a separate set per Dialog.  So setting os-CurrentDialog 
to Dialog # 6 or to # 7 - means that you can apply the above methods 
without repetitious code - OR - specifically saying :-

invoke os-Dialog(6) "setEmpty" using Field-Number

 - bearing in mind I'm using a template for ALL dialogs - it becomes

invoke os-CurrentDialog "setEmpty" using Field-Number

 - and REMEMBER - at any one point in time you are only actioning ONE 
dialog

Now to GUIs and Collections - think back to our young friend who 
concluded  'Dialog System sucks' because he couldn't get his droplist to 
function properly.  ('Wonder how he made out ?). So I want to 
EditMaterials - types of metals used in Vessels (think hot water tanks). 
Left pane - record format, right pane - Listbox containing a 
SortedCollection. No problem there - only one - so I invoke os-Listbox 
"doThis".

Now the left pane the record format contains two dropdown lists (1) The 
major categories for Materials, nine of them and (2) the second, the 
sub-category for Materials, varying from 2 to some 7 per sub-category. 
Initially yes, I set up with Materials
categories so there were 10 of them (1 - 9) being the subs and 10 = 
Major, and yes, I referred to them as os-Droplist(6) initially. But tied 
into collections I wanted REUSE putting features in a super class 
DBIMain (Database Interface). At this point in time I create ten 
instances which has os-List in Global W/S and "new" returns the ten 
individual references to the Business class.

so I have :-

01 occurs 10.
     05 os-DroplistCollection object reference value null.

# 10 is static - that's the major which *never* changes - Dialog 
Droplist # 1. So set the major first :-

set os-CurrentCollection  to os-DropListCollection(10)
invoke os-CurrentDialog "setContents" using Field-Number, 
os-CurrentCollection

Note that the receving method "setContents" doesn't give a shoot what 
I'm dong - it is going to action based on Filed-Number, which 
incidentally relates to an index position in a Controlling 
OrderedCollection in the Dialog and 'set contents' with the 
vanilla-object reference I send - either OK or I'll get an error - 
trying to do it with wrong type.

New record or changes - user selects from the Dialog Droplist # 1 (the 
Major). Based on selection  I now have to 'fill' Dialog DropLisd # 2 
with the contents of say os-DropListCollection(6) :-

set os-CurrentCollection  to os-DropListCollection(6)
invoke os-CurrentDialog "setContents" using Field-Number, 
os-CurrentCollection

When I get a return event from the Dialog then using 
os-CurrentCollection it is going to automatically 'search' the 
appropriate instance, which via the super, (DBIMain), related to the 
instance,  has os-List - a copy of DropListCollection(6).

Now you can argue that you could do it either way - but I'm a lot more 
comfortable at the moment referencing by os-Currentxxxxxxx - it seems to 
me it creates less likelihood of error. As usual there's more than one 
way to skin a cat.
If only I could see some code - and boy is that sparse - then sure, I'm 
open to making improvements. There's a whole bunch of N/E users who are 
considerably smarter than I am at GUIs, plus the Web of course  - but 
you only ever see tiny snippets of code - I get the impression they fear 
if they show it, some smart ass is going to jump all over them. (Some 
dingbats of course shouldn't show you their code - like the gent who 
complained he had exceeded the 1,000 method-name limit in a class !!!!!)

>  
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
- Currently N/E - Yes, has class Array and probably will do so for many 
more moons - 'backwards support and compatibility'.

- Future - J4 - as of to-day, unless they change their minds,  only has 
Ordered, Unordered and Keyed (Dictionary)

- Seeing you mentioned 'table' - then of course Bill and I  pointed you 
at John Piggott's paper on Dynamic Tables - but that is enhancing the 
traditonal COBOL W/S Table.
```

On Fri, 17 Sep 2004 02:51:31 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:

>>>WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
>>>01 os-CurrentObject  
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>object.

'set os-CurrentDialog to myObject(6)' is copying the pointer.

> Give you the benefit of the doubt Robert, because I don't think 
>you have dabbled in GUIs. 

I have.

>The GUI classes are individual windows 
>controls each subordinate to GUIbase, in turn subordinate to Dependent 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>invoke os-CurrentDialog "setEmpty" using Field-Number

Why not abstract the dialog number in the same way you abstracted the
field number?

invoke os-Dialog(CurrentDialog) "setEmpty" using Field-Number

>If only I could see some code - and boy is that sparse - then sure, I'm 
>open to making improvements. There's a whole bunch of N/E users who are 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>complained he had exceeded the 1,000 method-name limit in a class !!!!!)

Why should there be an arbitrary limit? Sounds like someone used an
Array. Or name lookup is so slow they set a limit to avoid
embarrassment.

>>>Apart from which - don't be surprised when you don't see Arrays in the 
>>>J4 Standard.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>Ordered, Unordered and Keyed (Dictionary)

Can't Ordered and Dictionary both be accessed via a key? Why do we
need two classes that do the same thing?

>- Seeing you mentioned 'table' - then of course Bill and I  pointed you 
>at John Piggott's paper on Dynamic Tables - but that is enhancing the 
>traditonal COBOL W/S Table.

In the right way -- by removing the arbitrary limit. I wish OO people
would take the hint and make their Array class work the same.
```

Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote

> >If I were inventing CoBOL, it wouldn't have the ADD, SUBTRACT, MULTIPLY, and
> >DIVIDE verbs.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>                salaried-employee-count + 1

Would they ? What _evidence_ do you have to support that ?  Were
FORTRAN people at that time complaining about having to use that form
?

> The next release would solve the problem with:
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> changes all counters to 1. So the next release would have:

No.  Omitting one character changes _one_ counter to 1. Also, that is
called a programmer error, not a language error.

It seems that thousands of programmers (myself included) cope quite
well with operators such as +=, -=, and many others.

The same can be said of:

       ADD 10 TO Check-Position

Omitting one character (0) means that the result is not the right
answer.

> Old-timers groan, continue using += and pray it's never deleted.
> Meanwhile, some scholars crash the party insisting on a whole new
> paradigm, the benefit of which will be obvious to all right-thinking
> programmers. Not knowing what to make of it, old timers let them
> design this:

I am not sure what point you are attempting to make here with your
little fantasy productions, they seem to be like stamping around in a
temper tantrum because no one accepts your ideas uncritically.
```

I have to say that with 20/20 hindsight, there are several things
that business data processing used that were NOT considered or
even thought of at the time as some of the math type funtions were
not usually found in tab cards but on desk calculators. The funtions
were used to create graphic displays of various gross relationships
of the results of + - / *.

Warren Simmons

Howard Brazee wrote:
> If I were inventing CoBOL, it wouldn't have the ADD, SUBTRACT, MULTIPLY, and
> DIVIDE verbs.
```

_(reply depth: 40)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2004-09-17T19:11:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cifctp0ge4@news4.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <c5mdk0l0c7g8g2f2uea3qrsf16ub6pfbte@4ax.com> <ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <10kfaieo0lolh13@corp.supernews.com> <joegk05mipj3jee12evgs016daukv6u7hm@4ax.com> <ci9kvo0gb7@news1.newsguy.com> <3k82d.10805$P_3.2148@fe40.usenetserver.com>`

```

In article <3k82d.10805$P_3.2148@fe40.usenetserver.com>, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> writes:
> Michael Wojcik wrote:
> > 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> we'd have nearly the confusion about the math verbs that we do.

Sure.  Structurally it makes sense; I just agree with Robert that
it's counterintuitive, if you're intuiting from the usual meaning of
the English phrase "multiply x by y".  "by" was probably the wrong
preposition to use.  If I were inventing COBOL, I'd use "times":
"multiply a times b" can be thought of as an inversion of "multiply
b, a times" (where "b" is the direct object, and "a times" is a noun
phrase acting as an adverb), which ISTM implies that b is the
receiving item.

Nothing like a little Monday-morning language design, eh?  Even on a
Friday afternoon.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 42)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:cifg46$47q$1@peabody.colorado.edu...

> I'd just avoid that syntax altogether.   English speakers who understand
> arithmetic understand COMPUTE just fine.

I don't mind using the arithmetic verbs, but I *always* do so with GIVING,
which eliminates any ambiguity, as a matter of style.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

Robert Wagner wrote:

>On Fri, 17 Sep 2004 14:41:20 -0700, "Chuck Stevens"
><charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Hey stay with the sarcasm. You aint much good at jokes - they really 
aren't your style.

And where did you get the idea 'OO will soon be in your compiler'', I 
WISH ! It isn't going to happen. Currently 6 members on J4  of which 4 
are vendor representatives. So who on J4 has OO - Micro Focus and  IBM - 
sort of - if you take the basic class syntax, but heavily dependent upon 
using Java. The remainder, Unisys and HP - no jungle drums in the 
distance indicating the likelihood - but who knows they might be 
planning; same goes for the PC-compiler vendors.

The only other one, (non-J4), seriously considering an OO compiler - and 
no idea on introductory date - Fujitsu-Siemens, nowt to do with F/J 
directly, but a partnership between F/J and Siemens.

Without question OO in COBOL would give it a shot in the arm to compete 
right up against C++, C# and Java. It might just happen some day - but 
probably far too late to regain ground - remembering languages have to 
be proselytised - who is going to persuade the academics to give COBOL a 
second look, now that so *very* many have dropped it from their curriculum..
```

On Sat, 18 Sep 2004 03:46:43 GMT, James J. Gavan wrote:

On Sat, 18 Sep 2004 03:46:43 GMT, James J. Gavan wrote:

>And where did you get the idea 'OO will soon be in your compiler'', I 
>WISH ! It isn't going to happen. Currently 6 members on J4  of which 4 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>distance indicating the likelihood - 

   The one Unisys representative on J4 may not have yet noted that Unisys
offers a OO-COBOL for their other mainframe line, the "Clearpath IX" aka
Univac OS/1100. It is on release level 2R1. 

   Actually, Unisys has licensed the compiler from Hitachi, and the front end
has to run on a PC. 

   Manuals may be downloaded from the Unisys documentation site, publication
numbers 78515863 for volume 1 with the basic COBOL language, 78515848 for
volume 2 with the object-oriented language. There are two other volumes. 

Yours, 
L. Willms

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:3n_2d.445482$M95.294865@pd7tw1no...

> Plus blinkers were up on Hitachi, particularly as in response to a query
> from someone else, they replied 'Only marketed in Japan'. - I knew they
> had OO but I was never clear whether or not it was mainframe or a PC
> version.

My recollection (no personal experience) is that it runs on a PC with a
back-end code generator for the OS2200 architecture.  MCP management watched
the project very carefully to see how it was received, and seriously
considered a version for the MCP environment, but the reception and
consultation with existing MCP users resulted in the "wait and see" approach
being extended significantly.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OO and "mainframes" (was: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 50)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-20T20:36:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<abH3d.1740357$y4.302541@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <10kfaieo0lolh13@corp.supernews.com> <joegk05mipj3jee12evgs016daukv6u7hm@4ax.com> <ci9kvo0gb7@news1.newsguy.com> <3k82d.10805$P_3.2148@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cifctp0ge4@news4.newsguy.com> <cifg46$47q$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cifh4f$6dj$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <ekkmk09j75gauo5t6o6uvsql6pr6b1piu2@4ax.com> <ciflm0$9iq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <kt1nk0l2s6m8riekph7vt1qgoctm7o57mv@4ax.com> <DcO2d.439106$M95.88468@pd7tw1no> <yhrxbjvyyzfgbayvarqr.i48tcm5.pminews@news.t-online.de> <3n_2d.445482$M95.294865@pd7tw1no> <cimrn9$242b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
What Chuck reports for the two Unisys systems closely resembles my *impression* 
of what has happened in the IBM mainframe environments.  (z/OS "supports" OO - 
but it isn't used much; while VM and VSE do not have OO support).

Personally, I have never known how much this is a "chicken-and-the-egg" issue. 
On workstations (particularly Windows) there are "extensive" (semi-)language 
independent Class libraries - most notably for "GUI" development.  In fact, the 
most common (not only - but most common) way to do GUI's is via "OO" interfaces.

For mainframes (IBM and - according to my understanding of Chuck's comments) 
there are NOT any "commonly used" class libraries (for any programming 
language).  Without off-the-shelf class libraries (and application needs that 
they meet) there doesn't seem to be "demand" for OO programming (or programming 
languages) - without common usage of OO programming, there isn't demand for 
off-the-shelf  class libraries.

OBVIOUSLY, this is a "generalization" and there are some exceptions, but this 
does seem somewhat common to me.

NOTE:
  Even if J4/WG4 add "collection classes" *and* (although I doubt it will 
happen) mainframes offer such classes, I don't think this will (significantly) 
increase the use of OO on mainframes.  What needs to happen (for OO to "take 
off") is for some vendor to provide OS-independent, Language-independent, class 
libraries that actually MEET current application demand (for something other 
than GUI interfaces).  As most mainframes already support MOST of the things 
that applications need to do on mainframes, I don't see this happening (as I 
don't see good business cases for their development).

If there were OO class libraries for
   XML
   SQL databases

*maybe* these would be used on mainframes (and increase the use and demand for 
OO there) but without this, modular programming is viewed as meeting the 
"maintenance" issues that OO (purports to) solves and there simply is no need 
for old or new development using OO.
```

wrote:

>On 20-Sep-2004, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
>

>curious as to the mindsets and applications that do so.

There are millions of servers running Unix and having horsepower
equivalent to a mainframe. Applications include those running on
mainframes. The mindset is cost/benefit. Why spend $1M when you can
get the same bang for $10K?

> Of course it is entirely possible to have a mainframe running Unix.   I am
> curious as to the mindsets and applications that do so.

I've seen Linux and AIX running on IBM zSeries (and, for AIX, earlier
versions of the 3xx architecture) in order to run Unix applications
on a system that has production-quality hardware (also available on
upper-tier non-mainframe servers, of course) and the degree of
administrative control provided by mainframe systems.  Unix on IBM
mainframes typically runs under VM (I think you can also run it
directly on an LPAR), which makes it easy to have multiple test and
production regions on a single system, for example.  

For shops with applications under both Unix and traditional mainframe
OSes (MVS / zOS, etc), Unix on the mainframe also means one server
hardware platform to administer rather than two.  This is actually a
pretty common case, if our customer base is representative.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO and "mainframes" (was: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 51)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-20T14:11:02-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cinh16$2iks$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <10kfaieo0lolh13@corp.supernews.com> <joegk05mipj3jee12evgs016daukv6u7hm@4ax.com> <ci9kvo0gb7@news1.newsguy.com> <3k82d.10805$P_3.2148@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cifctp0ge4@news4.newsguy.com> <cifg46$47q$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cifh4f$6dj$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <ekkmk09j75gauo5t6o6uvsql6pr6b1piu2@4ax.com> <ciflm0$9iq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <kt1nk0l2s6m8riekph7vt1qgoctm7o57mv@4ax.com> <DcO2d.439106$M95.88468@pd7tw1no> <yhrxbjvyyzfgbayvarqr.i48tcm5.pminews@news.t-online.de> <3n_2d.445482$M95.294865@pd7tw1no> <cimrn9$242b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <abH3d.1740357$y4.302541@news.easynews.com>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:abH3d.1740357$y4.302541@news.easynews.com...

> If there were OO class libraries for
>    XML
>    SQL databases
>
> *maybe* these would be used on mainframes (and increase the use and demand
for
> OO there) but without this, modular programming is viewed as meeting the
> "maintenance" issues that OO (purports to) solves and there simply is no
need
> for old or new development using OO.

There was considerable discussion both within J4 and at the WG4 level on the
subject of whether XML handling should be done through the OO model or
through "traditional" COBOL syntax, and WG4's firm direction to J4 was that
it be handled in the language *first* while allowing for the specification
of standard classes for XML as a future refinement.  This decision was part
of a general pattern reflecting the sentiment that new features in COBOL
should generally be implemented *first* in COBOL syntax extensions, not in
class libraries, unless the feature were utterly unusable outside of the OO
development framework (not the case here).

I don't think SQL's been addressed by J4 at all; I agree that providing some
sort of standard COBOL syntactic support for the latest version of ISO/IEC
9075 would arguably be worthwhile, but given that ISO has published parts
1-5 and 9-10 of the 2003 version of this standard, I 'd be inclined to wait
to decide exactly how COBOL should handle this until more information about
what's in the ISO-blessed versions of parts 6-8 is available!

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO and "mainframes" (was: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 52)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-20T21:51:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ihI3d.1151607$ic1.117854@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <10kfaieo0lolh13@corp.supernews.com> <joegk05mipj3jee12evgs016daukv6u7hm@4ax.com> <ci9kvo0gb7@news1.newsguy.com> <3k82d.10805$P_3.2148@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cifctp0ge4@news4.newsguy.com> <cifg46$47q$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cifh4f$6dj$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <ekkmk09j75gauo5t6o6uvsql6pr6b1piu2@4ax.com> <ciflm0$9iq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <kt1nk0l2s6m8riekph7vt1qgoctm7o57mv@4ax.com> <DcO2d.439106$M95.88468@pd7tw1no> <yhrxbjvyyzfgbayvarqr.i48tcm5.pminews@news.t-online.de> <3n_2d.445482$M95.294865@pd7tw1no> <cimrn9$242b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <abH3d.1740357$y4.302541@news.easynews.com> <cinh16$2iks$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
Sorry if my "intent" wasn't clear(er).

I wasn't (in my reference to XML and SQL class libraries) talking about a 
"Standard" (much less a COBOL Standard).  My (personal) impression is that what 
is and isn't in a published (or draft) COBOL Standard has become 
semi-unimportant to many COBOL implementors.

What I was trying to say was that *if* there were 3rd party language-independent 
(operating system independent) class libraries for XML and or SQL, that *then* 
the mainframe users (and compiler vendors) *might* want to start using OO (and 
actually create a demand for implementations of the 2002 COBOL OO definition).

Without the creating of "independent" class libraries, I don't think there will 
be enough demand (at least on the mainframes that I know about) for 
implementation of the 2002 OO definition - much less for any enhancements (or 
Standard-defined enhancements and class libraries).
```

whatever else comes along. There's always the likelihood that J4 having 
decided and agreed the format for an XML (Extended Mark-up Language) 
file format, along comes some  Johnny who 'invents' XMDL (Extended  
Mark-down Language) !

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO and "mainframes"

_(reply depth: 53)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-21T16:31:34-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ciqdkn$1g08$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <10kfaieo0lolh13@corp.supernews.com> <joegk05mipj3jee12evgs016daukv6u7hm@4ax.com> <ci9kvo0gb7@news1.newsguy.com> <3k82d.10805$P_3.2148@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cifctp0ge4@news4.newsguy.com> <cifg46$47q$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cifh4f$6dj$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <ekkmk09j75gauo5t6o6uvsql6pr6b1piu2@4ax.com> <ciflm0$9iq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <kt1nk0l2s6m8riekph7vt1qgoctm7o57mv@4ax.com> <DcO2d.439106$M95.88468@pd7tw1no> <yhrxbjvyyzfgbayvarqr.i48tcm5.pminews@news.t-online.de> <3n_2d.445482$M95.294865@pd7tw1no> <cimrn9$242b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <abH3d.1740357$y4.302541@news.easynews.com> <cinh16$2iks$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <8x14d.65660$%S.32685@pd7tw2no>`

```

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:8x14d.65660$%S.32685@pd7tw2no...

> My two cents. I'd leave both XML and SQL well alone - they both have
> their ISO Standard, why try and incorporate into COBOL. Only recently
> switched to SQL, but I can quite happily handle SQL in OO without any OO
> SQL Classes. I may have blinkers on, but I can't visualize where
> specific SQL classes might help.

It's not necessarily that XML is being directly incorporated into the
language, but rather that the ability to manipulate XML specifications is
provided for.

> XML - just me - but can't think why J4 is considering a file type. If
> you came up with something like a Byte-Stream file concept, reading in
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Mark-down Language) !

J4 was willing, and fully prepared, to go either way.  The specific reason
that *J4* decided to go with syntax in the language for manipulating XML
rather than classes to do so, as their first priority, because that's what
*WG4* said their priority should be.

WG4 is a working group of SC22, a standing subcommittee of JTC1, a joint
technical committee of ISO and IEC.  And in case you hadn't yet noticed, the
official reference number for the standard is "ISO/IEC 1989:2002", not "ANSI
X3.23-2002".

Providing object-oriented mechanisms for XML manipulation is still on the
candidates list for future enhancements, but WG4's clear direction to J4 at
last year's meeting, with as I recall the US, New Zealand, Great Britain,
Germany and the Netherlands delegations in attendance, was "native syntax
first", and the US delegation didn't care all that much one way or the
other.  That's why the priority list *from WG4* is worded the way it is.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

>"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message

…[38 more quoted lines elided]…

Yep, I've got the message - ISO(via WG4) 'calls the shots' - the

>Providing object-oriented mechanisms for XML manipulation is still on the
>candidates list for future enhancements, but WG4's clear direction to J4 at
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

Only five countries at WG4 ? Only six J4 members ? What gives ?

 From Jerome Garfunkel, (COBOL 85 Example Book) :-

1959 - CODASYL Exec Committee - 7
1959 - CODASYL Short Range Committee  - 9
1985 - CODASYL COBOL Committee - 20
1985 - ANSI COBOL Committee - 22

1986 - ISO COBOL Committee - 7 (Austria, Canada, France,Germany, 
Netherlands, UK and US) - Well if that's all there were back in '86, I 
suppose current attendance is not bad. When are the Ukrainians and 
Chinese going to chip in at WG4 on Collections ?

2000 - J4 (when I was at Newbury -  from memory - EDS, HP, IBM, ITJC 
(you know I mean Japan, even if I have the initials wrong), MF,  Chief 
Designer for Hitachi, three of us 'representing Stephen Spiro', (boy did 
he believe in keeping his bases covered !),  Bill K., Wim from 
Netherlands and Janet and her hubby standing in for Bob Karlin; then 
John Piggott - AND nearly forgot - Robert Jones from Gloucester ! Can't 
be sure, but think there was another one there from Hitachi.. Artur was 
taken ill before emplaning, so didn't make it on behalf of F/J. Don't 
want to add insult to injury - but I can't visualize a face on behalf of 
Unisys (?). - That's a total of 16 'participants/visitors', not members..

 "COBOL, COBOL, wherefore art thou COBOL ?"

Jimmy
```
