```

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:Cl74d.487588$gE.52800@pd7tw3no...

> Yep, I've got the message - ISO(via WG4) 'calls the shots' - the
> vernacular version of your statement above :-) .

Yeah, pretty much.  J4 can *suggest*, J4 can *recommend*, but it's an
ISO/IEC standard, and what goes in and what stays out is an ISO/IEC
decision.


> Only five countries at WG4 ? Only six J4 members ? What gives ?

As to WG4, others have expressed interest, but those are the delegations I
remember being at the last WG4 meeting.

>  From Jerome Garfunkel, (COBOL 85 Example Book) :-  ...

Yes, I agree.  When I took over as Unisys rep on WG4 in late 2000 there were
>
> 1959 - CODASYL Exec Committee - 7
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> taken ill before emplaning, so didn't make it on behalf of F/J.

> Don't want to add insult to injury - but I can't visualize a face on
behalf of
> Unisys (?). -

The Unisys representative on J4 at the time was John Brieschke, who had
decades with the COBOL compilers on the OS2200.  Around this time his line
management decided his time was better spent elsewhere than on the standards
process and declined to fund his attendance at J4 or WG4 meetings.  He
missed enough meetings, including the Newbury meeting, to cause Unisys'
membership to lapse.  The line management from the ex-Burroughs "side" was
not consulted on this, and sent the chief architect for our COBOL85 compiler
to the very next J4 meeting to begin the process of restoring our
membership.  The J4 negotiations weren't his cup of tea, and I took over as
the J4 representative at the December 2000 meeting.   Since then I've missed
exactly one J4 meeting and one WG4 meeting.

> That's a total of 16 'participants/visitors', not members..

At WG4 it's member countries that count.  Even at J4, it's member
*companies* that count.

I think the situation looks worse than it actually is.

As of the December 2000 meeting in Carmel, the active members were Compaq,
EDS, Fujitsu, HP, IBM, ITSCJ, Karlins' Korner, Merant, Unisys, Victor
Consulting, Wizard Systems, and two individuals, Wim Ebbinkhuijsen and Bill
Klein.

Compaq got swallowed by HP.  Stephen Spiro (Wizard Systems) retired.
Merant's membership was restored to Micro Focus.  Wim lobbied INCITS
unsuccessfully for a less-costly "individual" INCITS membership fee and left
when this wasn't offered.  (By the way, Wim's retirement celebration will
occur at the upcoming WG4 meeting in Den Haag).  Bill Klein shifted his
membership status to "advisory" after missing a number of meetings, I
believe for health reasons.  ITSCJ membership recently lapsed for failure to
attend meetings but they hope to restore it next year.   That leaves EDS and
Fujitsu, and both, I believe, have simply decided not to continue to finance
their membership in the COBOL standardization process since then.  I do not
know their deeper motives.

All I can take responsibility for is my own participation in and
contributions toward J4, and my lobbying within Unisys to continue to
support the COBOL standardization process, and I think my record has been
pretty good over my tenure as Unisys' representative.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message 
news:cis8ha$2od0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com...
<snip>
>
> I think the situation looks worse than it actually is.
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Chuck (and others),
   Ignoring for the moment who belongs to J4 versus who actually produces papers 
(does the work) and ignoring for the moment which countries actively participate 
in WG4, the thing that "bothers" me the most is the state of the 2002 Standard 
and implementations.

By the time we reach the two year "birthday" of the 2002 Standard this December, 
to the best of my knowledge:

 - One company (Fujitsu-Siemens) has a "significantly" implemented 2002 
Standard, but I don't know what their public position is on producing a FULLY 
conforming compiler.  (If Karl is watching he may be able to comment)

- Before Dec 2002, Micro Focus indicated that their NEXT release would be fully 
conforming - but since then they have introduced a couple (???) releases and 
have NOT produced a fully conforming compiler (or made any additional 
clarifications on their stance concerning this)

- Several of the COBOL vendors *not* involved with the current J4/WG4 work have 
indicated that they see little or no "user demand" for fully conforming 
compilers (e.g. CA-Realia, RM, AcuCOBOL, KOBOL, Fujitsu)

- Several of the COBOL vendors who are involved with J4/WG4 seem (and some have 
publicly stated) that they are using a "phased in" approach to 2002 Standard 
features *and* that they do not have current demand for a fully conforming 
compiler (e.g. IBM and possibly Micro Focus).  While other J4 members (e.g. your 
Unisys - either side and the ITSCJ companies) seem to be "silent" (as least as 
far as I have heard) on this topic.

   ***

In general, my impression is that the 2002 Standard is being used as a "JOD" and 
each vendor is "picking and chosing" what features (if any) to implement and 
that "FULL portability" of source code using any of the 2002 features is highly 
restricted.

Given this status and the fact that J4/WG4 currently (I believe) hope to have 
the first public review of the draft 2008 Standard out next year, it is hard for 
me to believe that nay *programmers* (rather than vendors) will be able to 
comment on enhancements/changes - as they won't have access to a 2002 conforming 
compiler to work from.  I also don't know how many vendors will be able to 
comment as they too will not have a 2002 base to work from.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<cisls2$fs$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <10kfaieo0lolh13@corp.supernews.com> <joegk05mipj3jee12evgs016daukv6u7hm@4ax.com> <ci9kvo0gb7@news1.newsguy.com> <3k82d.10805$P_3.2148@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cifctp0ge4@news4.newsguy.com> <cifg46$47q$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cifh4f$6dj$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <ekkmk09j75gauo5t6o6uvsql6pr6b1piu2@4ax.com> <ciflm0$9iq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <kt1nk0l2s6m8riekph7vt1qgoctm7o57mv@4ax.com> <DcO2d.439106$M95.88468@pd7tw1no> <yhrxbjvyyzfgbayvarqr.i48tcm5.pminews@news.t-online.de> <3n_2d.445482$M95.294865@pd7tw1no> <cimrn9$242b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <abH3d.1740357$y4.302541@news.easynews.com> <cinh16$2iks$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <8x14d.65660$%S.32685@pd7tw2no> <ciqdkn$1g08$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Cl74d.487588$gE.52800@pd7tw3no> <cis8ha$2od0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Zvk4d.1287884$ic1.131004@news.easynews.com>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:Zvk4d.1287884$ic1.131004@news.easynews.com...

> In general, my impression is that the 2002 Standard is being used as a
"JOD" and
> each vendor is "picking and chosing" what features (if any) to implement
and
> that "FULL portability" of source code using any of the 2002 features is
highly
> restricted.

I don't disagree, Bill.  As I understand it, for the '74 and '85 standards,
and probably the '68 standard as well, a US government policy (I think both
within and without the Defense Department) required that any vendor
proposing a compiler for which a standard existed must ensure that that
compiler had been officially validated as compliant (this may not be worded
exactly correctly, but it conveys the general idea).  I went through a
couple of cycles of validation with our COBOL74 compiler, and I recall the
same being done early in the life of our COBOL85 compiler.

That requirement was relaxed sometime after the adoption of the '85
standard, I believe.

So far as I know, *no* US agency requires validation of COBOL or other
languages against a standard, current or otherwise.  Without such a blanket
requirement, the only external incentive that any implementation be fully
compliant with the latest standard is individual customer demand.  For that
matter, the only external incentive to a vendor to provide any *feature*
described in such a standard is individual customer demand.

I happen to feel that decision on the part of the US government was not a
good decision; I believe it harmed COBOL, and I believe it harmed and
continues to harm the computer industry as a whole.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
Chuck Stevens wrote:

>"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
>news:Zvk4d.1287884$ic1.131004@news.easynews.com...
…[52 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I very much agree with your comments about the DOD ( or Pentagon) opting 
out of the decision process. The layman's version I heard aeons ago, was 
that BRUNCH  wanted to sell hardware to DOD - who turned around and 
said,  "You all have different architectures, different language 
approaches, and we want a COMMON language before we buy in. We don't 
want teams of people specializing on languages related to each 
manufacturer's hardware".

On that basis, COBOL was 'cobbled' together in some six months. Really 
remarkable. It may have been from Warren I got the quote, but possibly 
in reading a history of COBOL - one of those early DOD people said 
something like, "If we had realized the impact of COBOL, we wouldn't 
have taken a short-term view. We would have hung in there for its 
enhancements".

Bit too late for DOD to get involved again - but IF they were still 
involved, and accepting ISO(WG4) 'calls the shots' - DOD involvement 
logically shouldn't put anybody's nose out of joint. ISO gives the 
imprimatur, but anybody wishing to sell to DOD has to fully comply with 
the ISO Standard. Implication of that - we would probably see an 
increase in membership of J4 - so that current vendors not on the list 
have a chance to 'get their oar in'.

Provocatively, and supposing DOD was still involved, they could have 
taken a squint at C, C++ and Java, (or Smalltalk for that matter), and 
stipulated, "Want to sell to us, then your COBOL compiler has to support 
GUIs etc.". Perhaps even those folks in Bellingham Wa.,  might have 
signed up as a J4 member ! (On the other hand, DOD might have suggested 
keep COBOL 'pure and sweet' and provide inter-language communication for 
OO and GUIs).

Agree about Screen Section - very handy in its day but much, much, much 
too late.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO and "mainframes"

_(reply depth: 59)_

- **From:** charles.stevens@unisys.com (Chuck Stevens)
- **Date:** 2004-09-23T12:56:40-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c60a0b82.0409231156.3b841a0e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3n_2d.445482$M95.294865@pd7tw1no> <cimrn9$242b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <abH3d.1740357$y4.302541@news.easynews.com> <cinh16$2iks$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <8x14d.65660$%S.32685@pd7tw2no> <ciqdkn$1g08$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Cl74d.487588$gE.52800@pd7tw3no> <cis8ha$2od0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Zvk4d.1287884$ic1.131004@news.easynews.com> <cisls2$fs$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <8dm4d.477229$M95.45769@pd7tw1no>`

```
James Gavan wrote:

> Bit too late for DOD to get involved again - but IF they were still
> involved, and accepting ISO(WG4) 'calls the shots' - DOD involvement
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> have a chance to 'get their oar in'.

Actually, I wouldn't care whether it's DOD, GSA, DIN or ITSCJ that  made
such a requirement -- the point right now is that there's no requirement.

There's a chicken-and-egg problem here, too, and that has to do with the
state of the validation suite:  I know for certain that no full 2002
validation suite has been developed, and so far as I know the validation
suite that *does* exist for the '85 standard doesn't even include provisions
for the Intrinsic Function amendment of 1989, much less the Corrections
Amendment of 1994.

The imposition of a requirement that vendors validate their compilers
against a suite of programs has meaning only if such a suite of programs
exists before the requirement is in force, and that suite does not currently
exist (at least for the new features in 2002 COBOL).  Barring a volunteer
effort on the part of the COBOL community to provide the revisions to the
current '85 validation suite, it pretty much falls to any agency demanding
compliance to undergo the expense of ensuring that a means of validation was
available, and that obligation forms a significant disincentive to making
the requirement in the first place.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO and "mainframes"

_(reply depth: 60)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-23T20:49:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1FG4d.1359237$ic1.137797@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3n_2d.445482$M95.294865@pd7tw1no> <cimrn9$242b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <abH3d.1740357$y4.302541@news.easynews.com> <cinh16$2iks$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <8x14d.65660$%S.32685@pd7tw2no> <ciqdkn$1g08$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Cl74d.487588$gE.52800@pd7tw3no> <cis8ha$2od0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Zvk4d.1287884$ic1.131004@news.easynews.com> <cisls2$fs$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <8dm4d.477229$M95.45769@pd7tw1no> <c60a0b82.0409231156.3b841a0e@posting.google.com>`

```
Chuck,
  The final NIST test suite *did* include the intrinsic functions - but not the 
corrections amendment. (It was while FIPS marked "Intrinsic Function Module" as 
required - while ANSI called it "optional".

There were even "fixes" done to it along the way (particularly for some 
functions where the original test suite expected "exact" results - while the 
Amendment wasn't clear.  NumVal or NumVal-C - as I recalled caused HP some 
problems - as they did their evaluation in floating-point leading to some ODD 
but "conforming" results)
```

```
Chuck Stevens wrote:

>James Gavan wrote:
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I used DOD purely as an example, but I think the correct one is GSA ( is 
that a 'Supplies' division in Washington DC ?).

>There's a chicken-and-egg problem here, too, and that has to do with the
>state of the validation suite:  I know for certain that no full 2002
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Bureaucracy, yet again ! You have to have the ultimate before you can go 
forward. On the assumption (????) that *most* compilers are at least 
COBOL 85 compliant, (assuming each vendor has a COBOL 85) - isn't that a 
reasonable starting point. I have no info on the existing test suite you 
refer to - but taking that as the starting point,  GSA (?) stipulates 
they will be writing 'additionals' that test the following new features 
in COBOL 2002, not a 100% validation, but a sampling. Your compiler 
fails, you are SOL, they wont buy your product  - but allow for an 
appeal process.

Channel surfing last night and you have to be a masochist to watch the 
channel - it's called CPAC - covers government meetings up here, (not 
parliamentary sessions). Back shortly after dear Jean Chretien nearly 
lost it to Quebec separatists, the government introduced a Sponsorship 
Program to persuade Quebeccers that 'Canada is just great !'. It became 
a slush fund for Liberal handouts to their buddies in Quebec, (can't 
remember sum, but we are talking $millions) - picked up by the Auditor 
General - handouts were made without *any* paper work trail in some 
instances.. Investigations have been on-going  for some six months or 
more. The latest is a judicial enquiry chaired by a judge. 
The*suspected* villain (certainly not yet proven) is a Chuck Guitte - a 
civil servant who *supposedly* dished out the largesse. So last night I 
saw this eminence grise of an Anglo lawyer quizzing civil servants. The 
thrust of his argument was the promotion of Monsieur Guitte. The lawyer 
suggested that the process was speeded up, to go from one executive 
level to another, by getting around 'the red tape'. Oh no ! Our smooth 
civil servant department heads didn't like that one at all, replying 
"Avoiding the red tape, implies breaking the rules".They were so 
convincing even the judge was inclined to agree with them, (on a point 
of law, if you like).

As you probably have already seen - and ignoring portability - I already 
use the M/F feature, 'ignore the red tape' and can have a method :-

Method-id. "Pushbutton-ByName".
set PB-ByName to true
invoke self "createCollection"
End Method "Pushbutton-ByName".

Am I breaking the rules ? And even if I am , so...... ?

Most likely Monsieur Guitte will be dead from over indulgence in Arizona sunshine before they arrive at a conclusion about *anything* ! Similarly, I'll probably be dead before portability has any significance to OO COBOL   :-) 

PS: I'm not a rabid red-neck Westerner criticizing the Liberals. I have been a middle-of-the-road liberal (note small 'L'), since the early 50's.
 
Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO and "mainframes"

- **Date:** 2004-09-24T08:52:35-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cj1fs3$86v$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3n_2d.445482$M95.294865@pd7tw1no> <cimrn9$242b$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <abH3d.1740357$y4.302541@news.easynews.com> <cinh16$2iks$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <8x14d.65660$%S.32685@pd7tw2no> <ciqdkn$1g08$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Cl74d.487588$gE.52800@pd7tw3no> <cis8ha$2od0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <Zvk4d.1287884$ic1.131004@news.easynews.com> <cisls2$fs$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <8dm4d.477229$M95.45769@pd7tw1no> <c60a0b82.0409231156.3b841a0e@posting.google.com> <bsI4d.485463$M95.464306@pd7tw1no>`

```

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:bsI4d.485463$M95.464306@pd7tw1no...

> Bureaucracy, yet again ! You have to have the ultimate before you can go
> forward. On the assumption (????) that *most* compilers are at least
> COBOL 85 compliant, (assuming each vendor has a COBOL 85) - isn't that a
> reasonable starting point.

No, I don't think it is.  A compiler that validates as "compliant" today
with Version X.2.14.97 of the test suite will not necessarily validate as
"compliant" tomorrow with Version X.2.14.97a as the test suite gets refined.
Compliance to a given standard has to be a yes-no proposition applied
equally to all comers over the entire life of the standard, I think.

> I  have no info on the existing test suite you
> refer to -

I beliefe EDS is the current "keeper" of this test suite.

> but taking that as the starting point,  GSA (?) stipulates
> they will be writing 'additionals' that test the following new features
> in COBOL 2002, not a 100% validation, but a sampling. Your compiler
> fails, you are SOL, they wont buy your product  - but allow for an
> appeal process.

It's a moving target.  Your compiler may pass today, you gear up to fill the
order, and GSA changes the criteria out from under you.  Now you not only
lose the order you risk getting branded publically as "non-compliant".

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO and "mainframes"

_(reply depth: 56)_

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2004-09-23T19:43:56-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
"L�ko Willms" <lw-nospam@t-online.de> wrote in message
news:yhrxbjvyyzfgbayvarqr.i48tcm5.pminews@news.t-online.de...
> On Sat, 18 Sep 2004 03:46:43 GMT, James J. Gavan wrote:
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Univac OS/1100. It is on release level 2R1.  ...

Yes, I have noted that, and I have mentioned it in this forum before.  It is
not completely conformant with the 2002 standard (it reflects an earlier
draft). It has not, so far as I know, been met with overwhelming enthusiasm
by the user community.  It seems to me the market response to OO COBOL on
the OS2200 systems, and the paucity of market demand for the mechanism on
the MCP systems, form the main reasons I haven't heard much enthusiasm for
providing such a product on the latter platform.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

- **In-Reply-To:** `<cimrgk$2410$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <10kfaieo0lolh13@corp.supernews.com> <joegk05mipj3jee12evgs016daukv6u7hm@4ax.com> <ci9kvo0gb7@news1.newsguy.com> <3k82d.10805$P_3.2148@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cifctp0ge4@news4.newsguy.com> <cifg46$47q$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cifh4f$6dj$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <ekkmk09j75gauo5t6o6uvsql6pr6b1piu2@4ax.com> <ciflm0$9iq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <kt1nk0l2s6m8riekph7vt1qgoctm7o57mv@4ax.com> <DcO2d.439106$M95.88468@pd7tw1no> <yhrxbjvyyzfgbayvarqr.i48tcm5.pminews@news.t-online.de> <cimrgk$2410$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
Chuck Stevens wrote:
> "Lï¿½ko Willms" <lw-nospam@t-online.de> wrote in message
> news:yhrxbjvyyzfgbayvarqr.i48tcm5.pminews@news.t-online.de...
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> providing such a product on the latter platform.

Our biggest problem is that our configuration management system has been 
scripted (with ECL and whatnot) to automate a mainframe compile from a 
web page.  The PC-based preprocessor hasn't shown enough benefit yet for 
  us to try to script that, then send the intermediate results up to the 
mainframe to let UCOB and LINK finish them off.  It would take a big 
change in policy to let it work.  That's why I'd LOVE for Unisys to port 
some OO COBOL to the mainframe compiler, even if it's still a 
preprocessor for UCOB.  As long as it's on the mainframe, we can put it 
in our ECL.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OO-COBOL for Unisys IX (was: If you were inventing CoBOL...)

_(reply depth: 50)_

- **From:** "L���ko Willms" <lw-nospam@t-online.de>
- **Date:** 2004-09-21T05:27:49+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yhrxbjvyyzfgbayvarqr.i4dwyd0.pminews@news.t-online.de>`
- **References:** `<ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <10kfaieo0lolh13@corp.supernews.com> <joegk05mipj3jee12evgs016daukv6u7hm@4ax.com> <ci9kvo0gb7@news1.newsguy.com> <3k82d.10805$P_3.2148@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cifctp0ge4@news4.newsguy.com> <cifg46$47q$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cifh4f$6dj$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <ekkmk09j75gauo5t6o6uvsql6pr6b1piu2@4ax.com> <ciflm0$9iq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <kt1nk0l2s6m8riekph7vt1qgoctm7o57mv@4ax.com> <DcO2d.439106$M95.88468@pd7tw1no> <yhrxbjvyyzfgbayvarqr.i48tcm5.pminews@news.t-online.de> <cimrgk$2410$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <sqI3d.11775$vl.11324@fe40.usenetserver.com>`

```
On Mon, 20 Sep 2004 17:00:54 -0500, LX-i wrote:

>>> Unisys
>>> offers a OO-COBOL for their other mainframe line, the "Clearpath IX" aka
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>mainframe to let UCOB and LINK finish them off.  

   This is a misunderstanding. OO-COBOL is not a preprocessor for UCOB. At
least according to the manuals, you could use OO-COBOL _instead_ of UCOB, and
providing all the extensions like Embedded SQL, DMS access, and IBM Tape
processing. 

    The PC produces intermediate code which is sent to the host and is
processed there by @OCOB, with an O, not by @UCOB. @OCOB produces an Omnibus
which could be directly @XQTed or @LINKed. 

    The transfer of this intermediate code from the NT-workstation to the
host is done automatically by the workstation program. 

> It would take a big 
>change in policy to let it work.  That's why I'd LOVE for Unisys to port 
>some OO COBOL to the mainframe compiler, even if it's still a 
>preprocessor for UCOB.  

   Probably the compiler licenced from Hitachi is to much tied into WinNT
that it can easily be ported to OS/1100. 

   Also, I think that a workstation based editor can be more productive --
OO-COBOL comes with a COBOL editor with syntax highlighting, and I doubt that
this can be done as effectively with @IPF. See Vol. 4 of the OO-COBOL manuals
(publication number 7851 5855-001). 

Yours, 
L. Willms
------------------------------ all rights reserved ------------------------
```

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>On Sat, 18 Sep 2004 03:46:43 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
>wrote:
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>
No you are one up on me with the above reference, Robert. I only knew, 
some years back,  via Bill K. that Karl Kistler was the 'main architect' 
for F/J-S compilers. Fairly recently saw reference to FACTORY in another 
Newsgroup. Asked them about it. Huh ? "COBOL has OO !".  They politely 
declined to answer - didn't understand what I was getting at. Karl 
stepped in from out of the blue, to re-phrase my question - still didn't 
understand and declined to answer again. Karl over there in Germany 
keeps a watching brief to see if they mention anything of interest on 
OO. F/J went gobbling up everything in sight computer-wise in UK. 
Meanwhile Siemens did it small-scale in Germany, one acquisition being 
Nixdorf. Then subsequently the F/J-S corporate partnership.

>>Without question OO in COBOL would give it a shot in the arm to compete 
>>right up against C++, C# and Java. It might just happen some day - but 
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
As indicated in the article - OO was a success. It mentions three names 
I know :-

- Frank Coyle co-author with Ed Arranga of my first OO book.

- Michael Turk (another ex-Limey) from an Australian University - he 
tried his damnedest - but his university has given up on COBOL. A buddy 
of Will Price, he was a great proponent  of OO COBOL

- Will Price - along with Ed Arranga the organizers of the COBOL Expo, 
and of course author of my second favourite OO book. But I can tell you 
Will is greatly disappointed, (I could perhaps rephrase that to 
'bitterly disappointed'),  at the lack of enthusiasm there has been for 
OO COBOL.  There were a  fair number of academics in the States using 
his book to teach OO. No numbers available, but it's a fair chance many 
of those institutions were ones that have dropped COBOL.

OO COBOL is still an uphill struggle - you know, I know, and others know 
that it works. You want to find out about disbelievers - just watch some 
reactions from this group if you make one of your provocative 
statements, like 'OO kicks the shit out of Procedural'. (There are 
people using current Fujitsu - who wouldn't touch OO with a bargepole).

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:6bdok05mmv20cokoddnq4rjq4dlvdovrsr@4ax.com...

> This reportage on Cobol Expo 2002 shows customer demand:
>
> "The biggest draw at Cobol Expo 2002! was the object-oriented related
> sessions, as determined by eyeballing attendance in each room.
> Anything OO was packed."

I don't doubt the reportage, but I certainly wouldn't form the same
conclusion from it.

If attendance at these sessions included a high percentage of CEO's, CFO's
and CIO's of major corporations, maybe.  Those are the folks who write the
big checks.  I don't see the people who want to know the latest whizbang
feature of a computer language as the *customers* for a major feature that
requires a significant financial committment from large companies.

    -Chuck Stevens.
```

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote 

> I don't mind using the arithmetic verbs, but I *always* do so with GIVING,
> which eliminates any ambiguity, as a matter of style.

Certainly for MULTIPLY and DIVIDE it doesn't make a lot of sense to
try writing

   MULTIPLY Width BY Length 

without GIVING Area.

However, I don't think it necessary to have GIVING when incrementing a
counter or adding a value to a running total.
```

```
Michael Wojcik wrote:
> In article <3k82d.10805$P_3.2148@fe40.usenetserver.com>, LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net> writes:
> 
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> receiving item.

This just supports the supposition that although COBOL is English-based, 
it is it's own language.  :)

I don't know if my experience translates to many other folks, but I was 
a whiz with Commodore 64 BASIC.  I had been exposed to IBM BASIC at 
computer camp, and I knew that most of the stuff worked in both 
environments.  When I first started learning COBOL, "MOVE" seemed like 
the most awkward thing in the world.  "You mean, I can't just say 'a = 
b'?  I have to say 'move b to a'?  That's not only longer, it's backwards!"

Needless to say, I figured it out quickly, and have used that knowledge 
(along with other things) to support my family now for almost 7 years.

> Nothing like a little Monday-morning language design, eh?  Even on a
> Friday afternoon.

How's "Friday evening reminiscing"?  ;)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 45)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-18T17:58:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6unok0pafa4msh5hsvisir388ue7tcu527@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <8zK2d.10885$P_3.10715@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cift12$faf$1@panix5.panix.com> <n88nk0d24sc83hmhc5bug60073fg99h8rk@4ax.com> <cihfif$50t$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
On 18 Sep 2004 10:09:19 -0400, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <n88nk0d24sc83hmhc5bug60073fg99h8rk@4ax.com>,
>Robert Wagner  <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote:
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>holds true today.

Good point. No other programming language can match Cobol's longevity.
But new development has almost dried up. The work I see is conversion
and maintenance, which often comes with a beeper schedule (I've been
able to avoid that). Occasionally I get to rewrite from scratch a
program written in a language that was hot ten years ago. But that
effort isn't classified as Development, it's called Conversion. The
program spec is one sentence: Make it do what the old program did.

New development uses whatever the Language Of The Month Club sent when
decisions were made. I worked on projects that used Cognos Reporting,
Foundation (FCP), SQL Plus, ABAP, SQL:Report, etc. Who maintains this
stuff in years hence? In some cases, months were lost waiting for an
obscure skillset. During those months, we could have written it in
Cobol .. twice. 

Face it, in the minds of management, language du jour is sexy,
fashionable and progressive. Cobol is like a utilitarian truck they
park in the back lot.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 46)_

- **From:** Warren Simmons <wsimmons5@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2004-09-18T20:41:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<414C9CEC.9040001@optonline.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<6unok0pafa4msh5hsvisir388ue7tcu527@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <8zK2d.10885$P_3.10715@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cift12$faf$1@panix5.panix.com> <n88nk0d24sc83hmhc5bug60073fg99h8rk@4ax.com> <cihfif$50t$1@panix5.panix.com> <6unok0pafa4msh5hsvisir388ue7tcu527@4ax.com>`

```
Your point about the way upper management accepts the new stuff, is well
taken, but.... who is trying to make sure they know the stuff the
people here know? When the original CODASYL group retired and or died,
there was not passing of the task of keeping the world aware and 
supporting COBOL. That is except for those representing their employers
who must see to it that any extension is not that earth shaking. The
kinds of stories I read in this news group need to be posted where the
big guys read about it. Seems to me, as I try to put the burden on all
of us, that you guys/and gals should be doing something to organize and
inform. Helping to identify those new features that are needed and 
supporting them as extensions to COBOL, is a very good thing to do with
your time and energy instead of bickering over some little thing. I can
see why Pete and others feel it's a lost cause. Can't do that as the
International Standards group would not approve, ... talking about 
things without producing distributed papers, and participating in the
whole world is not that much help.

One thing I will grant all of you. It takes the backing of some large
users, and even the DOD is not that helpful anymore. Perhaps a letter
to the candates for CIC would stimulate some review and rebirth.

The popular response I get when I complain about something is "well
stop that, or get off your ass, and do something about it." I had
higher hopes for the thread about what "you" would do if you were
inventing COBOL, today. The responses seem to be, lets wait for someone
else to do it or until the moss is three feet thick on the North side
of the tree.

Warren Simmons


Robert Wagner wrote:
> On 18 Sep 2004 10:09:19 -0400, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> 
…[48 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 47)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-19T14:33:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4f5rk0pj6pikgul61pg09qvfo6eq33fmih@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <8zK2d.10885$P_3.10715@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cift12$faf$1@panix5.panix.com> <n88nk0d24sc83hmhc5bug60073fg99h8rk@4ax.com> <cihfif$50t$1@panix5.panix.com> <6unok0pafa4msh5hsvisir388ue7tcu527@4ax.com> <414C9CEC.9040001@optonline.net>`

```
On Sat, 18 Sep 2004 20:41:21 GMT, Warren Simmons
<wsimmons5@optonline.net> wrote:

>Your point about the way upper management accepts the new stuff, is well
>taken, but.... who is trying to make sure they know the stuff the
>people here know? 

Nobody. They don't know it, nor think knowing it is relevant.

>The kinds of stories I read in this news group need to be posted where the
>big guys read about it. 

Airline magazines!

>The popular response I get when I complain about something is "well
>stop that, or get off your ass and do something about it." I had
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>of the tree.

People's actions are largely motivated by opportunities and incentives
.. cost/benefit. Managers don't know Cobol because their success in
climbing the ladder isn't determined by technical knowledge; it's
based on politics and *appearing* to be progressive .. in the eyes of
people who know even less. In other words, their boss's approval
matters; the opinions of foot soldiers don't.

What are our opportunities and incentives? We could maintain Web sites
like CobolReport.com. That's a lot of work for little benefit. We
could write white papers and send them to J4 .. also a lot of work for
tenuous benefit. We could try to set an example in the trenches, at
risk of being told we violated shop standards based on '70s
technology. We could post serious writing here (e.g. my Best
Practices, some of Peter's articles), then watch threads devolve to
bickering rather than synergy. 

My contribution has been managing programming shops in which good
Cobol techniques were taught, encouraged and rewarded. We produced
several million lines of quality code. If there had been thousands of
shops doing the same, the face of Cobol would look different today. In
the end, the fate of a programming language is determined by its
*users* (or lack thereof), not determined by a standards committee nor
even by management. 

How to develop an enthuiastic user base?  With hands-on experience.
Wide usage is promoted by free/inexpensive compilers and a few stellar
programs to demonstrate what's possible. Cobol lacks both. There were
some luminous programs written in the '80s -- Peoplesoft and Realia
come to mind -- but they weren't hot enough to start a fire. Now,
Peter is trying to re-light the torch.  I think his approach is the
best one individual can take. A few knock-your-socks-off tools will do
more to rejuvenate Cobol than a hundred white papers.

Now, if we just had an open source compiler that included OO ..

>One thing I will grant all of you. It takes the backing of some large
>users, and even the DOD is not that helpful anymore. 

Large organizations no longer lead the computer industry; they've
become followers. Look at Linux. The success of Java came from its
user base, not from Sun, corporate management nor DOD. Granted, IBM is
*now* helping it along, but they didn't get on board until it was
established.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 46)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2004-09-18T18:57:43-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ciieh7$dfv$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <n88nk0d24sc83hmhc5bug60073fg99h8rk@4ax.com> <cihfif$50t$1@panix5.panix.com> <6unok0pafa4msh5hsvisir388ue7tcu527@4ax.com>`

```
In article <6unok0pafa4msh5hsvisir388ue7tcu527@4ax.com>,
Robert Wagner  <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote:
>On 18 Sep 2004 10:09:19 -0400, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>able to avoid that).

I always express my willingness to carry a beeper is exactly equal to the 
client's willingness to comply with the provisions of the 1936 Fair Wages 
and Standards Act, in that when an hourly-wage employee either reports for 
a regularly-scheduled shift or is called in to work then said employee is 
to receive no less than four hours' wages... this was instated in order to 
prevent abuses like 'well, we only have two hours' worth of work for you 
today so do the job and go home.'

When I am told that the 1936 Fair Wages and Standards Act does not apply 
to me due to the fact that I make more than six time minimum wage I 
respond that just as my client would hope that I give them a bit more than 
is the legally-required minimum so I, too, expect reciprocally that my 
client will deal with me in a manner that is above the legal minimum... 
works both ways, don'cha know.

After making it clear that I am deadly serious about this... I've never 
been asked to carry a beeper and *not* been able to apply a four-hour 
charge for each call.

>Occasionally I get to rewrite from scratch a
>program written in a language that was hot ten years ago. But that
>effort isn't classified as Development, it's called Conversion. The
>program spec is one sentence: Make it do what the old program did.

What it is called does not matter to me... what the billing rate is does.

[snip]

>Face it, in the minds of management, language du jour is sexy,
>fashionable and progressive.

I can barely speak for my own mind, let alone the minds of others.

DD
```

```
On 19 Sep 2004 18:25:31 -0400, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <uv5rk0demj4hfk6i92ubkj2t2b0nl61vbt@4ax.com>,
>Robert Wagner  <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>doing that for a living, as well.

Good answer. I feel the same, although running my own shops was a lot
more fun than playing by dumb rules. 

>>Why not find your own clients and cut the pimp's share to 10%?
>
>Because my attempts to do so have demonstrated to me that I do not have 
>those skills... I do what I do and make my nickel at it, the pimp does 
>what the pimp does, and likewise.

On my last project, I worked with people who were good at it. I
learned it's about image,  how you present yourself. Decision makers
want someone who makes them FEEL good.

The difference is huge -- $50/hr vs. $100/hr = $100K in the bank at
the end of a year.
```

```
That may be, Mr Wagner... but there has also been at work - at least

with which I have shown to have a bit of difficulty.

'This problem you've had for the past half-decade or so, the one that sunk 
your predecessor's career and his predecessor's as well... this problem 
that you've spent half your time and budget coding around?  I got this guy 
who can take care of it, clean it right out in three months, no problem!'

I have trouble doing things like this with a straight face.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 47)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-19T21:46:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <8zK2d.10885$P_3.10715@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cift12$faf$1@panix5.panix.com> <n88nk0d24sc83hmhc5bug60073fg99h8rk@4ax.com> <cihfif$50t$1@panix5.panix.com> <6unok0pafa4msh5hsvisir388ue7tcu527@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-3FC777.12383119092004@knology.usenetserver.com>`

```
On Sun, 19 Sep 2004 12:38:31 -0400, Joe Zitzelberger
<joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:

>In article <6unok0pafa4msh5hsvisir388ue7tcu527@4ax.com>,
> Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Fortran and assembler both predate Cobol.

Both are now dead languages for business and general-purpose
programming. A search of dice.com for "fortran programmer" found 12
hits. In only one, Fortran appeared to be the primary development
language. The ad emphasized Recent Experience, which suggests it's
hard to find.

Assembler is still spoken in the land of Embedded .. so named because
inhabitants have the letters EE embedded in their pocket protectors.
They are so deficient in tools that they use logic probes as
debuggers.

>The Pascal family, with direct roots in Algol, pretty much equidates 
>Cobol.

True.

>The C family of languages is only a few short years younger than Cobol.
>
>If it were a difference between 5 and 10 years, you might have a point.  
>But a difference between 35 and 40 years is much smaller...

Cobol went live in 1960; C and Unix appeared in 1972 .. difference of
12 years.

If you want to talk about ancestors, Flow-Matic was working in 1955
compared to BCPL in 1967 .. again 12 years.
```

```
In article <dssrk0doh55g2qem53gqko8oqm50s0aiia@4ax.com>, Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> writes:

> <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:

> programming.

Fortran was never particularly popular for business programming; I'm
not sure what would constitute "general-purpose programming".  (Surely
it's not "programming for general-purpose computers", where Fortran
remains popular.)  Fortran is still widely used in the scientific
programming community.

We have commercial products written in assembler, which customers are
still buying, and which are still actively maintained and enhanced.  I
doubt we're the only ones.

optimizing a commercial product written in IA32 assembler.  See?
That's another one right there.  Pretty active for a "dead" language.

> Assembler is still spoken in the land of Embedded .. so named because
> inhabitants have the letters EE embedded in their pocket protectors.
> They are so deficient in tools that they use logic probes as
> debuggers.

This is a particularly stunning display of ignorance, and mighty
insulting to boot.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rlh0l0l9vhqju5am50ta5a1rb41tih35g3@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <8zK2d.10885$P_3.10715@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cift12$faf$1@panix5.panix.com> <n88nk0d24sc83hmhc5bug60073fg99h8rk@4ax.com> <cihfif$50t$1@panix5.panix.com> <6unok0pafa4msh5hsvisir388ue7tcu527@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-3FC777.12383119092004@knology.usenetserver.com> <dssrk0doh55g2qem53gqko8oqm50s0aiia@4ax.com> <cipd3t01e5f@news4.newsguy.com>`

```
On 21 Sep 2004 14:16:29 GMT, mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
wrote:

>In article <dssrk0doh55g2qem53gqko8oqm50s0aiia@4ax.com>, Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> writes:
>> On Sun, 19 Sep 2004 12:38:31 -0400, Joe Zitzelberger
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>Fortran was never particularly popular for business programming;

I worked on a business system written in Fortran in the early '60s.  A
function to edit numbers like zzz,zzz.99- took 60 lines of code. They
wouldn't let me call Cobol for .. umm religious reasons.

> I'm not sure what would constitute "general-purpose programming".  (Surely
>it's not "programming for general-purpose computers", where Fortran
>remains popular.) 

By general-purpose I meant shrink-wrap and the like.

> Fortran is still widely used in the scientific programming community.

Help wanted ads show very little demand. Some mention it as secondary
to C, which suggests they once used it but have since switched to C. 

>We have commercial products written in assembler, which customers are
>still buying, and which are still actively maintained and enhanced.  I
>doubt we're the only ones.

Twelve years ago I worked for a software company said to be the last
in the US working primarily in assembler. There was no way of knowing,
so that may not have been true. When I asked why, the answer was Time
To Market.

Assembly is still used in games but only where they need speed, for
example 3D rotations. 

In a well-known BIOS I saw, everything added in the last ten years was
in C.

>> Assembler is still spoken in the land of Embedded .. so named because
>> inhabitants have the letters EE embedded in their pocket protectors.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>insulting to boot.

I used to be an embedded programmer. When I tried to get back in a few
years ago, questions asked were primarily about hardware. One
interviewer set a motherboard on the table and asked me to identify
every package on it. At another place, they handed me a logic probe
and asked me to demonstrate its use. It became apparent they wanted
EEs, not programmers. In case someone didn't Get It, the job title was
usually Embedded Engineer rather than Embedded Programmer, and the
education requirement was usually 'BSEE or equivalent'.
```

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

<Fortran no longer used based on hiring lists snippage>

> On 21 Sep 2004 14:16:29 GMT, mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
> 
>>Fortran is still widely used in the scientific programming community.
> 

Very few PHD math types *hire* people to code their calculations.  That 
does not mean than it is not being used extensively at all.

Fortran is still used extensively for research. I know several people 
that not only use it, but have several grad students working away at it. 
I doubt that one of them ever even considered running an ad for a 
computer programmer. They run ad's for advanced 
chemists/physicists/mathmaticians, and expect them to be able to learn.

It's a lot easier to teach a research-level chemist to code Fortran that 
it is to hire a code hacker and get them to the reasearch level some 
specialized field.

Donald
```

```
In article <joe_zitzelberger-3FC777.12383119092004@knology.usenetserver.com>, Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> writes:
> In article <6unok0pafa4msh5hsvisir388ue7tcu527@4ax.com>,

> 
> > Good point. No other programming language can match Cobol's longevity.
> Fortran and assembler both predate Cobol.

Lisp, too.  (Prolepsis: Lisp is still being used to develop production
systems.)

> The C family of languages is only a few short years younger than Cobol.

Well, I'd call it 10-18 years, depending on what event you consider the
"beginning" of C.  (K&R1 came out in 1978; obviously C was around before
then, but all the references I found just say that Ritchie developed it
"in the 1970s".  B and BCPL were earlier, of course, but they're pretty
firmly obsolete and substantially different from C.)
```

```
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 49)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2004-09-21T19:08:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cipu8601vb1@news4.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <8zK2d.10885$P_3.10715@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cift12$faf$1@panix5.panix.com> <n88nk0d24sc83hmhc5bug60073fg99h8rk@4ax.com> <cihfif$50t$1@panix5.panix.com> <6unok0pafa4msh5hsvisir388ue7tcu527@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-3FC777.12383119092004@knology.usenetserver.com> <cipco602jmn@news3.newsguy.com> <7kh0l0p9qqtl78scdnd72kn91im99iuhig@4ax.com>`

```

In article <7kh0l0p9qqtl78scdnd72kn91im99iuhig@4ax.com>, Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> writes:
> On 21 Sep 2004 14:10:14 GMT, mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
> wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> The first C compiler was available in 1972 (also the first Unix).

In 1972 C was still pretty far from the C we know today.  It didn't
support structures, for example.  Ritchie writes "By early 1973, the
essentials of modern C were complete",[1] but what really counts as
the origin of C is a matter of opinion.  (Ritchie also notes some
significant features, such as casts, were introduced in 1977.)  I
agree that 1972 is more appropriate than 1978, though.

> That's 12 years from the first Cobol compiler.

Agreed, and that's really the point - I'd call it more than a "few"
years.


1. http://cm.bell-labs.com/cm/cs/who/dmr/chist.html
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 49)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-09-21T19:53:27-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0409211853.506bf5b8@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <8zK2d.10885$P_3.10715@fe40.usenetserver.com> <cift12$faf$1@panix5.panix.com> <n88nk0d24sc83hmhc5bug60073fg99h8rk@4ax.com> <cihfif$50t$1@panix5.panix.com> <6unok0pafa4msh5hsvisir388ue7tcu527@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-3FC777.12383119092004@knology.usenetserver.com> <cipco602jmn@news3.newsguy.com> <7kh0l0p9qqtl78scdnd72kn91im99iuhig@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

> The first C compiler was available in 1972 (also the first Unix).
> That's 12 years from the first Cobol compiler.
> 
> BCPL dates to 1967; Flow-Matic to 1955 .. also 12 years.

BCPL is 'Basic CPL', where Basic is used in the sense of fundemental.
CPL dated from 1963 (ref: Barron) and stands for Combined Programming
Language which was an approach to merge the features of APL (Atlas
Programming Language), ACL (Atlas Commercial Language) plus other
ideas.  In fact just as Cobol merged ideas from several predecessors.
```

- **References:** `<d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <cipco602jmn@news3.newsguy.com> <7kh0l0p9qqtl78scdnd72kn91im99iuhig@4ax.com> <9HPwxnZuflB@jpberlin-l.willms.jpberlin.de> <6io5l09jbpaont30l63o5oasg41rr07fk6@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

> It was available globally to everyone working for Bell Labs and
> running a PDP-7. The first commercial C compiler was Whitesmiths in
> 1978.

According to Ritchie and Kernighan writing in the 'Bell System
Technical Journal':

"""C was originally written for the PDP-11 under Unix, but the
language is not tied to any particular hardware or operating system. C
compilers run on a wide variety of machines, including the Honeywell
6000, the IBM System/370, and the Interdata 8/32."""

Note that Unix at the time was written in assembler and was recoded to
C for edition 6.

Also note that the very first machine to run what became Unix was a
PDP-9, not a -7.  Not that there is much difference.
```

```
going after a job in another language.  He said that the reason for this 
was that COBOL programmers seemed to comment more, and organize their 
code in a more coherent fashion than someone who has never used a 
procedural language.

Again, that may not go any wider than that one job, but one's really all 
you need (at a time).
```

```
there.  what got missed was:

In article <j2O2d.10940$P_3.365@fe40.usenetserver.com>,
LX-i  <lxi0007@netscape.net> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>> In article <8zK2d.10885$P_3.10715@fe40.usenetserver.com>,
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>going after a job in another language.

Pfah... it is my experience that most potential employers who have to run 
their paperwork through an HR office wind up demanding to see 
four-to-eight years' worth of experience in two-year-old technologies.  
This someone who told you this didn't happen to be a COBOL jockey, no?

>He said that the reason for this 
>was that COBOL programmers seemed to comment more, and organize their 
>code in a more coherent fashion than someone who has never used a 
>procedural language.

Again, pfah... it reminds me of an article I read in the Wall Street 
Journal about how businesses were tired of narrow-visioned specialists and 
were looking for more 'generalists' with broad backgrounds.  I sent the 
Journal a letter saying they were welcome to come to my apartment and 
photograph the bathroom wall, which contained a goodly number of 'ding 
notes' from some of the largest companies in the world... and they might 
then go to the HR offices and ask how it was that a person with hands-on 
experience in industry, the military, a liberal-arts degree and moderate 
capabilities in Ancient Greek and calculus was insufficiently 'general'.

>
>Again, that may not go any wider than that one job, but one's really all 
>you need (at a time).

Again, what they say they want and what they wind up hiring - never mind 
at what salary - just might possibly not be the same thing.

DD
```

```
>Again, what they say they want and what they wind up hiring - never mind 
>at what salary - just might possibly not be the same thing.

Companies don't speak with a single voice. The WSJ reported some CEO's
words. People hiring programmers have a different outlook.

When dealing with individuals who seem to contradict themselves, the
best course is watch what they do and disregard their words. Actions
reveal their true feelings; words can be wishful thinking.
```

```
wrote:

>> On Tue, 14 Sep 2004 22:32:25 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>"multiply".

>> occasionally introduce the object being affected.

>effect.

be affected. 

>> Consider: He came by airplane,
>

>"came" here has no object (as we'd expect, since it's intransitive).

>definitely is not an object of the verb "did".)

>result by 5", I expect the listener to consider the quantity named by
>"result" to now be the product.

It has the same structure as the airplane sentence, except transitive.
You're right. Very good.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 40)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2004-09-20T16:14:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cimvl501lsk@news4.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <c5mdk0l0c7g8g2f2uea3qrsf16ub6pfbte@4ax.com> <ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <10kfaieo0lolh13@corp.supernews.com> <joegk05mipj3jee12evgs016daukv6u7hm@4ax.com> <ci9kvo0gb7@news1.newsguy.com> <vp1jk09k4hptq8lrchanub6mntmm6vsfc8@4ax.com>`

```

In article <vp1jk09k4hptq8lrchanub6mntmm6vsfc8@4ax.com>, Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> writes:
> On 15 Sep 2004 14:52:40 GMT, mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
> wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> be affected. 

Yes, B will be affected, because the adverbial phrase modifies the
verb, so it qualifies the action being performed; but the preposition
"by" does not state what action will be performed.

The grammatical object of the verb is often not the only thing that's
affected by the action.  In English (and in many other languages),
verbs are often qualified by other parts of speech (particularly
adverbs and adverbial phrases, but also by eg dependent clauses acting
adverbially and so forth), which may specify other entities that
semantically are affected by the action, even though syntactically
they're not the object of the verb.

> >> He did right by his children.
> >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> How about 'He gave money to his children'?

"money" is direct object; "to his children" is a prepositional phrase
acting as indirect object (that is, the "recipient" of the "whole
action" - verb plus direct object).  Some grammarians reserve the
term "indirect object" for cases where the indirect object isn't a
prepositional phrase:

   I wrote Robert a letter

(here, "a letter" is the direct object - the thing I wrote - and
"Robert" is an indirect object).  They would say that "to his
children" is an adverbial prepositional phrase.  Just a difference of
terminology, really; I like to call parts of the sentence by their
syntactic function, not based on silly rules about what part of
speech a given word usually is.

But part-of-speech makes more sense in a language where grammar is
more explicit - where words are inflected or particles are used or
something along those lines.  We can say that in a Japanese sentence
"oishii" is an adjective, because Japanese adjectives have a certain
form so they can be inflected ("oishii" becomes "oishiku", etc).  In
a language like English, where syntatic function is heavily dependent
on context, it's often unclear.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>

- **References:** `<vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <c5mdk0l0c7g8g2f2uea3qrsf16ub6pfbte@4ax.com> <ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <10kfaieo0lolh13@corp.supernews.com> <joegk05mipj3jee12evgs016daukv6u7hm@4ax.com> <ci9kvo0gb7@news1.newsguy.com> <vp1jk09k4hptq8lrchanub6mntmm6vsfc8@4ax.com> <cimvl501lsk@news4.newsguy.com>`

```
The one "English" phrase in CoBOL that I have seen cause confusion to
non-programmers is the MOVE verb.    In real life when something is moved from
here to there, it is no longer here.     I wonder why they didn't use the word
COPY.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

- **From:** Warren Simmons <wsimmons5@optonline.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<414F15F5.4020707@optonline.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<cin1tp$aup$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <c5mdk0l0c7g8g2f2uea3qrsf16ub6pfbte@4ax.com> <ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <10kfaieo0lolh13@corp.supernews.com> <joegk05mipj3jee12evgs016daukv6u7hm@4ax.com> <ci9kvo0gb7@news1.newsguy.com> <vp1jk09k4hptq8lrchanub6mntmm6vsfc8@4ax.com> <cimvl501lsk@news4.newsguy.com> <cin1tp$aup$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> The one "English" phrase in CoBOL that I have seen cause confusion to
> non-programmers is the MOVE verb.    In real life when something is moved from
> here to there, it is no longer here.     I wonder why they didn't use the word
> COPY.

I think the reason may have been because in either caae, it would take a
clear of the source field to delete the source fieldj, and in the case
of Move, there are possibilities that you want to address the sending
field again to copy somewhere else, but not at the same time.

Just a guess. That was a long time ago.

Warren
```

```
On 20-Sep-2004, Warren Simmons <wsimmons5@optonline.net> wrote:

> > The one "English" phrase in CoBOL that I have seen cause confusion to
> > non-programmers is the MOVE verb.    In real life when something is moved
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Just a guess. That was a long time ago.

So they're copying Assembly - not emulating English.   In English, "move" is
unambiguous - when something is moved, it is no longer at its old location.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 42)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-20T19:13:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<m08uk057lvf6m77tt94ctue6tlp92b6jjr@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <c5mdk0l0c7g8g2f2uea3qrsf16ub6pfbte@4ax.com> <ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <10kfaieo0lolh13@corp.supernews.com> <joegk05mipj3jee12evgs016daukv6u7hm@4ax.com> <ci9kvo0gb7@news1.newsguy.com> <vp1jk09k4hptq8lrchanub6mntmm6vsfc8@4ax.com> <cimvl501lsk@news4.newsguy.com> <cin1tp$aup$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
On Mon, 20 Sep 2004 16:53:13 GMT, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
wrote:

>The one "English" phrase in CoBOL that I have seen cause confusion to
>non-programmers is the MOVE verb.    In real life when something is moved from
>here to there, it is no longer here.     I wonder why they didn't use the word
>COPY.

Cobol got MOVE from assembly language, where it is used the same way.

In ferrite core memory, the machine read a bit by driving it to zero
and detecting whether it changed. Thus, the read phase of a move
really did destroy the original. That's why engineers called it MOVE
when they designed assembly languages. 

Destroying the original was seen as a bad idea, so MOVE became read
followed by two writes. The second wrote data back to the sending
location.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 38)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-15T09:58:36-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ci9sbs$2das$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <ci468f$naj$1@panix5.panix.com> <vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <c5mdk0l0c7g8g2f2uea3qrsf16ub6pfbte@4ax.com> <ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <10kfaieo0lolh13@corp.supernews.com> <joegk05mipj3jee12evgs016daukv6u7hm@4ax.com>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:joegk05mipj3jee12evgs016daukv6u7hm@4ax.com...

> >B is the object of Multiply. A is the object of by.
>
> No, B is the object of by. It's correct English because By can
> occasionally introduce the object being affected.

It's correct English, but not for the reason you state.

> Consider: He came by airplane, He did right by his children.

In these cases I strongly believe that "to come by" and "to do right by"
function grammatically as *verbs* in English.   That "by" is a separate -- 
and even separable -- word is simply another reflection of the rules of
English.  The verb *in COBOL* is MULTIPLY, not MULTIPLY BY with a "separable
suffix".

> But Cobol is internally
> inconsistent. DIVIDE A BY B stores the result into A whereas MULTIPLY
> A BY B stores the result into B. That IS an error.

No, DIVIDE A BY B is a syntax error, according to the standards.  There are
five DIVIDE formats:  into, into-giving, by-giving, into-remainder, and
by-remainder.  In both the by-giving and by-remainder cases, GIVING is
required.   There may be vendors that support it, but I think it'd be a bad
idea for the very reason you suggest.   It would be DIVIDE A BY B that would
be inconsistent with COBOL statement formation rules, not MULTIPLY A BY B.

> Memory aid: if you avoid MULTIPLY ..BY, the four arithmetic verbs
> store the result into the rightmost word.

So does MULTIPLY ... BY.  What are your grounds for avoiding it again?

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 39)_

- **From:** mwojcik@newsguy.com (Michael Wojcik)
- **Date:** 2004-09-15T22:07:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ciaef60ju0@news3.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <c5mdk0l0c7g8g2f2uea3qrsf16ub6pfbte@4ax.com> <ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <10kfaieo0lolh13@corp.supernews.com> <joegk05mipj3jee12evgs016daukv6u7hm@4ax.com> <ci9sbs$2das$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```

In article <ci9sbs$2das$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>, "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> writes:
> "Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
> news:joegk05mipj3jee12evgs016daukv6u7hm@4ax.com...
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> English.

While it's certainly true that English has verb forms that consist of
multiple words, and while English grammar is not a science, and there
are many cases where the grammatical function of a word or other unit
is ambiguous, I think in this case the better analysis is simply to
recognize "by airplane" and "by his children" as adjectival
prepositional phrases.

Consider that "to do right by" is not generally used in isolation ("He
did right by."), and while "to come by" is ("He came by."), it's
plausibly just an elision of "to come by here".

Already, we're treating "to do right" as a verb, while some would
have it verb ("to do") and direct object ("right").  What does he do?
Right.  In what manner?  By his children, ie the rightness he does is
in relation to them.  That is the more precise analysis; but since "to
do right" is an idiom, we can grant that it functions in the whole as
a verb.

If we allow the compound verb "to do right" to swallow up the "by" as
well, then it's not clear what the grammatical function of "his
children" can be.  By the conventions of English syntax, it appears
that it must be a direct object.  But "to do right [by]" is
intransitive, so it can't take a direct object.

The situation is equally bad with "to come by", in the sense that
Robert used it.  Of course "to come by" is also an idiom for "to
receive" - "he came by money" - but this is obviously not the same
usage as in "he came by airplane".  ("He came by an airplane" is
nicely ambiguous.)  Again, "to come [by]" is intransitive, and
moreover "airplane", all by itself there at the end of the sentence,
is not a well-formed direct object in standard English, which wants
an article or other indexical part of speech to introduce a discrete
noun used as a direct object (eg "he flew airplane" should instead be
"he flew the airplane" or similar).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 38)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-09-15T11:52:40-07:00

- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0409151052.6c3a4cfe@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <c5mdk0l0c7g8g2f2uea3qrsf16ub6pfbte@4ax.com> <ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <10kfaieo0lolh13@corp.supernews.com> <joegk05mipj3jee12evgs016daukv6u7hm@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

> But Cobol is internally inconsistent.

> DIVIDE A BY B stores the result into A 

It never does that.  Without a 'giving' the compiler will flag this as
an error.
There is no inconsistency.

(I may have to repeat this over the next two weeks until you test it
and find it is what your compiler does  ;-).

> whereas MULTIPLY
> A BY B stores the result into B. That IS an error.

No. It isn't an error at all.  It is exactly what is defined for the
Cobol syntax.

> Memory aid: if you avoid MULTIPLY ..BY, the four arithmetic verbs
> store the result into the rightmost word. 

Memory aid for Robert: it was DIVIDE .. BY that you wrongly claimed as
the exception.
```

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0409151736.c869e43@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <c5mdk0l0c7g8g2f2uea3qrsf16ub6pfbte@4ax.com> <ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <10kfaieo0lolh13@corp.supernews.com> <joegk05mipj3jee12evgs016daukv6u7hm@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote

> DIVIDE A BY B stores the result into A 

No it does not.  You are quite wrong, this is just more of your
misinformation. Looking at the only reference to DIVIDE BY that does
not require the GIVING, that of TOPS-20 Cobol '68 (where it may be an
extension):

  Option 2

       DIVIDE {identifier-2} BY identifier-1 ....
              {literal-2}

It quite clearly states:

  "In option 2, the resulting quotient replaces the value of
identifier-1."
```

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>

- **References:** `<d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <ci468f$naj$1@panix5.panix.com> <vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <c5mdk0l0c7g8g2f2uea3qrsf16ub6pfbte@4ax.com> <ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <10kfaieo0lolh13@corp.supernews.com> <1gggk0d8jqa2gn9gn7nt8abinc71kj61ok@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:1gggk0d8jqa2gn9gn7nt8abinc71kj61ok@4ax.com...
> Correction, I meant to say:
>
> If you avoid DIVIDE .. BY, the four arithmetic verbs store their
> result into the rightmost word.

No, once again, standard COBOL does not allow DIVIDE ... BY, it allows BY
only in DIVIDE statements of the form DIVIDE ... BY ... GIVING and DIVIDE
... BY ... GIVING ... REMAINDER.  If your implementation allows DIVIDE...BY
without GIVING, that is an extension that indeed violates the basic
precedent followed by the other arithmetic verbs, and I would argue against
both its use and its very existence on those grounds.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
On Wed, 15 Sep 2004 10:02:06 -0700, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>both its use and its very existence on those grounds.

Apparently DIVIDE BY without GIVING was allowed by at least two
compilers before '68. We don't know whether the form was in CODASYL or
an extension. 

Richard and Realia convinced me the result was stored in the rightmost
word, the denominator. That follows the righty-tighty rule but is
backwards from DIVIDE INTO, which alters the numerator.
```

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>

- **References:** `<vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <c5mdk0l0c7g8g2f2uea3qrsf16ub6pfbte@4ax.com> <ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <10kfaieo0lolh13@corp.supernews.com> <1gggk0d8jqa2gn9gn7nt8abinc71kj61ok@4ax.com> <ci9sie$2dfb$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <fquik0hlpj2vq17ptv674smmdcio7ptse5@4ax.com>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:fquik0hlpj2vq17ptv674smmdcio7ptse5@4ax.com...

> Apparently DIVIDE BY without GIVING was allowed by at least two
> compilers before '68. We don't know whether the form was in CODASYL or
> an extension.

The evidence I have before me leads me to believe it was an extension.

> Richard and Realia convinced me the result was stored in the rightmost
> word, the denominator. That follows the righty-tighty rule but is
> backwards from DIVIDE INTO, which alters the numerator.

One of the many peculiarities of DIVIDE ... BY without GIVING that explains
its absence from any "de jure" standard for the language, and from *any*
standard for the language developed within the last thirty-six years.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 36)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-15T02:41:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<xZN1d.789930$ic1.80992@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com> <10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com> <d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <ci468f$naj$1@panix5.panix.com> <vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <c5mdk0l0c7g8g2f2uea3qrsf16ub6pfbte@4ax.com> <ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote in message 
news:10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com...
>
> "Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Rick,
   Isn't that invalid COBOL syntax?  (I think that when you use the BY phrase 
you must also use the GIVING phrase)

So if you had

   Divide A by B Giving C.

that seems syntactically comparable to (standard) English

 Cut the Pie by a knife giving individual pieces
```

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote

>    Isn't that invalid COBOL syntax?  (I think that when you use the BY phrase 
> you must also use the GIVING phrase)

For the DIVIDE, yes.
 
> So if you had
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>  Cut the Pie by a knife giving individual pieces

It is ambiguous whether 'by a knife' is a qaulifier of 'cut' or of
'pie'.

This illustrates why Cobol follows its own set of rules and not those
of some other language.
```

```
wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>B is the object of Divide. A is the object of by.

A is the object of Divide because A is the thing being divided. The
result goes into A.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 37)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2004-09-15T08:10:30-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10kgcc3bs5nuv24@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com> <d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <ci468f$naj$1@panix5.panix.com> <vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <c5mdk0l0c7g8g2f2uea3qrsf16ub6pfbte@4ax.com> <ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <dr4gk0t7cs6utfac6lc8nalv6v1bour21i@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:dr4gk0t7cs6utfac6lc8nalv6v1bour21i@4ax.com...
> On Tue, 14 Sep 2004 21:34:53 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
> wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> result goes into A.

Actually, Mr Wagner, I erred by using divide as an example.

Interestingly, when presented as an instruction to a person,
the result may be left on a calculator or written to the right of
the statement or on a scratch pad. To say that the result goes
into A, is to apply behavior not indicated.

Since the COBOL statements for DIVIDE BY always
include GIVING, that ambiguity is eliminated.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 38)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-15T16:51:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<k5sgk01dr0sklr5vdmvt5j8srh1pat7alf@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<ci468f$naj$1@panix5.panix.com> <vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <c5mdk0l0c7g8g2f2uea3qrsf16ub6pfbte@4ax.com> <ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <dr4gk0t7cs6utfac6lc8nalv6v1bour21i@4ax.com> <10kgcc3bs5nuv24@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Wed, 15 Sep 2004 08:10:30 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
wrote:

>Interestingly, when presented as an instruction to a person,
>the result may be left on a calculator or written to the right of
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>include GIVING, that ambiguity is eliminated.

GIVING wasn't required in the Good Old Days. '68 and '74 manuals are
online at:

http://www.36bit.org/dec/manual/

Unfortunately the site is very slow, PDFs are not searchable and the
'74 manual is missing pages, including the one with DIVIDE syntax.

The '68 manual clearly shows GIVING was not required on DIVIDE BY. The
'74 seems to say the same, but without a syntax diagram I can't tell
with certainty.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 39)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-15T10:25:35-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ci9tug$2fsa$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<ci468f$naj$1@panix5.panix.com> <vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <c5mdk0l0c7g8g2f2uea3qrsf16ub6pfbte@4ax.com> <ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <dr4gk0t7cs6utfac6lc8nalv6v1bour21i@4ax.com> <10kgcc3bs5nuv24@corp.supernews.com> <k5sgk01dr0sklr5vdmvt5j8srh1pat7alf@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:k5sgk01dr0sklr5vdmvt5j8srh1pat7alf@4ax.com...

> GIVING wasn't required in the Good Old Days. '68 and '74 manuals are
> online at:
>
> http://www.36bit.org/dec/manual/

CODASYL COBOL (a COBOL-60 dialect) for the B6500 required GIVING in DIVIDE
BY.

IBM COBOL for DOS/360 (an ANSI-68 implementation) required GIVING in DIVIDE
BY.

Burroughs B6700 COBOL (another ANSI-68 implementation) required GIVING in
DIVIDE BY.

ANSI X3.23-1974 required GIVING in DIVIDE BY.

ANSI X3.23-1974 makes no mention of GIVING in DIVIDE BY as a substantive
change that might affect existing programs.

"I saw an implementation once that allowed ... " is a far cry from "COBOL
allows".   Again, there may well have been implementations that allowed
DIVIDE ... BY without GIVING, but if there were, it was *clearly* an
extension to standard COBOL, and for the very reasons you've cited, it was
one that I would have argued against had I had the opportunity.  The
benefits provided by this extension pale in comparison to the pitfalls.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
