###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 61)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-22T10:30:50-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ciscsa$2rj8$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<cicg80$16gn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9g5nk0184nfu5rp4v30bnim8tjfe166qjp@4ax.com> <YKO2d.439433$M95.194954@pd7tw1no> <o0dok05f6pk2t8cccit8jblg7hgmo3dqud@4ax.com> <cihg8b$5oc$1@panix5.panix.com> <p5kok05csh17o8qg5p4ombe50rrcc1erji@4ax.com> <ZW%2d.43563$%S.39122@pd7tw2no> <cin039$a09$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <N_%3d.484875$gE.434491@pd7tw3no> <wi04d.13781$pA.932477@news20.bellglobal.com> <rN24d.1816964$y4.314700@news.easynews.com> <rjg1l0lfug8qpt7gh1safepurkvnvqel2j@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:rjg1l0lfug8qpt7gh1safepurkvnvqel2j@4ax.com...

> Continuation should be obsolete.

I think it's probably moving that way, given the availability of the
concatenation operator in 2002 COBOL.  Personally, though, I think limiting
the size of a literal produced by the concatenation operator to 160
characters, as the 2002 standard does, missed the boat.

> In 40+ years, I've NEVER used
> continuation lines.

I have.  I think concatenation is an improvement, but I also think the
160-character limit on literals causes some coding challenges.

> There were ways around it before and since we got
> free form, it's clearly obsolete.

Mostly those "ways around it" involved declaring data items.

Here's one that involves both continuation and substitution of data spaces:
    INSPECT identifer-1 CONVERTING literal-4 TO literal-5
where literal-4 and literal-5 each represents the characters in an 8-bit
character set.

If it were a 7-bit character set in '74 or '85 COBOL you'd still have to use
continuation to build the literals (you could use the concatenation operator
in 2002 COBOL).  But the maximum size of a literal is still 160 characters
in 2002 COBOL, so representing the full 256-character set in a single
literal is prohibited.

This particular construct is one of the reasons for the proposal to increase
the maximum size of an alphanumeric or national literal in the 2008 draft.

If the implementor was able to make proper use of the "structured constant"
mechanism also proposed for the 2008 draft, it's possible that
    INSPECT identifier-1 CONVERTING structured-constant-1 TO
structured-constant-2
could be done with no more overhead than the literal-to-literal case, but
unless that's the case if you're going to avoid continuation there's almost
certainly going to be two instantiations of each of the character sets
somewhere in the program memory!

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Long literals (was: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 62)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-22T22:10:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<oLm4d.1886960$y4.325077@news.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<cicg80$16gn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9g5nk0184nfu5rp4v30bnim8tjfe166qjp@4ax.com> <YKO2d.439433$M95.194954@pd7tw1no> <o0dok05f6pk2t8cccit8jblg7hgmo3dqud@4ax.com> <cihg8b$5oc$1@panix5.panix.com> <p5kok05csh17o8qg5p4ombe50rrcc1erji@4ax.com> <ZW%2d.43563$%S.39122@pd7tw2no> <cin039$a09$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <N_%3d.484875$gE.434491@pd7tw3no> <wi04d.13781$pA.932477@news20.bellglobal.com> <rN24d.1816964$y4.314700@news.easynews.com> <rjg1l0lfug8qpt7gh1safepurkvnvqel2j@4ax.com> <ciscsa$2rj8$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
A slight "clarification" on what Chuck writes below.

There are two separate enhancements in the 2002 Standard that relate to "long 
literals"

1) Concatenation operand - uses "&" operator
2) "new-style" quoted literal concatenation, e.g.
      "ABC"-
      "XYZ"
            or
      B"01"-
        "11"
              or
       NX"12AB"-
            "78EF"

The difference between these two features (both of which are available in both 
free and fixed form reference format) is important to understand *IF* one uses 
COPY REPLACING or REPLACE statements.

   "ABC" & "XYZ"    *> is 3 text words
        while
   "ABC"-
  "XYZ"   *> is a single text word

***

I certainly would support moving the "old style" quoted literal concatenation to 
archaic or obsolete (if done in the 2008 Standard, OBSOLETE would mean that it 
would be removed from the 201x Standard <G>).  However, I also think that this 
is a VERY commonly used feature (especially by code generators and "alien code 
translators" like the IBM mainframe CICS translator's handling of EXEC CICS 
statements.   Therefore, I wouldn't (personally) expect to see it removed from 
many compilers in the next decade or so.
```

```
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>160-character limit on literals causes some coding challenges.

Why should there be a limit? Is the machine out of memory?

>> There were ways around it before and since we got
>> free form, it's clearly obsolete.
>
>Mostly those "ways around it" involved declaring data items.

Why is it remarkable to put DATA items in the DATA division?

I recently wrote a program that sent letters via email, from text in a
table. Under old style (8-72) I would have been required to break each
line into two, accurately counting the number of bytes in the first
fragment. Using free form, each line had a pic x(100) and the source
program values read like the letter. That format greatly simplified
maintenance.

>This particular construct is one of the reasons for the proposal to increase
>the maximum size of an alphanumeric or national literal in the 2008 draft.

How about maximum one meg?

>If the implementor was able to make proper use of the "structured constant"
>mechanism also proposed for the 2008 draft, it's possible that
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>somewhere in the program memory!

If values are constants, there's no need to store either character set
in memory.They should be converted to a translation table at compile
time.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 63)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-23T08:08:53-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ciuou5$1ec3$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<9g5nk0184nfu5rp4v30bnim8tjfe166qjp@4ax.com> <YKO2d.439433$M95.194954@pd7tw1no> <o0dok05f6pk2t8cccit8jblg7hgmo3dqud@4ax.com> <cihg8b$5oc$1@panix5.panix.com> <p5kok05csh17o8qg5p4ombe50rrcc1erji@4ax.com> <ZW%2d.43563$%S.39122@pd7tw2no> <cin039$a09$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <N_%3d.484875$gE.434491@pd7tw3no> <wi04d.13781$pA.932477@news20.bellglobal.com> <rN24d.1816964$y4.314700@news.easynews.com> <rjg1l0lfug8qpt7gh1safepurkvnvqel2j@4ax.com> <ciscsa$2rj8$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <tpr4l01jfv44mg7udjat7n9q3t7v58lehr@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:tpr4l01jfv44mg7udjat7n9q3t7v58lehr@4ax.com...
> Why should there be a limit? Is the machine out of memory?

Primarily because it's impossible to detect syntax errors without one.

> Why is it remarkable to put DATA items in the DATA division?

It's not, but it's likely you'll end up with two instantiations of the value
somewhere in read-write memory if you have to do a data item to contain the
value.

> I recently wrote a program that sent letters via email, from text in a
> table. Under old style (8-72) I would have been required to break each
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> maintenance.

I don't doubt that one bit.  Has not much to do with continued literals,
though.

With '02-style free-form reference format it *is* possible to put a whole
160-byte literal on a single source line without continuation because the
format allows a maximum of 255 characters per source line.   My bigger
problem isn't with continued literals, it's with the 160-character limit.

> How about maximum one meg?

The consequences of a malformed literal are too great.  How often are *you*
going to encounter a situation in which a one-megabyte literal is the best
way to resolve it?

> If values are constants, there's no need to store either character set
> in memory.They should be converted to a translation table at compile
> time.

If the compiler is able to ascertain that a *data item* is indeed a
constant, never modified in any way (and in monolithic-working-storage
environments, with or without bounds checking on references to other
items!), that's fine, but with all the bells and whistles introduced since
'74 that becomes increasingly difficult.  A "constant data item" is subject
to the same limitations as literals, so they can't be used for this purpose.
I've already mentioned the "structured constants" proposal for the '08
draft, and handling the conversion into a translate table at compile time
could certainly be a feasible optimization.  But since the ability to
declare (currently) a 8191-byte alphanumeric literal (or constant data item,
or concatenation expression) is being separately proposed for the same
draft, the motives for using structured constants for this particular
purpose are a bit less pressing.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
In article <7mf8l0tb7n1kbkfvp4i4eg85ado96750pk@4ax.com>,
 Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote:

> On Fri, 24 Sep 2004 07:43:47 -0400, Joe Zitzelberger
> <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> no one but the programmer will be able to change it. Brilliant.

Nothing sensitive about a splash screen graphic ... just don't want the 
casual twit playing with it.  

It isn't any fun to have some annoying punk replace "my_app_splash.jpg" 
with "hot_nasty_skanky_teenage_poodle_from_russia.jpg" in your distro.

Inlining "my_app_splash.jpg" as a resource or a literal for use in your 
display function prevents that problem easily.  As this is not the sort 
of thing that is likely to change without a recompile, the feeding a 
literal to REZ/DEREZ makes some sense.

Just a random example where large literals can be nice...
```

```
going to be the best way.
```

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-09-23T12:15:40-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0409231115.3f697d54@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<9g5nk0184nfu5rp4v30bnim8tjfe166qjp@4ax.com> <o0dok05f6pk2t8cccit8jblg7hgmo3dqud@4ax.com> <cihg8b$5oc$1@panix5.panix.com> <p5kok05csh17o8qg5p4ombe50rrcc1erji@4ax.com> <ZW%2d.43563$%S.39122@pd7tw2no> <cin039$a09$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <N_%3d.484875$gE.434491@pd7tw3no> <wi04d.13781$pA.932477@news20.bellglobal.com> <rN24d.1816964$y4.314700@news.easynews.com> <rjg1l0lfug8qpt7gh1safepurkvnvqel2j@4ax.com> <ciscsa$2rj8$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <tpr4l01jfv44mg7udjat7n9q3t7v58lehr@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

> I recently wrote a program that sent letters via email, from text in a
> table. Under old style (8-72) I would have been required to break each
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> maintenance.

I would suggest that having the text of the letter in the program will
be a source of continuing requirement for maintenence.

I have several programs that send EMails, such as announcing that
their order has been despatched or that their data has been received
and processed.  The message texts are all done via templating from a
text (line sequential) file.  This allows the client to change the
message as they require, at any time, without having to bother the
programming staff at all (me).

It also makes it very easy to have alternate texts by the simple
expedient of having different template file names depending on certain
conditions, for example the customer's language, or the method of
despatch.

In fact most of my text output is templated these days: web pages,
EMails, EDI, printed doxuments.  It means that if, for example, they
want an XML acknowedgement instead of an EMail letter, it might be
done by just changing the template text.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 64)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-23T21:43:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ue6l09rqcptsstpemjb2d99266e4mdp86@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<cihg8b$5oc$1@panix5.panix.com> <p5kok05csh17o8qg5p4ombe50rrcc1erji@4ax.com> <ZW%2d.43563$%S.39122@pd7tw2no> <cin039$a09$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <N_%3d.484875$gE.434491@pd7tw3no> <wi04d.13781$pA.932477@news20.bellglobal.com> <rN24d.1816964$y4.314700@news.easynews.com> <rjg1l0lfug8qpt7gh1safepurkvnvqel2j@4ax.com> <ciscsa$2rj8$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <tpr4l01jfv44mg7udjat7n9q3t7v58lehr@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409231115.3f697d54@posting.google.com>`

```
On 23 Sep 2004 12:15:40 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>programming staff at all (me).

I agree. If I had designed the program, I would have done it the same
way. 

This was a large bureaucratic company. If text had been line
sequential, it would have had to go through Version Control. There was
no file type there for Output Template and they wouldn't have created
one because they frowned on flat files. They would have told me it
belongs in a database table. Creating a new table involved lots of
paperwork, required scripting to insure it was promoted through five
levels between Development and Production as well as changes to
backup/restore scripts, and would have raised questions about
maintenance and security  -- How can we insure a prankster doesn't
change the text? What do you mean 'Update it with Toad?' We don't
permit programming tools in Production. You'll have to write an update
screen that runs off a menu, and that's not on our Development
Schedule. What are you, a trouble-maker?

Moreover, the project was porting a system from one platform to
another. We weren't permitted to make design changes except for good
reason. Letters were hard-coded on the old platform (in another
programming language), so they're hard-coded on the new. That's life
in big companies.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 59)_

- **From:** "L���ko Willms" <lw-nospam@t-online.de>
- **Date:** 2004-09-22T10:53:51+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yhrxbjvyyzfgbayvarqr.i4g6pr5.pminews@news.t-online.de>`
- **References:** `<cicg80$16gn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9g5nk0184nfu5rp4v30bnim8tjfe166qjp@4ax.com> <YKO2d.439433$M95.194954@pd7tw1no> <o0dok05f6pk2t8cccit8jblg7hgmo3dqud@4ax.com> <cihg8b$5oc$1@panix5.panix.com> <p5kok05csh17o8qg5p4ombe50rrcc1erji@4ax.com> <ZW%2d.43563$%S.39122@pd7tw2no> <cin039$a09$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <N_%3d.484875$gE.434491@pd7tw3no> <wi04d.13781$pA.932477@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
On Tue, 21 Sep 2004 16:38:31 -0400, Donald Tees wrote:

>As a sidenote, I have always thought that Line Sequential should be 
>defined as whatever format *source code* is stored in.  On the PC and 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>over 25 years out-of-date. They do not use cards anymore do they? <G>.

    No, no cards. 

   I can only relate my Unisys (i.e. Univac) experience. On OS/1100, source
files would be elements in what is called a Program File. The program file
could contain source elements (SRC), RELative elements (i.e. OBJect in other
architectures), ABSolute elements (i.e. executable programs) and OMNibus
elements which could be of any type (the latest compiler generation no longer
produces RELative, but OMNibus elements). The file structure of such a
Program File is being described in some manual. 

    I'm not 100% sure about the format of a SRC element, but I think it
resembled that of the (main) data file format, i.e. that the record length is
variable, and the length is not determined by some special character or
character sequence in the data area (CR, CR/LF, or LF), but by a control
header for that record which contains the line length. Dealing with limits
between the records is not the business of applications, but of the operating
system. The application only gets access to the payload, not the file
administration. This is quite different from OSes derived from Unix or DOS,
for which a file is just a sequence of bytes, and where it is up to the
application to apply some structure to it. 

Yours, 

    
L. Willms
------------------------------ all rights reserved ------------------------
```

- **From:** LX-i <lxi0007@netscape.net>

- **References:** `<cicg80$16gn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9g5nk0184nfu5rp4v30bnim8tjfe166qjp@4ax.com> <YKO2d.439433$M95.194954@pd7tw1no> <o0dok05f6pk2t8cccit8jblg7hgmo3dqud@4ax.com> <cihg8b$5oc$1@panix5.panix.com> <p5kok05csh17o8qg5p4ombe50rrcc1erji@4ax.com> <ZW%2d.43563$%S.39122@pd7tw2no> <cin039$a09$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <N_%3d.484875$gE.434491@pd7tw3no> <wi04d.13781$pA.932477@news20.bellglobal.com> <yhrxbjvyyzfgbayvarqr.i4g6pr5.pminews@news.t-online.de>`

```
Lï¿½ko Willms wrote:
>    I can only relate my Unisys (i.e. Univac) experience. On OS/1100, source
> files would be elements in what is called a Program File. The program file
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Program File is being described in some manual. 

I think "REL" is actually "Relocatable", referring to its ability to be 
put wherever in the I-bank and D-bank of a basic mode ABSolute element. 
  (With the extended-mode ZOOM (Zero-Overhead Object Module), relocation 
is accomplished by the runtime system.)
```

```
.     Am  23.09.04
 schrieb  lxi0007@netscape.net (LX-i)
     bei  /COMP/LANG/COBOL
      in  SXJ4d.25925$vl.19805@fe40.usenetserver.com
   ueber  Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

l> I think "REL" is actually "Relocatable",

   You are right. My memory cheated me.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 59)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2004-09-21T23:25:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pL24d.467483$M95.142010@pd7tw1no>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<ee71l0pkf2qrdf28ksl3hi444h681ebbv7@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<cicg80$16gn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <9g5nk0184nfu5rp4v30bnim8tjfe166qjp@4ax.com> <YKO2d.439433$M95.194954@pd7tw1no> <o0dok05f6pk2t8cccit8jblg7hgmo3dqud@4ax.com> <cihg8b$5oc$1@panix5.panix.com> <p5kok05csh17o8qg5p4ombe50rrcc1erji@4ax.com> <ZW%2d.43563$%S.39122@pd7tw2no> <cin039$a09$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <N_%3d.484875$gE.434491@pd7tw3no> <ee71l0pkf2qrdf28ksl3hi444h681ebbv7@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>On Tue, 21 Sep 2004 20:16:45 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
>wrote:
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>
They wont. But consider the following situation. GUIs are NOT part of 
the J4 Standard, but the PC 'vendors' have seen fit to produce 
GUI/Webbing support in varying degrees, at least two OO, but the 
remainder not.. A tremendous amount of re-inventing the wheel - coupled 
with a big learning curve for developers to appreciate what the myriad 
of methods can do. For each Windows control there are a finite number of 
things you can do with it - until Bill Gates slips in something like 
'transparent' as in dotNet where you can see 'through' the top Dialog.. 
Now if you can come up with a standard set of classes for GUI/Webbing 
and 'common' method-names - the 'internals' of the methods are 
designed/coded by the vendor - so you still need his version of the 
classes to use with his compiler. (And 'Yes' depending upon whether or 
not it is for Windows or Linux then the COBOL class methods invoke 
either Windows or Linux etc.)

Where the competition comes in is the visual design tools which allow 
you to create windows/dialogs and generate the bread-and-butter code, 
events and which class and methods to return the event to. Each would 
take a different tack on this. Very likely you would see, "I wish, I 
wish, we had put this feature in our design tool......". As Bill once 
said, introduction of CBL_FILE routines in F/J was in response to those 
available in M/F.

As for easy switching of compilers - dream on ! How many F/J users are 
going to convert to Net Express, or vice versa. When are you going to 
switch to F/J,  Robert ?

Jimmy
```

```
>As for easy switching of compilers - dream on ! How many F/J users are

fees.
```

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

> >> At least three: Micro Focus, Fujitsu and IBM. And a fourth in Fujitsu
> >> mainframe, which runs Unix as its operating system.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> BS2000 has a POSIX shell that appears to be used a lot.

Fujitsu has had a range of Sun based servers that started as the ICL
DRS6000 many years ago, but these aren't the Fujitsu Mainframes:

"""On the mainframe side of the house the IBM S/390-compatible, 16-
way GS8800 CMOS mainframe Fujitsu Ltd will introduce next year as the
GS8800 performing at more than 2,000 MIPS will be introduced by both
Fujistu Siemens and Fujitsu's US-based Amdahl Corp unit in slightly
different variants. The Fujitsu Siemens variant called BS2000 will run
Siemens' IBM OS/VS1-alike BS2000 to which it has progressively added
MVS features. They are not quite plug- compatible with IBM S/390s.
There are said to be 3,000 BS2000 licensees. Amdahl will offer the
systems as its Millennium 2000E system."""

Robert, you are a veritable mine of misinformation.
```

- **References:** `<cihg8b$5oc$1@panix5.panix.com> <p5kok05csh17o8qg5p4ombe50rrcc1erji@4ax.com> <ZW%2d.43563$%S.39122@pd7tw2no> <cin039$a09$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <N_%3d.484875$gE.434491@pd7tw3no> <ee71l0pkf2qrdf28ksl3hi444h681ebbv7@4ax.com> <pL24d.467483$M95.142010@pd7tw1no> <sue1l05lqdmflhjok4hhpq7d8rjflsiilp@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409212330.17369897@posting.google.com> <rgu2l01kf7jde4t7c6glkk7ep9eeu8al50@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409221125.7058812a@posting.google.com>`

```
On 22 Sep 2004 12:25:41 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 
>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>Robert, you are a veritable mine of misinformation

"Munich 13 March 2002 Fujitsu Siemens Computers (FSC) announced at
CeBIT, Hannover, that it ported its proprietary mainframe operating
system BS2000/OSD from /390 CMOS processors to its own SPARC
processors. The SX130 is the first system of the new SX-series. It can
run BS2000/OSD and Solaris in parallel in different hardware
partitions. This allows a fibre channel connection on a mainframe and
the usage of all the Solaris application software. 

At CeBIT fair in Hannover, Fujitsu Siemens Computers (FSC) announced a
new mainframe/Solaris product line, the SX-series. The first is a
midrange system with 35 to 250 RPF (a FSC internal performance
measure), following FSC, 1.3 to 1.5 RPF equal one MIPS. The S-line,
based on /390 CMOS processors has a range of 156 to 1920 RPF with up
to 14 processors and an additional one as hot stand-by. FSC will
announce the next high-end S-system in Q3/2002. Up to four S-systems
can be connected via HIPLEX load connection. The SR-series uses MIPS
processors, up to four in scalable SMP (symmetrical multiprocessing).
That means BS2000/OSD on MIPS.

The new business server demonstrates the FSC strategy to open
BS2000/OSD for modern hardware technology and Solaris application
software. New features are CoD (Capacity on Demand), OLR (OnLine
Replacement) and a new partitioning with flexible resource management.

Standard Fibre Channel connections allow the optimal and efficient
usage of SAN (Storage Area Network) from the Solaris side. BS2000/OSD
supports ESCON and KT2 (Channel Type 2) connections. 

The partitioning concept allows the usage of only BS2000/OSD or
BS2000/OSD and Solaris in parallel on the SX130. Three hardware
partitions are allowed, for BS2000/OSD the maximum is 2. A partition
contains one or several system boards (CPUs, memory, I/O connections).
With VM2000 (Virtual Machine) several guest systems, instances of
BS2000/OSD can run in parallel.

The SX130 has a performance range of 25 to 250 RPF and can be equipped
with up to up to 6 SPARC64-GP processors, developed by the American
Fujitsu daughter HAL Computer Systems. FSC told in a press meeting
that these processors have a better performance than the Sun
Microsystem's SPARCs. The memory is expandable to up to 8 GByte for
BS2000/OSD. Both operating systems can run in parallel. This allows
the connection of new applications on Solaris access to mainframe
data. 

FSC offers a package, OSD/XC (Open Server Dimension (Extended
Configuration) for the operation in a computer centre and business
critical computing. It contains BS2000/OSD, Web Transactions, Open Net
Server up to security software. Other components are workload
management, automatisation and backup tools. It is pre-integrated and
tested, easy to install and ready to operate.

The next steps will be processors with a higher performance, support
of new and additional hardware models, dynamic reconfiguration,
virtual partitioning, communication of the system domains via
Hipersockets and Enhanced CoD. FSC expects the main sales in Europe."

http://www.hoise.com/primeur/02/articles/monthly/UH-PR-04-02-1.html

The S-line sounds much like the Sun servers I'm accustomed to working
on. They are boxes that serve thousands of users and  run for months
between reboots, delivering performance comparable to  mainframes for
a fraction of the cost. 1,250 MIPS for S-line is in the vicinity of
2,000 MIPS for FS8800. Delivered performance is not a linear function
of MIPS, it's a function of how well the OS exploits parallel
processing. Solaris  is better at multi-threading than zOS.
```

```
> >DRS6000 many years ago, but these aren't the Fujitsu Mainframes:
> >
```

```
On 23 Sep 2004 11:49:27 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

>> The S-line sounds much like the Sun servers I'm accustomed to working
>> on. They are boxes that serve thousands of users and  run for months
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>outperform the Fujitsu mainframes. 

The SunFire line already does. Maximum 72 CPUs are capable of about
3,000 MIPS ... if one could keep them all busy.

> However, you are making an
>assumption that this will be 'at a fraction of the cost'.  Your box
>may be a tiny fraction, but 2,000 MIPS boxes won't be.

A new SunFire E4900 with 36 CPUs and 48G sells for $500,000. By
extrapolation, the max configuration should be $1M. A comparable IBM
z990 T-Rex with 48 CPUs costs at least $5M, when software cost is
included.
```

- **From:** "L���ko Willms" <lw-nospam@t-online.de>
- **Date:** 2004-09-18T10:32:54+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yhrxbjvyyzfgbayvarqr.i48r2u1.pminews@news.t-online.de>`
- **References:** `<10kgcc3bs5nuv24@corp.supernews.com> <k5sgk01dr0sklr5vdmvt5j8srh1pat7alf@4ax.com> <ci9tug$2fsa$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <d8vik09juqiqgm1hoci50idp18gkegopp4@4ax.com> <cicg80$16gn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <lc0kk0pouakiandr6r0hqdnbihlc6tho09@4ax.com> <cid8eh$1mi1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <acfkk0t7mo2qu3b57fdih6vdi6sh524lmj@4ax.com> <cif1p0$2tiq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <o07mk09ncj10u85nmskka8a97328v9pdn9@4ax.com> <cifa8v$1tb$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <v7gmk0tq9q3t0pb1irns6ep8u14oac32rr@4ax.com>`

```
On Fri, 17 Sep 2004 21:00:25 GMT, Robert Wagner wrote:

>
>By what *standard* mechanism do your programs return an exit status to
>the operating system?

   The standard provided a template where the implementor could provide
"Implementor-name-x" in the SPECIAL-NAMES paragraph of the CONFIGURATION
SECTION of the ENVIRONMENT DIVISION. And then the programmer could define a
mnemonic name for it, and condition-names for ON and OFF status. 

   "If implementor-name-1 references an external switch, the associated
mnemonic-name may be specified only in the SET statement", says paragraph (1)
in section 4.5.3 "Syntax Rules" of the COBOL-85 standard. 

   Different operating systems provided and still do provide different
switches to communicate between a run stream (JCL) and a user program. 


Yours, 
L. Willms
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 39)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-09-15T16:36:15-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0409151536.734ef227@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<ci468f$naj$1@panix5.panix.com> <217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <c5mdk0l0c7g8g2f2uea3qrsf16ub6pfbte@4ax.com> <ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <dr4gk0t7cs6utfac6lc8nalv6v1bour21i@4ax.com> <10kgcc3bs5nuv24@corp.supernews.com> <k5sgk01dr0sklr5vdmvt5j8srh1pat7alf@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote

> >Since the COBOL statements for DIVIDE BY always
> >include GIVING, that ambiguity is eliminated.
> 
> GIVING wasn't required in the Good Old Days. '68 and '74 manuals are
> online at:

> The '68 manual clearly shows GIVING was not required on DIVIDE BY. The
> '74 seems to say the same, but without a syntax diagram I can't tell
> with certainty.

I can assure you that in the '74 standard DIVIDE BY required the
GIVING. I have several references that indicate this clearly.  eg MF
CIS Cobol and Level II Cobol.

In "Writing Structured Cobol", David L. Johnson describes ANS'74 and
explains differences from ANS'68. He takes pains to qualify DIVIDE BY
as not permitted without the GIVING and raises no issues that this is
different from ANS'68, yet the book has many marginal notes on
differences.
 
I can distinctly remember that with earlier versions of Cobol (pre
'68) there was no DIVIDE BY at all, there was only DIVIDE INTO, at
least with the compilers that I used, it was a source of some
discussion.  The '68 compiler introduced DIVIDE BY GIVING.

It may be that the TOPS compiler was non-standard in this respect.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 40)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-16T12:33:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vlrik0dlu295chr5vubi680ms0pbmr89t3@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <c5mdk0l0c7g8g2f2uea3qrsf16ub6pfbte@4ax.com> <ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <dr4gk0t7cs6utfac6lc8nalv6v1bour21i@4ax.com> <10kgcc3bs5nuv24@corp.supernews.com> <k5sgk01dr0sklr5vdmvt5j8srh1pat7alf@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409151536.734ef227@posting.google.com>`

```
On 15 Sep 2004 16:36:15 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote
>
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>It may be that the TOPS compiler was non-standard in this respect.

I remember working on a mid-60s compiler, probably IBM, which didn't
require GIVING.  I remember making a mental note to avoid BY because
it was backwards. 

If the result goes to the right operand, it IS backwards. INTO put the
result in the numerator; BY put it in the denominator.

I tried DIVIDE foo BY 3 on an early-80s Realia compiler that was very
compatible with IBM.  It issued a warning about missing GIVING, and a
fatal error about storing the result into a literal. That suggests IBM
once had it, later added the requirement for GIVING.
```

```
Robert,

the IBM COBOL documentation that I have that any (mainframe) IBM COBOL compiler 
ever accepted "BY" without "GIVING" for DIVIDE.
```

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:vlrik0dlu295chr5vubi680ms0pbmr89t3@4ax.com...
> On 15 Sep 2004 16:36:15 -0700, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:
>
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> require GIVING.

So from that infallible and universal observation, we should all draw the
conclusion that "GIVING wasn't required in the good old days"?

> I remember making a mental note to avoid BY because
> it was backwards.

Most of us avoided BY without GIVING because it resulted in some sort of
syntax error.

> If the result goes to the right operand, it IS backwards. INTO put the
> result in the numerator; BY put it in the denominator.

Unless you're writing *standard* COBOL, in which case you wouldn't use BY
without GIVING in the first place.

> I tried DIVIDE foo BY 3 on an early-80s Realia compiler that was very
> compatible with IBM.  It issued a warning about missing GIVING, and a
> fatal error about storing the result into a literal. That suggests IBM
> once had it, later added the requirement for GIVING.

And a person with extensive IBM background has asserted otherwise.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 41)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-09-16T17:13:05-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0409161613.7d1c592e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <c5mdk0l0c7g8g2f2uea3qrsf16ub6pfbte@4ax.com> <ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <dr4gk0t7cs6utfac6lc8nalv6v1bour21i@4ax.com> <10kgcc3bs5nuv24@corp.supernews.com> <k5sgk01dr0sklr5vdmvt5j8srh1pat7alf@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409151536.734ef227@posting.google.com> <vlrik0dlu295chr5vubi680ms0pbmr89t3@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

> I tried DIVIDE foo BY 3 on an early-80s Realia compiler that was very
> compatible with IBM.  It issued a warning about missing GIVING, and a
> fatal error about storing the result into a literal. That suggests IBM
> once had it, later added the requirement for GIVING.

Using one product to attempt to know what a completely different
product may do when something undefined is tried is very poor practice
and may lead to completely invalid conclusions.

It is irrelevant that Realia tries to be compatible with IBM's syntax,
unless the compiler is the _same_ code then the way it determines
errors may be completely different.

A more reasonable conclusion may be that Realia gives a fatal error
about a literal on the right of any maths without a GIVING. In fact
what results do you get for MULTIPLY foo BY 3 ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 43)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-09-17T12:55:14-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0409171155.2c27f5f5@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<c5mdk0l0c7g8g2f2uea3qrsf16ub6pfbte@4ax.com> <ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <10kf742953dpf35@corp.supernews.com> <dr4gk0t7cs6utfac6lc8nalv6v1bour21i@4ax.com> <10kgcc3bs5nuv24@corp.supernews.com> <k5sgk01dr0sklr5vdmvt5j8srh1pat7alf@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409151536.734ef227@posting.google.com> <vlrik0dlu295chr5vubi680ms0pbmr89t3@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409161613.7d1c592e@posting.google.com> <qmflk0pfd7k7oim4qrg89nv9bo41ahp65p@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

> >> I tried DIVIDE foo BY 3 on an early-80s Realia compiler that was very
> >> compatible with IBM.  It issued a warning about missing GIVING, and a
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>   E Data-name '3' invalid as numeric destination

Thank you.  Yes, working from more than one data point now gives a
better picture of what may be happening.  It probably does a specific
check of DIVIDE BY for the GIVING and then has some general check for
receiving field being a literal.

I think that you can eliminate any conclusion about what IBM may or
may not have had.

You have a tendancy to start with a conclusion (in this case 'IBM once
had it') and then only look for evidence that may support this, not
even trying for evidence that dispells the conclusion.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 35)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-15T09:16:50-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ci9pti$2bg0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com> <10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com> <d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <ci468f$naj$1@panix5.panix.com> <vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <c5mdk0l0c7g8g2f2uea3qrsf16ub6pfbte@4ax.com> <ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com>`

```
I can think of lots of cases in which it is possible for procedure division
code to violate English syntax rules.  One that comes to mind is that COBOL
grammar does not require agreement in number between the literals and the
keywords that introduce them:  VALUE and VALUES are both permissible in an
88-level item regardless of how many literals there are.

E, VALUES IS and VALUE ARE are all permissible
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com...

> Yes, they do. (For simplicity, I omitted + Punctuation.) I can't think
> of a case where Cobol's procedure division syntax violates English
> syntax. Can you?

Sure I can.  Scope terminators!

I also think the "direct" and "indirect" objects (for want of a better
"offhand" term) in "MULTIPLY A BY B ROUNDED ..." are in direct conflict with
English syntax.

And if the VALUE clause in the data division is to mimic English syntax, the
fact that there is no requirement in COBOL that the number of literals used
in the list agree with the introductory keyword in number.

> The rule-breaker is INVOKE (or CALL) taking as its
> object a method name written as a non-gerundial verb. Defenders of
> INVOKE can say 'The standard didn't tell you how to name a method.
> Don't blame Cobol because YOU chose the ungrammatical word.' ...

It appears to me that the purpose of making COBOL "simple English or
pseudo-English"  was to make it, insofar as possible, self-documenting.
Whether the syntax violates the rules of *English grammar* is irrelevant so
long as the grammar to which the syntax belongs is unambiguously defined.

That leads to my opinion that the appropriate response to the complaint "But
INVOKE violates the rules of English grammar!" is thus "feh."

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

```
On Wed, 15 Sep 2004 09:16:50 -0700, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>I can think of lots of cases in which it is possible for procedure division
>code to violate English syntax rules.  One that comes to mind is that COBOL
>grammar does not require agreement in number between the literals and the
>keywords that introduce them:  VALUE and VALUES are both permissible in an
>88-level item regardless of how many literals there are.

Ok.

>E, VALUES IS and VALUE ARE are all permissible

I've never written IS or ARE. <shrug>

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
>news:icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com...
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Sure I can.  Scope terminators!

That's punctuation. English doesn't have subscripts either.

>I also think the "direct" and "indirect" objects (for want of a better
>"offhand" term) in "MULTIPLY A BY B ROUNDED ..." are in direct conflict with
>English syntax.

Michael said the same. Now I see you're both right.

Anyway, I prefer COMPUTE over MULTIPLY and DIVIDE. 

>And if the VALUE clause in the data division is to mimic English syntax, the
>fact that there is no requirement in COBOL that the number of literals used
>in the list agree with the introductory keyword in number.

Ok.

>> The rule-breaker is INVOKE (or CALL) taking as its
>> object a method name written as a non-gerundial verb. Defenders of
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>long as the grammar to which the syntax belongs is unambiguously defined.

The rules are easier to remember if they follow English. When in
doubt, people can follow rules they already know rather than looking
up syntax in a book.

>That leads to my opinion that the appropriate response to the complaint "But
>INVOKE violates the rules of English grammar!" is thus "feh."

When given a choice between two forms, one grammatical and the other
not, I'll choose the grammatical one. That's why I prefer Inline
Invocation over INVOKE. That's why I prefer Cobol over other
programming languages .. and am saddened when Cobol repudiates its
origins.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 37)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-16T10:26:52-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cicict$182t$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com> <d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <ci468f$naj$1@panix5.panix.com> <vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <c5mdk0l0c7g8g2f2uea3qrsf16ub6pfbte@4ax.com> <ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <ci9pti$2bg0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <710jk0dgv63vvik950ie8ru1n4vvq7j4b1@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:710jk0dgv63vvik950ie8ru1n4vvq7j4b1@4ax.com...

> The rules are easier to remember if they follow English. When in
> doubt, people can follow rules they already know rather than looking
> up syntax in a book.

When one is in doubt, yes, it's true, when writing COBOL programs one CAN
follow the rules "they already know" from another context instead of
following the rules of COBOL as they're actually stated.  If one is lucky,
one might even get the right answer.  If one is only slightly less lucky,
one might get a syntax error (as in the case of DIVIDE...BY).  If one is
lucky, but only somewhat, one might get an execution-time failure (e.g.
EC-ARGUMENT-FUNCTION).  If one is unlucky, one will corrupt his company's
data.   In all but the first case this is what I would call a *programming
error*.

Assuming that all the rules of COBOL directly reflect the rules of English,
and that therefore what one might do in English one can appropriately do in
COBOL, is a really, REALLY bad idea.

That's basically the reason that abbreviated combined relation conditions
cause so many headaches:  in English (and in some parts of COBOL!) elision
implies an association among succeeding or otherwise associated elements (as
for example your OPEN examples), but NOT in abbreviated combined relation
conditions.

> When given a choice between two forms, one grammatical and the other
> not, I'll choose the grammatical one.

One more time.  What is grammatical *in COBOL* is defined by the rules *for
COBOL*, not the rules for English, or the rules for C#, or the rules for
Sanskrit, or the rules for Basque, or the rules for RPG.

The word order and the structure of COBOL has never borne much more than a
*passing* resemblance to a *very limited* subset of English (and bears a
significant resemblance to similar forms in other languages, Indo-European
and otherwise!), and to insist that COBOL should distinguish between
adverbs, adjectives, participles and suchlike is to place requirements on
the language that never before existed.  The keywords words used in COBOL
*happen to be* English Keywords.  The form of statements in COBOL happens
*largely* to be based loosely on English imperative sentence structure,
which is shared by a number of languages.  I seriously doubt that a native
speaker of German or French would have any problem whatever understanding
the flow of a COBOL program were a preprocessor to substitute the
appropriate German or French imperative verbs for the statement keywords and
other words such as prepositions for clause and phrase identifications.

[An aside:  the choice of German verbs might have to avoid separable
prefixes, thus, schliessen would be preferable to zumachen, because
"schliesse <filename>" is grammatical while "zumache <filename> isn't; one
would have to write "mache <filename> zu".  But aside from such
peculiarities, the input to such a word-substitution preprocessor would be
recognizable to a native speaker.]


> That's why I prefer Cobol over other
> programming languages .. and am saddened when Cobol repudiates its
> origins.

Its origins were at the Pentagon under the sponsorship of the US military,
at a meeting whose purpose was to consider "both the desirability and the
feasibility of establishing a common language for the programming of
electronic computers for business applications."

The use of "simple English or pseudo-English" was a means to an end, not a
purpose in and of itself, and when the needs of COBOL and its functionality
are in conflict with the rules of English grammar the latter are quickly,
and correctly, discarded.

If you think that COBOL should be dragged kicking and screaming back to the
days when it was a good ol' 'Murkin language designed for good ol' 'Murkins,
and the rest of the world could go hang, well, get your employer to sponsor
you as a member of J4, and you will be in a much better position to debate
such issues!

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>Assuming that all the rules of COBOL directly reflect the rules of English,
>and that therefore what one might do in English one can appropriately do in
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>conditions.

In English, elision doesn't cross into or out of parentheses. The
problems with abbreviated combined relation conditions exist in part
because Cobol doesn't follow that rule.
```

_(reply depth: 37)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2004-09-16T12:44:10-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0409161144.671dba27@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com> <ci468f$naj$1@panix5.panix.com> <vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <c5mdk0l0c7g8g2f2uea3qrsf16ub6pfbte@4ax.com> <ci71pu$fli$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <icmek0dhec01s1pjkbbg26o6pk6p7ahmdh@4ax.com> <ci9pti$2bg0$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <710jk0dgv63vvik950ie8ru1n4vvq7j4b1@4ax.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote

> The rules are easier to remember if they follow English. 

I entirely dispute that. English has no pretensions of being rigourous
and unambiguous. eg

  The stripes of a Zebra are black and white.
  The stripes of a Zebra are black or white.  
  The stripes of a Zebra are not red and yellow.  
  The stripes of a Zebra are not red or yellow.  

Can we, from this, work out whether to use AND or OR in an IF and how
NOT should be used ?

The whole basis of having a maths language is that natural languages
cannot express accurately enough what is required.

> When in doubt,
> people can follow rules they already know rather than looking
> up syntax in a book.

You are renowned for 'not looking it up', this leads to much of your
misinformation.

You complained when someone made a mistake that if it had been you it
would have gone on for two weeks.  Yes it would, because you would
have claimed that it was right until you finally did look it up.

For example 'DIVIDE BY' was done and dusted over a month ago, yet you
were still arguing over it, until you finally noticed your error.

see:

http://groups.google.com/groups?hl=en&lr=&ie=UTF-8&c2coff=1&threadm=217e491a.0408142223.70cceb59%40posting.google.com&rnum=2&prev=/groups%3Fhl%3Den%26lr%3D%26ie%3DUTF-8%26c2coff%3D1%26q%3Ddivide%2Brichard%26btnG%3DSearch%26meta%3Dgroup%253Dcomp.lang.cobol

> When in doubt,

I suspect the real problem is that you never doubt anything that you
have in your thoughts, you have such a high regard for your own
thoughts that you think  you do not need to 'look it up'. In some this
may be considered arrogant.
```

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote

> I kind of would like to learn Reverse Polish before going to Reverse Poland on
> vacation.   (Coming from Poland?)

Interestingly, they don't use 'Reverse Polish notation' in Poland,
they use 'Polish Notation', which is the reverse of Reverse Polish
Notation.

However, this does not mean that you have to come back from Poland
before going there.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT People spelling their own name wasRe: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 36)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2004-09-15T16:12:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ci9pll$6bo$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com> <10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com> <d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <ci468f$naj$1@panix5.panix.com> <vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <ci6tua$kd9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0409141137.3e1fedcc@posting.google.com> <ocpek0pi167k6v6b8eus0cvs439ltjki30@4ax.com> <2qr6brF12pmtoU1@uni-berlin.de>`

```

On 15-Sep-2004, "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote:

> > Poland may be the only country where people don't know how to spell
> > their own names. My step-father, his two brothers and their sister
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> regularity of spelling is a relatively recent phenomenon (1800's or later).

Shakespeare spelled his name multiple ways.   Lewis and Clark did not use
standardized spelling.   I wonder when English spelling was standardized, I
suspect it happened during the 19th century when there was a huge cultural
attempt to standardize everything with rules and authority for everything.   
(The AMA is an example)

In the world, I grew up with a western standard of spelling for Chinese names
which has recently been changed.   Interesting that the Chinese authorities
picked a new standard that really doesn't fit our alphabet any better than the
old one.

As a programmer, I expect everything to have one meaning and appreciate
standards - probably too much.   It's hard for me to quite get the idea that
Carlos Kleiber could be a genius symphonic conductor - after all, Beethoven
wrote the music, he's just the operating system with the performers executing
the commands as written!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT People spelling their own name wasRe: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 37)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-15T10:17:09-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ci9tem$2fdq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com> <10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com> <d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <ci468f$naj$1@panix5.panix.com> <vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <ci6tua$kd9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0409141137.3e1fedcc@posting.google.com> <ocpek0pi167k6v6b8eus0cvs439ltjki30@4ax.com> <2qr6brF12pmtoU1@uni-berlin.de> <ci9pll$6bo$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:ci9pll$6bo$1@peabody.colorado.edu...

> In the world, I grew up with a western standard of spelling for Chinese
names
> which has recently been changed.   Interesting that the Chinese
authorities
> picked a new standard that really doesn't fit our alphabet any better than
the
> old one.

Actually, it does.  Pinyin is much more regular and intuitive than
Wade-Giles or competing romanizations of Chinese languages.  The Roman
alphabet is but symbols representing sounds -- it's just that the
distinctions among the symbols aren't the same as what we're used to (e.g.,
the difference between "B" and "P" isn't voice, it's that "P" is plosive and
"B" isn't.).  That's true for most languages that use that alphabet (or, for
that matter, any other set in which multiple languages use the same symbols
for phonemic representation).

I'm reminded of my high school chemistry teacher's introductory statement
"My name is spelled exactly as it's pronounced.  Wojczynski."

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 35)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-15T09:22:23-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ci9q80$2br7$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com> <10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com> <d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <ci468f$naj$1@panix5.panix.com> <vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com> <ci6tua$kd9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0409141137.3e1fedcc@posting.google.com> <ocpek0pi167k6v6b8eus0cvs439ltjki30@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:ocpek0pi167k6v6b8eus0cvs439ltjki30@4ax.com...

> Poland may be the only country where people don't know how to spell
> their own names. My step-father, his two brothers and their sister
> used four different spellings of their common surname.

Poland may be one of a number of places -- regardless of political
boundaries -- in which there may be a number of equally-legitimate ways to
spell your name, even presuming there is only one way to pronouce it!   I
can think of at least one *spoken* language that has two entirely separate
*written* forms using different alphabets.

Some languages have few ambiguities between spelling and pronunciation, some
have many, some have rigorous standards for spelling, some don't give a darn
so long as the phonetics represented by the spelling come close enough to
the phonemics.  I don't think this is a *fault* of the country of Poland,
merely a difference in emphasis and priority.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 32)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-14T08:06:33-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ci71dq$f9j$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com> <10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com> <d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <ci468f$naj$1@panix5.panix.com> <vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com>`

```
> You are wrong. I strongly doubt that anyone claimed that Cobol was
> designed to use  'English grammar' as you appear to claim.  What they
> have said is that, for example: "COBOL was designed ... using an
> English-like command syntax."

One reference manual I have from before the ANSI era has "The language is
designed so that a COBOL program reads much like ordinary English, and can,
therefore, provide much of its own documentation automatically."   A second
reference manual, this for an ANSI '68 implementation, is worded almost the
same -- "... English and, therefore, automatically provides ...".

Another '68 reference states "COBOL ... is a programming language, similar
to English, that is used for commercial data processing."

However, I don't see wording like this in the '74 standard, or in the
documentation available to me for '74-compliant compilers.

It seems clear to me that the reason for the basic form of COBOL
statements -- imperative sentences based on the rules of English -- is to
make the programs *more* self-documenting and *less* obscure than they might
otherwise be.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 32)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-14T08:18:02-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ci723a$fol$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0409082213.5c0cc708@posting.google.com> <n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com> <10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com> <d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <ci468f$naj$1@panix5.panix.com> <vn8bk05fhgd4i5jck6pb7tt69rk7s7ftpo@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131058.78019f64@posting.google.com> <091ck0de5ak2k39ccbp04v710rbqrpve2u@4ax.com> <217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com>`

```

"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0409131928.f88cbcd@posting.google.com...

> In fact Cobol doesn't have a 'grammar' at all, let alone the English
> one, it has a syntax (which is very much a subset of grammar), and not
> that of English but a highly stylised and contrived one of its own.

As I indicated to Robert in another response, I believe COBOL does have a
*grammar* because it includes not only a vocabulary but also a description
of the way additional words are to be formed.  I believe "a study of what is
to be preferred and what avoided in inflection and syntax" applies to COBOL
and to most other programming languages, and thus, "grammar" applies to them
as well.

Note that because the syntax of a language is "highly stylised and
contrived" has no bearing on whether that language has a grammar.  Esperanto
is *entirely* contrived (though it borrows heavily from a number of
Indo-European languages) yet the rules for the language consists of more
than mere syntax.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com...
> On Sun, 12 Sep 2004 17:24:35 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
> wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >contrived imperative language.

Mr Wagner, I misspoke. I wrote 'the weakness in your
argument'. I should have written 'one weakness in your
argument'. I apologize.

> Special pleading is a common defense for illiteracy. People say the
> stodgy old rules don't apply because they've been ... what, liberated
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> we make up.

Mr Wagner, what troubles me is that you wrote 'Terms ...
recall images' rather than, say, 'Terms ... remind us'. I made,
what I see as, a similar error in some of what I wrote. Given
'invoke cake "bake"', I referred to 'a cake' rather than 'this
cake'. The translation becomes 'to cause this cake to bake'
or the command 'bake this cake'.

> To that I say, you can't send up what you don't have down. You can't
> break the rules until you've first mastered them. Anything less is
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> expect a pair of elephants to give issue to a goldfish?

Mr Wagner, 'an object of its own class' is not returned. The
use of 'class', in rule 1 as "class, category, and content", is
unrelated to objects. See FDIS, page 110, 8.5.2 Class and
category of data items and literals.

> Cobol cannot do type conversions between user-defined objects, it can
> only diagnose mismatches. So unless you're returning a string or a
> number, INVOKE has no advantage over Inline Invocation.

Mr Wagner, you have this reversed. If a string or a number
is returned, INVOKE has no advantage.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 28)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-13T22:11:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cr3ck0dr1qo522ucn5me2ppjsh4m1jmovb@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<217e491a.0409092007.46cc04db@posting.google.com> <2514k09nj41epdgrmsgiejth6lh0kpc8n5@4ax.com> <10k4fqr41odggdb@corp.supernews.com> <mt76k0dbt25is9dnsgmqcfqj3nehr293hc@4ax.com> <10k6j81qr3092c2@corp.supernews.com> <6rd8k0l8ejudlnug25mppml9qu3qod2rfq@4ax.com> <10k8miq8v4lq21b@corp.supernews.com> <n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com> <10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com> <d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <10kbhoii5mahhe8@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Mon, 13 Sep 2004 12:10:58 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
>news:d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com...
>> On Sun, 12 Sep 2004 17:24:35 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
>> wrote:

>> Special pleading is a common defense for illiteracy. People say the
>> stodgy old rules don't apply because they've been ... what, liberated
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>recall images' rather than, say, 'Terms ... remind us'.

Recall is defined: b. to bring back to mind <recall those early years>
c : to remind one of. I don't see an error.

>> >> The word invoke is NOT required for syntax. The standard says we can
>> >> use Inline Invocation instead.
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>category of data items and literals.

I stand corrected. It has the class and category of the RETURNING
item, not the object invoked. 

>> Cobol cannot do type conversions between user-defined objects, it can
>> only diagnose mismatches. So unless you're returning a string or a
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>is returned, INVOKE has no advantage.

Suppose the returning item was class & category alphanumeric. INVOKE
could store it into a numeric field according to the rules of MOVE (I
believe, the standard doesn't say), and the following line of code
could add 1 to it. However, it would be invalid to say 'compute
a-numeric = object-reference::'method'(..) + 1'. That would be the
same as 'compute a-numeric = pic x(2) + 1', which is an invalid
arithmetic expression.

Similarly, if the returning item was numeric, INVOKE could store it
into alphanumeric and the next statement could INSPECT it. However, it
could be invalid to say 'inspect object-reference::'method'(..) ..'.

If the returning item were a (strongly typed) object, INVOKE would
have no advantage over Inline Invocation.
```

- **References:** `<217e491a.0409092007.46cc04db@posting.google.com> <2514k09nj41epdgrmsgiejth6lh0kpc8n5@4ax.com> <10k4fqr41odggdb@corp.supernews.com> <mt76k0dbt25is9dnsgmqcfqj3nehr293hc@4ax.com> <10k6j81qr3092c2@corp.supernews.com> <6rd8k0l8ejudlnug25mppml9qu3qod2rfq@4ax.com> <10k8miq8v4lq21b@corp.supernews.com> <n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com> <10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com> <d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <10kbhoii5mahhe8@corp.supernews.com> <cr3ck0dr1qo522ucn5me2ppjsh4m1jmovb@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:cr3ck0dr1qo522ucn5me2ppjsh4m1jmovb@4ax.com...
[snip]
> Similarly, if the returning item was numeric, INVOKE could store it
> into alphanumeric and the next statement could INSPECT it. However, it
> could be invalid to say 'inspect object-reference::'method'(..) ..'.

It would be valid if the returning item was USAGE DISPLAY.

> If the returning item were a (strongly typed) object, INVOKE would
> have no advantage over Inline Invocation.

FDIS, page 114, 8.5.3.2 Strongly-typed group items
"The only kind of items that may be strongly typed are
group items." Perhaps, Mr Wagner, you intended something
other than 'object'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 30)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-14T01:20:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<16hck09rfbgi1ea924cnkaasoc0ornje5g@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<10k4fqr41odggdb@corp.supernews.com> <mt76k0dbt25is9dnsgmqcfqj3nehr293hc@4ax.com> <10k6j81qr3092c2@corp.supernews.com> <6rd8k0l8ejudlnug25mppml9qu3qod2rfq@4ax.com> <10k8miq8v4lq21b@corp.supernews.com> <n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com> <10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com> <d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <10kbhoii5mahhe8@corp.supernews.com> <cr3ck0dr1qo522ucn5me2ppjsh4m1jmovb@4ax.com> <10kcbqdsqmm689a@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Mon, 13 Sep 2004 19:36:46 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
>news:cr3ck0dr1qo522ucn5me2ppjsh4m1jmovb@4ax.com...
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>It would be valid if the returning item was USAGE DISPLAY.

Are you saying this is valid?

01  a-number DISPLAY PIC 99.
INSPECT a-number ...

>> If the returning item were a (strongly typed) object, INVOKE would
>> have no advantage over Inline Invocation.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>other than 'object'.

No. A strongly typed object is represented by an object-pointer to a
group item defined by TYPEDEF STRONG.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 31)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2004-09-13T22:33:06-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<10kcm5r7hipct6b@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<10k4fqr41odggdb@corp.supernews.com> <mt76k0dbt25is9dnsgmqcfqj3nehr293hc@4ax.com> <10k6j81qr3092c2@corp.supernews.com> <6rd8k0l8ejudlnug25mppml9qu3qod2rfq@4ax.com> <10k8miq8v4lq21b@corp.supernews.com> <n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com> <10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com> <d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <10kbhoii5mahhe8@corp.supernews.com> <cr3ck0dr1qo522ucn5me2ppjsh4m1jmovb@4ax.com> <10kcbqdsqmm689a@corp.supernews.com> <16hck09rfbgi1ea924cnkaasoc0ornje5g@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:16hck09rfbgi1ea924cnkaasoc0ornje5g@4ax.com...
> On Mon, 13 Sep 2004 19:36:46 -0400, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
> wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> INSPECT a-number ...

Yes. INSPECT SR1, FDIS, page 463, 14.8.21.2 Syntax rules
"1) Identifier-1 shall reference either an alphanumeric or national
group item or an elementary item described implicitly or explicitly
as usage display or national."

'a-number' is 'an elementary item described ... explicitly as usage
display'. In the case of the returning item from inline method
invocation, temp-identifier is 'an elementary item described
implicitly ... as usage display' by FDIS, page 94, 8.4.2.4.3
General rules, 1b, 'temp-identifier has the same description,
class, and category ...'. 'description' is covered by FDIS, page
251, 13.14 Data description entry. "A data description entry
specifies the characteristics of a particular item of data."
Description includes usage.

See also, FDIS, page102, 8.4.4 Explicit and implicit references.
"A source element may reference data items either explicitly or
implicitly in procedure division statements. An explicit reference
occurs when the name of the referenced item is written in a
procedure division statement. An implicit reference occurs when
the item is referenced by a procedure division statement without
the name of the referenced item being written in the source
statement. ...".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 31)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-14T07:49:49-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ci70ed$enn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<10k4fqr41odggdb@corp.supernews.com> <mt76k0dbt25is9dnsgmqcfqj3nehr293hc@4ax.com> <10k6j81qr3092c2@corp.supernews.com> <6rd8k0l8ejudlnug25mppml9qu3qod2rfq@4ax.com> <10k8miq8v4lq21b@corp.supernews.com> <n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com> <10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com> <d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <10kbhoii5mahhe8@corp.supernews.com> <cr3ck0dr1qo522ucn5me2ppjsh4m1jmovb@4ax.com> <10kcbqdsqmm689a@corp.supernews.com> <16hck09rfbgi1ea924cnkaasoc0ornje5g@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:16hck09rfbgi1ea924cnkaasoc0ornje5g@4ax.com...

> Are you saying this is valid?
>
> 01  a-number DISPLAY PIC 99.
> INSPECT a-number ...

I think the standard does.  ISO/IEC 1989:2002 Page 463, 14.8/21.2, INSPECT
statement, syntax rule 1:   "Identifier-1 shall reference either an
alphanumeric or national group item or an elementary item described
implicitly or explicitly as usage display or national."

You can even replace nonnumeric characters into the digit positions of a
USAGE DISPLAY numeric item using this methodology (e.g., "INSPECT a-number
REPLACING ALL '0' BY 'Q'.").  The standard is clear, however, that the
consequences of doing so -- in particular, what sort of numeric value you
get if you try to retrieve the value of a-number -- are not defined!

I have a little experience with this area; I once had to deal with a
compiler bug in which an INSPECT...REPLACING by an alphanumeric literal
which happened to correspond to the character position containing the sign
in the numeric item didn't do what the standard said it was supposed to do!

    -Chuck Stevens
```

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
- **Date:** 2004-09-14T22:10:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<q6qek09c2sg4rq6hs58kttflth8m4637am@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<10k6j81qr3092c2@corp.supernews.com> <6rd8k0l8ejudlnug25mppml9qu3qod2rfq@4ax.com> <10k8miq8v4lq21b@corp.supernews.com> <n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com> <10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com> <d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <10kbhoii5mahhe8@corp.supernews.com> <cr3ck0dr1qo522ucn5me2ppjsh4m1jmovb@4ax.com> <10kcbqdsqmm689a@corp.supernews.com> <16hck09rfbgi1ea924cnkaasoc0ornje5g@4ax.com> <ci70ed$enn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
On Tue, 14 Sep 2004 07:49:49 -0700, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>I have a little experience with this area; I once had to deal with a
>compiler bug in which an INSPECT...REPLACING by an alphanumeric literal
>which happened to correspond to the character position containing the sign
>in the numeric item didn't do what the standard said it was supposed to do!

The standard says the sign should be stripped and then restored. For
example:

01  a-number pic s99.

move -11 to a-number
inspect a-number replacing all '1' by '2'

The result should be -22 (2K on IBM).

Who dreams up these labyrinthine rules? Common sense says it should
not be allowed, which is why I thought it was (not). Conversely, if
allowed for usage display with implicit type conversion to unsigned
string, it should be allowed for all numerics.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 33)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2004-09-15T13:33:51-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cia8vf$2nbq$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<10k6j81qr3092c2@corp.supernews.com> <6rd8k0l8ejudlnug25mppml9qu3qod2rfq@4ax.com> <10k8miq8v4lq21b@corp.supernews.com> <n5r8k0p2m26ohkb1oesv4rnie271fv4d68@4ax.com> <10k9fn4jhl98880@corp.supernews.com> <d22bk0to6d3b5psrk5oabfrv9f8mv0pmsd@4ax.com> <10kbhoii5mahhe8@corp.supernews.com> <cr3ck0dr1qo522ucn5me2ppjsh4m1jmovb@4ax.com> <10kcbqdsqmm689a@corp.supernews.com> <16hck09rfbgi1ea924cnkaasoc0ornje5g@4ax.com> <ci70ed$enn$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <q6qek09c2sg4rq6hs58kttflth8m4637am@4ax.com>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote in message
news:q6qek09c2sg4rq6hs58kttflth8m4637am@4ax.com...

> The standard says the sign should be stripped and then restored. ...

Actually, that depends on *which* standard you're talking about.  True for
ANSI X3.23-1985 (page VI-96, 6.18.4, INSPECT statement, general rule 2c last
sentence) and for ISO/IEC 1989:2002 (page 464, 14.8.21.3, INSPECT statement
general rule 3d), but I don't see similar wording in ANSI X3.23-1974 (page
II-69, 5.144, INSPECT statement, general rule 2c or anywhere else).

Note that the INSPECT statement was introduced in the '74 standard; although
the specification that the sign is retained for a signed item seems to have
been added in the '85 standard I don't see anything in the "substantive
change" list (of either the '85 standard itself or of the corrections
amendment) that indicates it's a difference.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests> wrote 

> >Nor can one in a file such as a data file as used by CISAM, the well
> >known Informix underlying file system.
> 
> The underlying file system is irrelevant. 

It was you that started arguing that there was a relevant similarity
between a table and a database table. Now its all hand waving on your
part.

> In database view, one can not only insert a row in the middle, 

You cannot insert a record into the middle of a phyical file (without
moving all the data beyond that point), a logical insert puts it at
the end.

If there actually is a requirement to keep a path through records
without a physical insert it is easy enough to have 'next' and 'prior'
subscripts in the table, of course this then defeats the SEARCH ALL.

> it can even have a duplicate key.
> Try creating duplicate subscripts in a Cobol table.

Subscripts are not 'keys' they are equivalent to a record number.  Try
having a file with two records with the same number.

> We can sort a file and we can sort a table (2002 & MF). Why can't we
> say SORT .. GIVING tablename?

What's your point ?  It is only a handful of lines of code.

> Most compilers already do. It's generically called a collection class,
> specifically a dictionary. J4 appears to be standardizing the class.

If you were promoting the use of collection classes this would be a
different discussion.  You weren't, you were promoting hand coding low
level address fiddling as done by C in the 70s.
 
> Informix temp tables reside in memory and are automagically defined.
> They offer the benefit of database logic without the overhead of disk
> access, separate definition and security issues. When the job ends,
> they disappear in puff of smoke.

Exactly, I don't have to care how it does this, I just throw the data
at it and ask for it back as I want it.  This is far more valuble than
how to fiddle non-standard memory addresses.  They can read that in
'Computer Science' if they care.

> Everyone's not a dumb consumer. Someone has to write those easy-to-use
> tools. I was talking to the tool makers and anyone curious about the
> subject.

How many 'tool makers' do you think visit clc ?  Your code and example
was directly aimed at applications programmers, ie those who visit
here.


> >Do you do this by dividing the pointer by 2 ?
> 
> By using SEARCH ALL. In the demo, tree nodes lived  in a table.

Well bang goes the only advantage of lists and trees, the freedom from
a limit imposed by having a table.  Presumably you have to sort the
table so that SEARCH ALL can work.

You seem to have gone full circle and just picked up a huge baggage on
the way.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If you were inventing CoBOL...

_(reply depth: 20)_

- **From:** Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net.yourmammaharvests>
